- *(Optional mention for full credit — 1 mark):* Based on source — Anthropogenic (stationary, mobile, area) and Natural (volcanic, biogenic).

**(ii) Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) — 2.5 marks:**
- **Chemical Nature:** Colourless, pungent, suffocating gas; molecular weight 64.07 g/mol; soluble in water forming H₂SO₃; oxidises to SO₃ → H₂SO₄ (acid rain).
- **Sources (1 mark):** Burning of sulphur-containing fossil fuels (coal, oil) in thermal power plants (60 %), petroleum refineries, smelters, volcanic eruptions (natural).
- **Health Effects (1 mark):** At 5–10 ppm causes bronchoconstriction; at 20 ppm causes severe respiratory distress; aggravates asthma and chronic bronchitis; corrodes marble (calcite → gypsum), damages metals and masonry.

**(iii) Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂) — 2.5 marks:**
- **Chemical Nature:** Reddish-brown, paramagnetic gas; molecular weight 46 g/mol; formed by oxidation of NO emitted from high-temperature combustion (Zeldovich mechanism, T > 1300 °C). Photochemically dissociates by sunlight to give O₃.
- **Sources (1 mark):** Motor vehicles (50 % of urban NOₓ), thermal power plants, industrial furnaces, lightning (natural).
- **Health Effects (1 mark):** At 0.2 ppm causes inflammation of airways; chronic exposure reduces lung function; precursor to tropospheric O₃ and acid rain (HNO₃).

---

**Model Solution for Q. A(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Concept of NAAQS (2 marks):**
The **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** are statutory limits prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** in November 2009 under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** and the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. They define the **maximum permissible concentration** of pollutants in ambient (outdoor) air, averaged over a specified time, to protect human health, vegetation, and property. Two averaging periods: **24-hour** (short-term) and **Annual** (long-term).

**(ii) 24-hr Concentration Limits (2.5 marks):**

| Pollutant | 24-hr NAAQS Limit | Industrial Limit |
|---|---|---|
| PM₁₀ | 100 μg/m³ | 150 μg/m³ |
| PM₂.₅ | 60 μg/m³ | 90 μg/m³ |
| SO₂ | 80 μg/m³ | 120 μg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 80 μg/m³ | 120 μg/m³ |

**(iii) Health Implications of Exceedance in Metros (2.5 marks):**
- **Delhi, NCR, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Kolkata, Chennai** consistently exceed PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀ limits (often 2–5× the standard) in winter.
- **Consequences:** AQI enters "Very Poor" (301–400) and "Severe" (401–500) brackets; rise in **asthma, COPD, cardiovascular hospitalisations, lung cancer**, and reduced life expectancy (estimated 3–5 years lost in NCR).
- **Triggered Actions:** Odd-even vehicle schemes, brick-kiln shutdowns, GRAP (Graded Response Action Plan) stages I–IV, NGT directions, school closures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in A(a) & A(b):**
> 1. Confusing **Industrial vs Residential** NAAQS values (2× penalty per wrong column).
> 2. Writing "NAAQS" without naming the **CPCB notification year 2009** — 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. In A(a), writing "NO₂" sources without the **Zeldovich mechanism** — loses 1 mark.
> 4. Not mentioning the **Averaging Period** in NAAQS definition.

---

#### 🔹 Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Adapted to KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define the **Air Quality Index (AQI)**. The following **24-hour average concentrations** were measured at an urban monitoring station:

| Pollutant | Concentration |
|---|---|
| PM₁₀ | 180 μg/m³ |
| SO₂ | 75 μg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 90 μg/m³ |

Calculate the **sub-indices** and the **overall AQI**. State the AQI category and the **dominant pollutant**, and advise the public health measures. Use the CPCB breakpoint table provided. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **sources, health effects, and control measures** of Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅). Why is PM₂.₅ considered more hazardous than PM₁₀? **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q. B(a) — 7 marks**

**Definition of AQI (1 mark):**
The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a dimensionless number (0–500) that converts the measured concentrations of criteria pollutants into a single composite value indicating ambient air quality. The **CPCB AQI** is computed by **linear interpolation** between breakpoints and the **overall AQI is the worst (maximum) sub-index**, which also identifies the **dominant pollutant**.

**Given Data:**
- PM₁₀ = 180 μg/m³, SO₂ = 75 μg/m³, NO₂ = 90 μg/m³.

**Step 1 — Sub-index for PM₁₀ (2 marks):**
- Breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 101$, $BP_{Hi} = 250$, $I_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Hi} = 200$.

$$I_{PM10} = \dfrac{200 - 101}{250 - 101} \, (180 - 101) + 101 = \dfrac{99}{149} \times 79 + 101$$

$$I_{PM10} = 0.6644 \times 79 + 101 = 52.49 + 101 = 153.49 \approx 153$$

**Step 2 — Sub-index for SO₂ (1 mark):**
- Breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 0$, $BP_{Hi} = 80$, $I_{Lo} = 0$, $I_{Hi} = 50$.

$$I_{SO2} = \dfrac{50 - 0}{80 - 0} \, (75 - 0) + 0 = \dfrac{50}{80} \times 75 = 46.87 \approx 47$$

**Step 3 — Sub-index for NO₂ (1 mark):**
- Breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 81$, $BP_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Lo} = 51$, $I_{Hi} = 100$.

$$I_{NO2} = \dfrac{100 - 51}{180 - 81} \, (90 - 81) + 51 = \dfrac{49}{99} \times 9 + 51$$

$$I_{NO2} = 0.495 \times 9 + 51 = 4.45 + 51 = 55.45 \approx 55$$

**Step 4 — Overall AQI (1 mark):**

$$\text{AQI} = \max(153,\ 47,\ 55) = 153$$

**Step 5 — Result & Advisory (1 mark):**
- **AQI = 153** → **Moderate** category (101–200).
- **Dominant Pollutant = PM₁₀** (highest sub-index).
- **Public Health Advisory:** Active children and adults with respiratory disease (e.g., asthma) should **limit prolonged outdoor exertion**; general public can carry out normal activities.

---

**Model Solution for Q. B(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Sources of PM (2 marks):**
- **PM₁₀ — Coarse:** Road dust, construction activity, agricultural operations, mining, quarrying, biomass burning, sea salt, pollen, tyre and brake wear.
- **PM₂.₅ — Fine:** Vehicular diesel emissions, coal-fired power plants, oil refineries, secondary sulphate and nitrate aerosols (formed from SO₂ and NOₓ in the atmosphere), industrial metal processing.

**(ii) Health Effects (2.5 marks):**
- **PM₁₀:** Trapped in **nasopharynx and upper airways**; causes **bronchitis, asthma aggravation, chronic cough, eye irritation**.
- **PM₂.₅:** Penetrates **deep into alveoli**, enters **bloodstream** → causes **cardiovascular disease, arrhythmias, lung cancer (IARC Group 1 carcinogen), premature death**; in children impairs lung development.
- **PM₀.₁ (ultrafine):** Crosses **blood–brain barrier and placenta**; linked to **neurodegeneration** and **low birth weight**.

**(iii) Control Measures (2 marks):**
- **At-source:** Fabric filters (baghouses), electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), cyclone separators, wet scrubbers.
- **End-of-pipe:** Cyclones + scrubbers in series, HEPA filters for fine dust.
- **Urban level:** Vehicle emission norms (BS-VI), CNG adoption, road dust suppression (water sprinklers), green-belt development, shift to renewables.
- **Policy:** National Clean Air Programme (NCAP), construction debris rules, ban on crop residue burning.

**(iv) Why PM₂.₅ is More Hazardous (0.5 mark):**
PM₂.₅ has a **smaller aerodynamic diameter (≤ 2.5 μm)** that bypasses the **upper respiratory defence** (nasal hairs, mucociliary escalator) and reaches **alveolar gas-exchange regions**, where it can **translocate into the circulatory system** and trigger **systemic inflammation and oxidative stress**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in B(a) & B(b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the maximum** of sub-indices as the overall AQI — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating the dominant pollutant** explicitly — losing 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Rounding AQI** without saying "rounded to the nearest integer" — partial deduction.
> 4. In B(b), omitting **secondary aerosols** as a PM₂.₅ source — losing 1 mark.
> 5. Not giving **differential health effects** of PM₁₀ vs PM₂.₅ (examiner gives 0 if both are written identically).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Yield Board Points (Memorize Before Exam):**

- ✅ Air pollutants are classified as **Primary (emitted)** and **Secondary (formed in atmosphere)**.
- ✅ The **8 (or 12) Criteria Pollutants** are governed by **CPCB NAAQS (2009)** under the **Air Act, 1981**.
- ✅ **24-hr NAAQS values to memorize:** PM₁₀ = 100, PM₂.₅ = 60, SO₂ = 80, NO₂ = 80, CO = 4 mg/m³, O₃ = 100, NH₃ = 400, Pb = 1.0 μg/m³.
- ✅ **AQI Formula:** Linear interpolation between breakpoints; **AQI = max(sub-indices)**; identifies **dominant pollutant**.
- ✅ **CPCB AQI Categories:** 0–50 Good, 51–100 Satisfactory, 101–200 Moderate, 201–300 Poor, 301–400 Very Poor, 401–500 Severe.
- ✅ **PM₂.₅ > PM₁₀** in toxicity (deeper penetration, systemic effects).
- ✅ **CO is a "silent killer"** — binds haemoglobin 200× more than O₂ (forms COHb).
- ✅ **Photochemical smog** requires **NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight** → O₃ + PAN.
- ✅ **CPCB stack-height formula:** $H = 74 \, Q^{0.27}$ (power plants), $H = 14 \, Q^{0.27}$ (others), Q in kg/h of SO₂.
- ✅ **Holland's plume-rise formula** is the most cited stack-plume rise equation.
- ✅ **Zeldovich mechanism** explains thermal NO formation (T > 1300 °C).
- ✅ **Acid rain:** SO₂ → H₂SO₄, NOₓ → HNO₃; pH < 5.6.
- ✅ **Taj Mahal effect** = SO₂ + marble → gypsum crust (corrosion).
- ✅ **NCAP (National Clean Air Programme)** launched 2019 targets 40 % PM reduction by 2025–26.
- ✅ **Indoor air pollutants:** Radon (lung cancer), Formaldehyde (irritation), ETS (passive smoking), Asbestos (mesothelioma), Bioaerosols.
- ✅ **Gaussian Plume Model** is the standard EIA tool for predicting ground-level concentration from elevated sources.
- ✅ **Lead phasing out of petrol** in India (2000) is a landmark air-pollution control success.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sources # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, **Sources** refer to the discrete project activities, physical installations, operational processes, or natural phenomena that originate or release environmental stressors (pollutants, disturbances, or pressures) into the surrounding environment. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Environmental Impact Analysis, sources form the first and most critical link in the **Source–Pathway–Receptor (S-P-R)** model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):** > *"Sources are the activities, installations, or natural conditions that generate environmental changes through release of pollutants, energy emissions, resource consumption, or spatial-temporal disturbances within the proposed project boundary and its zone of influence."* A **Source** in EIA is characterized by four fundamental attributes: | Attribute | Description | |---|---| | **Location** | Spatial coordinates (latitude/longitude) or grid reference within the project boundary | | **Magnitude** | Quantitative measure of emission/release rate (kg/day, dB(A), m³/s) | | **Duration** | Temporal characteristic — short-term, intermittent, or continuous | | **Frequency** | How often the source activates — episodic, periodic, or constant | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smoking Chimney" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine a kitchen. When you fry food (the **source activity**), oil smoke (the **pollutant**), heat (the **energy emission**), and pungent smell (the **aesthetic impact**) spread through the house via air currents (the **pathway**) and affect you, your family, and your furniture (the **receptors**). In EIA, the **kitchen stove** is analogous to a **point source**, your entire kitchen's continuous frying over months is an **area source**, and the gas pipeline running into the house is a **line source**. Just as a doctor must first identify *what is causing the illness* before prescribing medicine, an EIA practitioner must identify *all sources* before predicting impacts. ## 1.3 Why "Sources" is the Foundation of EIA The entire impact prediction cascade in EIA hinges on accurate source identification. Errors at this stage propagate through every subsequent module — leading to flawed impact magnitude estimates, incorrect mitigation design, and invalid regulatory conclusions. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examination Gold Point:** Every impact prediction equation begins with the source term. If the source characterization is wrong, the entire **impact chain analysis** collapses. This is why Module 3 (Sources) precedes Module 4 (Impact Prediction) in the syllabus sequence. ## 1.4 Classification Overview — The Three Pillars of EIA Sources The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes three primary source categories used universally in international EIA practice (EPA, World Bank, and MoEF&CC frameworks): 1. **Point Sources** — Discrete, identifiable, geographically fixed outlets (e.g., a factory chimney, an effluent discharge pipe). 2. **Area Sources (Diffuse Sources)** — Multiple small releases distributed over a geographical area (e.g., agricultural runoff, urban vehicular emissions). 3. **Line Sources (Linear Sources)** — Continuous releases along a linear path (e.g., highway traffic, pipeline leaks, railway corridors). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Used in EIA:** Source strength is typically quantified in **mass per unit time (kg/s, t/day, t/yr)** for pollutants and **decibels (dB)** for noise. These are the **SI-aligned units** mandated by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) of India. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification of EIA Sources ### 2.1.1 Point Sources A **point source** is a single, stationary, identifiable outlet of pollution. Its emission can be measured at one specific location using standard sampling instruments. **Engineering Examples in EIA Context:** - **Air:** Smokestacks of thermal power plants, cement kilns, incinerators. - **Water:** Industrial effluent outlets, sewage treatment plant (STP) discharge points. - **Land:** Hazardous waste landfills, tailing ponds of mines. > [!NOTE] > **Why It Matters in KTU Exams:** Point sources are mathematically the *easiest* to model because their emission rate can be directly plugged into the Gaussian Plume Model (Module 4 content). Examiners frequently ask students to classify a given activity as point/area/line source. ### 2.1.2 Area Sources (Diffuse Sources) **Area sources** are distributed pollution generators that cannot be traced to a single outlet. They arise from aggregated minor activities spread across a defined area. **Engineering Examples in EIA Context:** - **Air:** Vehicular emissions across a city grid, construction dust from multiple sites. - **Water:** Agricultural fertilizer runoff, urban stormwater runoff (non-point). - **Soil:** Mining over a lease area, deforestation zones. ### 2.1.3 Line Sources (Linear Sources) **Line sources** generate emissions continuously along a defined linear feature such as a road, railway, canal, or pipeline. **Engineering Examples in EIA Context:** - Highway traffic noise (dB per meter of road length). - Crude oil pipeline leaks (liters per km per day). - Canal seepage losses (m³/day per km). ## 2.2 Source Inventory — The Data Foundation A **Source Inventory** is a comprehensive, tabular compilation of all emission sources within the project boundary. It is mandatory under India's **EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)**. The standard inventory format includes: | Parameter | Typical Value/Unit | |---|---| | Source ID | S-01, S-02, … | | Source Type | Point / Area / Line | | Coordinates | Latitude °N, Longitude °E | | Pollutant | SO₂, NOₓ, PM₁₀, BOD, COD, etc. | | Emission Rate | kg/hr or t/day | | Operating Hours | hr/day, days/yr | | Control Mechanism | ESP, cyclone, scrubber, bio-filter | ## 2.3 Theoretical Basis — Source Strength Equations ### 2.3.1 General Mass Emission Rate The fundamental equation defining source strength for any release is: $$Q = C \times F$$ where: - $Q$ = **emission rate** (kg/s) - $C$ = **concentration of pollutant** (kg/m³) - $F$ = **volumetric flow rate** of carrier medium (m³/s) ### 2.3.2 Annual Emission Load (Used in EIA Reporting) $$E_{annual} = Q \times T_{op} \times D_{op}$$ where: - $E_{annual}$ = **annual emission** (kg/yr or t/yr) - $Q$ = emission rate (kg/s) - $T_{op}$ = daily operating hours (s/day) - $D_{op}$ = annual operating days (days/yr) ### 2.3.3 Stack Emission Correction (For Combustion Point Sources) $$Q_{actual} = Q_{measured} \times \sqrt{\frac{T_{stack}}{T_{ref}}}$$ where: - $Q_{actual}$ = emission rate at stack conditions (Nm³/s, where N = normal conditions) - $T_{stack}$ = absolute stack gas temperature (K) - $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature, normally **298.15 K (25 °C)** ### 2.3.4 Line Source Emission Rate $$q_{L} = \frac{n \times E_v \times f}{3600}$$ where: - $q_L$ = line emission rate (kg/m·s) - $n$ = number of vehicles per hour - $E_v$ = emission factor per vehicle (g/km) - $f$ = correction factor for meteorology - $3600$ = seconds per hour conversion ### 2.3.5 Area Source Emission Rate $$q_A = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_{source}}$$ where: - $q_A$ = area emission density (kg/m²·s) - $Q_{total}$ = total emission from the area (kg/s) - $A_{source}$ = source area (m²) ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY FORMULA SHEET FOR KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass Emission Rate | $Q = C \times F$ | kg/s | All source types | | 2 | Annual Emission Load | $E_{annual} = Q \times T_{op} \times D_{op}$ | t/yr | EIA reporting | | 3 | Stack Correction | $Q_{actual} = Q_{measured} \times \sqrt{T_{stack}/T_{ref}}$ | Nm³/s | Combustion sources | | 4 | Line Source Rate | $q_L = n \times E_v / 3600$ | kg/m·s | Highway, railway | | 5 | Area Source Density | $q_A = Q_{total} / A_{source}$ | kg/m²·s | Agricultural, urban | | 6 | Fugitive Emission Factor | $E_f = k \times u^{a}$ | kg/ha·yr | Wind erosion | | 7 | Source Impact Score | $SI = M \times D \times F \times R$ | dimensionless | Source ranking | **Where for Source Impact Score:** - $M$ = Magnitude of emission - $D$ = Duration of activity - $F$ = Frequency - $R$ = Receptor proximity weighting > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** These equations are used by consulting firms (e.g., ERM, AECOM, ILF) and regulatory bodies (CPCB, State Pollution Control Boards) in India to prepare **EIA Reports** for projects worth ₹500 crores and above. Mastery of these formulas is essential for careers in environmental consultancy. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Instantaneous to Annual Emission Load This derivation bridges the **fundamental emission rate** to the **annual pollution load** — a quantity required in every EIA report submitted under MoEF&CC norms. ### Given Data - A coal-fired boiler (point source) emits SO₂ at a **measured concentration** of $C = 1200 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (= $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/m³). - The **flue gas flow rate** is $F = 25$ m³/s. - The plant operates **22 hours per day**, **330 days per year**. ### Step 1 — Compute the Instantaneous Emission Rate We begin with the fundamental mass-balance equation: $$Q = C \times F$$ Substituting the values: $$Q = (1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3) \times (25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s})$$ Performing the multiplication: $$Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 25$$ $$Q = 30 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 0.030 \text{ kg/s}}$$ > **Logic Check:** A concentration of 1200 mg/m³ is typical for an uncontrolled coal boiler. Multiplying by 25 m³/s (medium-scale boiler) gives 30 grams per second — physically realistic. ### Step 2 — Convert Daily Operating Hours to Seconds $$T_{op} = 22 \text{ hr/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr}$$ $$T_{op} = 79{,}200 \text{ s/day}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Daily Emission Load The daily emission is the product of the instantaneous rate and the operating seconds per day: $$E_{day} = Q \times T_{op}$$ $$E_{day} = 0.030 \text{ kg/s} \times 79{,}200 \text{ s/day}$$ Performing the multiplication step by step: $$E_{day} = 0.030 \times 79{,}200$$ $$E_{day} = 0.030 \times (8 \times 10^4 - 800)$$ $$E_{day} = 0.030 \times 79{,}200$$ $$\boxed{E_{day} = 2{,}376 \text{ kg/day}}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute Annual Emission Load Now we multiply by the number of operating days per year: $$E_{annual} = E_{day} \times D_{op}$$ $$E_{annual} = 2{,}376 \text{ kg/day} \times 330 \text{ days/yr}$$ Performing the multiplication: $$E_{annual} = 2{,}376 \times 330$$ Breaking it down: $$E_{annual} = 2{,}376 \times 300 + 2{,}376 \times 30$$ $$E_{annual} = 712{,}800 + 71{,}280$$ $$\boxed{E_{annual} = 784{,}080 \text{ kg/yr} \approx 784 \text{ tonnes/yr}}$$ ### Step 5 — Regulatory Comparison Under India's **CPCB emission standards for SO₂ from thermal power plants**, the limit (with modern FGD — Flue Gas Desulfurization) is typically **< 200 mg/Nm³**. Since our source emits 1200 mg/m³ (≈ 6× the limit), this confirms the plant would require an FGD system — a direct linkage to **mitigation planning** in Module 5. ## 3.2 Derivation: Line Source Emission from Highway Traffic ### Given Data - Traffic density: $n = 1{,}500$ vehicles/hour - Average emission factor (mixed fleet): $E_v = 2.5$ g/km/vehicle ### Step 1 — Convert Hourly Traffic to Per-Meter Emission The total emission per hour across all vehicles: $$E_{total} = n \times E_v$$ $$E_{total} = 1{,}500 \text{ veh/hr} \times 2.5 \text{ g/km/veh}$$ $$E_{total} = 3{,}750 \text{ g/hr} = 3.75 \text{ kg/hr}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Per-Second Rate $$\dot{E} = \frac{E_{total}}{3600}$$ $$\dot{E} = \frac{3.75}{3600}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{E} \approx 1.042 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Line Source Strength Assuming the highway extends for 1 km (= 1000 m), the line emission rate is: $$q_L = \frac{\dot{E}}{L}$$ $$q_L = \frac{1.042 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}}{1000 \text{ m}}$$ $$\boxed{q_L = 1.042 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m·s}}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** This value is fed into the **CALINE-3** or **CALPUFF** line source dispersion model in Module 4 to predict downwind pollutant concentrations at receptors. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Source Classification for Common Project Activities > [!NOTE] > **This matrix is high-yield for KTU ESE Part A questions.** | Project Activity | Primary Medium | Source Classification | Key Pollutant | |---|---|---|---| | Thermal Power Plant Stack | Air | Point | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂ | | Sewage Treatment Plant Discharge | Water | Point | BOD, COD, fecal coliform | | Highway Traffic | Air + Noise | Line | CO, NOₓ, dB(A) | | Agricultural Field Runoff | Water | Area | Nitrates, phosphates, pesticides | | Open Cast Mining | Air + Land | Area | Dust, SPM, land degradation | | Crude Oil Pipeline Leak | Soil/Water | Line | Hydrocarbons | | Municipal Solid Waste Dump | Land + Air + Water | Area | Leachate, methane, VOCs | | Hydroelectric Reservoir | Water | Area | CH₄ (decomposition), DO change | | Construction Site | Air + Noise | Area | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, dB(A) | | Industrial Effluent Outfall | Water | Point | Heavy metals, TDS | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Source Classification Master Tree ```mermaid graph TD A["Environmental Sources in EIA"] --> B["Point Sources"] A --> C["Area Sources"] A --> D["Line Sources"] A --> E["Fugitive Sources"] B --> B1["Stationary Chimney"] B --> B2["Effluent Outlet"] B --> B3["Hazardous Waste Pipe"] B --> B4["Single Industrial Stack"] C --> C1["Agricultural Runoff"] C --> C2["Urban Stormwater"] C --> C3["Construction Dust Cloud"] C --> C4["Deforested Zone"] D --> D1["Highway Traffic"] D --> D2["Railway Corridor"] D --> D3["Pipeline Network"] D --> D4["Canal System"] E --> E1["Wind-Blown Dust"] E --> E2["Volatile Organic Leaks"] E --> E3["Valve and Flange Leaks"] E --> E4["Open Storage Pile"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Source–Pathway–Receptor (S–P–R) Conceptual Model ```mermaid graph LR SRC["SOURCE
Project Activity
e.g. Power Plant Stack"] P1["PATHWAY 1
Atmospheric Dispersion
Gaussian Plume"] P2["PATHWAY 2
Surface Water Flow"] P3["PATHWAY 3
Soil Infiltration"] REC["RECEPTOR
Human / Ecosystem / Property"] SRC --> P1 SRC --> P2 SRC --> P3 P1 --> REC P2 --> REC P3 --> REC MIT["MITIGATION
Control Technology
e.g. ESP, FGD, STP"] MIT -.-> SRC MON["MONITORING
CPCB Station
Ambient Sampling"] MON -.-> REC ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Source Identification Workflow ```mermaid graph TD STEP1["STEP 1: Project Activity Listing"] --> STEP2["STEP 2: Medium Classification
Air Water Land Noise"] STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3: Spatial Classification
Point Area Line"] STEP3 --> STEP4["STEP 4: Pollutant Identification
SO2 NOX PM BOD COD"] STEP4 --> STEP5["STEP 5: Emission Quantification
Q = C x F"] STEP5 --> STEP6["STEP 6: Temporal Mapping
Construction vs Operation Phase"] STEP6 --> STEP7["STEP 7: Source Inventory Database"] STEP7 --> STEP8["STEP 8: Impact Prediction Module 4"] style STEP1 fill:#e1f5ff style STEP5 fill:#fff4e1 style STEP7 fill:#e8f5e9 style STEP8 fill:#fce4ec ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Phase-wise Source Generation Across Project Lifecycle ```mermaid graph TD PHASE1["PHASE 1: Pre-Construction
Land Clearing, Survey"] --> S1A["Source: Vegetation Removal"] PHASE2["PHASE 2: Construction
Excavation, Building"] --> S2A["Source: Dust, Noise, Diesel Emissions"] PHASE3["PHASE 3: Operation
Manufacturing / Generation"] --> S3A["Source: Continuous Emissions"] PHASE4["PHASE 4: Decommissioning
Demolition, Restoration"] --> S4A["Source: Short-Term Bursts"] S1A --> INV["Source Inventory Register"] S2A --> INV S3A --> INV S4A --> INV INV --> EIA["EIA Report Submission"] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Visualization Tip:** In exam answers, drawing even a simplified version of these flow diagrams (handwritten boxes + arrows) typically earns **2-3 extra marks** for "presentation" — a key differentiator between 80% and 95%+ answers. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "Source" in Environmental Impact Assessment. Differentiate between Point and Area Sources with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Source** in EIA refers to any project activity, installation, or natural condition that releases pollutants, energy, or disturbances into the environment, forming the first element of the Source–Pathway–Receptor model. **Difference:** | Aspect | Point Source | Area Source | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Discrete, identifiable outlet at a single location | Multiple small releases distributed over an area | | **Measurability** | Direct measurement at one point | Requires estimation/aggregation | | **Example** | Chimney of a thermal power plant | Agricultural fertilizer runoff across farmland | *(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for differentiation with example, 0.5 mark for tabular clarity)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List any six attributes used to characterize a Source in an EIA Source Inventory.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six key attributes are: 1. **Source ID** — unique identifier (e.g., S-01) 2. **Location** — geographic coordinates 3. **Type** — Point / Area / Line / Fugitive 4. **Magnitude** — emission rate (kg/s or t/day) 5. **Pollutant Type** — chemical/physical nature of release 6. **Operating Schedule** — duration and frequency (hr/day, days/yr) *(0.5 mark per attribute)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing "sources" with "impacts," (b) omitting units in numerical problems, (c) forgetting to classify by spatial AND temporal characteristics, and (d) not linking sources to subsequent pathway/receptor analysis. Always **tabulate** source classifications and **show full unit conversions**. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]* **(a) Classify in detail the different types of Sources considered in Environmental Impact Assessment with suitable examples for each. Discuss the Source Inventory methodology used in preparing an EIA report.** (7 marks) **(b) A sugar industry discharges effluent at the rate of 250 m³/hour through a single outfall. The BOD concentration in the effluent is 850 mg/L. The plant operates 20 hours per day and 300 days per year. Calculate: (i) the instantaneous BOD emission rate in kg/s, (ii) the annual BOD load in tonnes/year.** (7 marks) ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Classification of Sources in EIA:** Sources in EIA are classified along two axes: **spatial geometry** and **temporal behavior**. **(i) Spatial Classification (4 marks):** - **Point Sources:** Single, identifiable, fixed outlets. Example: Chimney stack, STP discharge pipe, industrial outfall. - **Area Sources:** Distributed emissions across a region. Example: Agricultural runoff, urban dust, mining lease area. - **Line Sources:** Linear continuous releases. Example: Highway traffic, oil pipeline, railway corridor. - **Fugitive Sources:** Unintended leaks from valves, flanges, storage tanks. Example: VOC emissions from petrochemical plants. **(ii) Temporal Classification (2 marks):** - **Continuous Sources** (e.g., power plant during operation) - **Intermittent Sources** (e.g., DG set during grid failure) - **Episodic/Short-term Sources** (e.g., blasting in mining, demolition) **Source Inventory Methodology (1 mark):** The Source Inventory is a structured database listing each source with its ID, location, type, pollutant, emission rate, operating schedule, and existing control mechanism. It follows CPCB/MoEFCC protocols and serves as input to dispersion modelling in subsequent EIA modules. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Volumetric flow rate $F = 250$ m³/hour - BOD concentration $C = 850$ mg/L = 850 g/m³ = **0.850 kg/m³** - Operating time $T_{op} = 20$ hr/day - Operating days $D_{op} = 300$ days/yr **(i) Instantaneous BOD Emission Rate (3 marks):** Convert the flow rate to SI units: $$F = \frac{250 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}}$$ $$F = 0.0694 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Apply the mass emission equation: $$Q = C \times F$$ $$Q = 0.850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.0694 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0590 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **(ii) Annual BOD Load (4 marks):** Convert operating hours to seconds: $$T_{op} = 20 \text{ hr/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 72{,}000 \text{ s/day}$$ Daily emission: $$E_{day} = Q \times T_{op} = 0.0590 \times 72{,}000 = 4{,}248 \text{ kg/day}$$ Annual emission: $$E_{annual} = 4{,}248 \times 300 = 1{,}274{,}400 \text{ kg/yr}$$ Converting to tonnes: $$\boxed{E_{annual} = 1{,}274.4 \text{ tonnes/yr} \approx 1{,}274 \text{ t/yr}}$$ **Regulatory Note:** CPCB inland water disposal norms typically require BOD < 30 mg/L; the 850 mg/L indicates the effluent **must** pass through an Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) before discharge — a key mitigation linkage. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) With the help of a neat schematic, explain the Source–Pathway–Receptor (S–P–R) model used in Environmental Impact Assessment. How does identification of sources influence the accuracy of impact prediction?** (7 marks) **(b) A 10 km stretch of national highway carries 2000 vehicles per hour. The weighted average emission factor for CO is 8 g/km/vehicle. Calculate: (i) the total CO emission rate in kg/hr, (ii) the line source emission rate in kg/(m·s), and (iii) the annual CO load assuming 18 hours of operation per day for 365 days.** (7 marks) ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Source–Pathway–Receptor Model (5 marks):** The S–P–R model is the fundamental conceptual framework of EIA comprising three sequential components: 1. **Source:** The origin of environmental change — a project activity releasing pollutants (e.g., power plant stack emitting SO₂). 2. **Pathway:** The medium through which the pollutant travels from source to receptor — air (dispersion), water (advection), or soil (leaching). 3. **Receptor:** The entity that experiences the impact — humans, ecosystems, buildings, or groundwater. A complete **impact chain** exists only when all three components connect. If any link is broken, no significant impact occurs. **Schematic (draw in exam):** ``` [SOURCE] → [PATHWAY] → [RECEPTOR] Stack Air dispersion Downwind village ``` **Influence on Impact Prediction (2 marks):** Accurate source identification directly determines: - The pollutant mass loading entering dispersion models. - The temporal profile (peak vs. average) used in predictions. - The control technology specifications for mitigation. An under-estimated source leads to under-designed controls and failed regulatory compliance. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Highway length $L = 10$ km = 10,000 m - Traffic flow $n = 2000$ veh/hr - Emission factor $E_v = 8$ g/km/veh - Operation: 18 hr/day × 365 days/yr **(i) Total CO Emission Rate (2 marks):** $$E_{hr} = n \times E_v$$ $$E_{hr} = 2000 \text{ veh/hr} \times 8 \text{ g/km/veh} \times 10 \text{ km}$$ $$E_{hr} = 160{,}000 \text{ g/hr}$$ Converting: $$\boxed{E_{hr} = 160 \text{ kg/hr}}$$ **(ii) Line Source Emission Rate (2 marks):** $$q_L = \frac{E_{hr}}{3600 \times L}$$ $$q_L = \frac{160 \text{ kg/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}}$$ $$q_L = \frac{160}{3.6 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{q_L \approx 4.44 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m·s}}$$ **(iii) Annual CO Load (3 marks):** Convert operating hours to seconds per year: $$T_{year} = 18 \times 3600 \times 365 = 23{,}652{,}000 \text{ s/yr}$$ Annual load: $$E_{annual} = E_{hr} \times T_{op,hr}$$ $$E_{annual} = 160 \text{ kg/hr} \times 18 \text{ hr/day} \times 365 \text{ days/yr}$$ Step-by-step: $$E_{annual} = 160 \times 6{,}570$$ $$E_{annual} = 1{,}051{,}200 \text{ kg/yr}$$ Converting: $$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 1{,}051.2 \text{ tonnes/yr}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 3: Sources** - ✅ **Source** = the originator of environmental change in the **S-P-R model**. - ✅ **Three spatial classifications:** Point, Area, Line (+ Fugitive as 4th). - ✅ **Three temporal classifications:** Continuous, Intermittent, Episodic. - ✅ **Four key attributes:** Location, Magnitude, Duration, Frequency. - ✅ **Core Equation:** $Q = C \times F$ (mass emission rate). - ✅ **Annual Load Formula:** $E_{annual} = Q \times T_{op} \times D_{op}$. - ✅ **Line Source Formula:** $q_L = n \times E_v / 3600$ (kg/m·s). - ✅ **Area Source Formula:** $q_A = Q_{total} / A_{source}$ (kg/m²·s). - ✅ **Units to remember:** kg/s (instantaneous), t/yr (annual), mg/Nm³ (concentration). - ✅ **Indian Regulatory Anchor:** EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020), MoEF&CC. - ✅ **Key Examples:** Chimney = Point; Farm runoff = Area; Highway = Line; Valve leak = Fugitive. - ✅ **Source Inventory:** Database of all sources — mandatory in EIA Reports. - ✅ **Linkage:** Sources feed into dispersion models → impact prediction → mitigation design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU Examinations:** Whenever a numerical is given, ALWAYS (1) write the given data with units, (2) state the governing formula, (3) substitute values step-by-step, (4) box the final answer with units. Examiners award **methodology marks** even if the final number is slightly off. ---

topics.title: Atmospheric Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atmospheric Interactions** in Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA) refers to the physical, chemical, and thermodynamic processes governing the **dispersion, transport, dilution, and transformation of airborne pollutants** released from point, line, or area sources into the troposphere. It encompasses the study of atmospheric stability, lapse rates, mixing layer dynamics, inversion phenomena, wind profiles, and turbulent diffusion — all of which determine the ground-level concentration (GLC) of pollutants for impact prediction.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, atmospheric interaction is the **predictive backbone** of air quality impact assessment. It uses the **Gaussian Dispersion Framework** (anchored to the Pasquill–Gifford stability classification) to mathematically model how an effluent plume from a stack interacts with the surrounding atmosphere before reaching receptors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ink Drop in Water" Intuition

Imagine you drop a single drop of **red ink** into a still fish tank. Three things can happen:
1. If the water is **warm on top and cold below** (stable), the ink sinks as a tight vertical streak — it does not spread. → **Stable atmosphere / Inversion**.
2. If the water is **uniform in temperature** (neutral), the ink slowly mushrooms out horizontally. → **Neutral atmosphere**.
3. If the water is **cold on top and warm below** (unstable), the ink churns violently in loops and eddies, spreading fast. → **Unstable / Convective atmosphere**.

The atmosphere behaves exactly the same way with a pollutant plume:
- **Wind speed (u)** = horizontal current carrying the ink.
- **Turbulence (σy, σz)** = the chaotic mixing intensity (eddy diffusivity).
- **Stability class** = the temperature "layer-cake" of the atmosphere determining how easily vertical mixing occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminder (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR) = **9.8 K/km** (≈ 1 °C per 100 m)
> - Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) varies (typically 6.5 K/km standard atmosphere)
> - Standard Temperature at ground = **288.15 K (15 °C)**
> - Standard Pressure = **101.325 kPa**
> - Gas constant for dry air, R = **287 J/(kg·K)**

## 1.3 Why This Matters in EIA

In any KTU-style EIA project (thermal power plant, cement kiln, highway, refinery), the **pre-monsoon and post-monsoon micrometeorological data** are crucial because they determine:
- Effective stack height design
- Predicted Maximum Ground Level Concentration (PMGC)
- Whether the plant violates NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards)
- Whether **Environmental Clearance** will be granted by the State PCB / MoEFCC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Atmospheric Layers & Vertical Structure

The atmosphere is stratified. For EIA, only the **troposphere** (0–11 km) is operationally significant because pollutants get trapped below the tropopause.

| Layer | Altitude | Temperature Trend | EIA Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Troposphere | 0–11 km | Decreases with height | All dispersion modeling happens here |
| Tropopause | 11–17 km | Isothermal (≈ −56.5 °C) | Acts as lid — caps pollutant mixing |
| Stratosphere | 17–50 km | Increases (ozone absorbs UV) | Contains ozone layer; not for surface pollution |

## 2.2 Lapse Rates — The Heart of Atmospheric Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lapse Rate (Γ)** = Rate of decrease of temperature with increasing altitude (units: K/km or °C/km). A *positive* lapse rate means temperature *decreases* with height.

### (a) Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR, Γₑ)
The *actual* temperature profile measured by a radiosonde on a given day. Highly variable.

### (b) Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR, Γd)
Rate at which a **dry (unsaturated) parcel** cools as it rises (or warms as it sinks) without exchanging heat with surroundings. Derived from Poisson's equation:

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{g}{c_p} = \frac{9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2}{1004 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}} \approx 9.8 \ \text{K/km}$$

### (c) Saturated (Wet) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR, Γs)
Rate at which a **saturated** parcel cools while condensation releases latent heat. Always **less than DALR**, typically **Γs ≈ 4–6 K/km** (varies with temperature and pressure).

### Stability Determination (KTU Board Memory Trick)
Compare **Γₑ** (environmental) with **Γd** (dry adiabatic reference):

| Condition | Criterion | Stability | Plume Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Γₑ > Γd | ELR steeper than DALR | **Absolutely Unstable** | Looping, vigorous mixing |
| Γₑ = Γd | ELR equals DALR | **Neutral** | Coning, moderate diffusion |
| 0 < Γₑ < Γd | ELR less steep than DALR | **Stable** | Fanning, restricted vertical spread |
| Γₑ < 0 | Temperature *increases* with height | **Inversion** (Strongly Stable) | Trapping, fumigation on break-up |

## 2.3 Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classification (A–F)

The single most exam-relevant classification system in KTU air-quality modules.

| Class | Stability | Surface Wind (m/s) | Insolation | Night Cloud Cover |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | < 2 | Strong | — |
| **B** | Moderately Unstable | 2–3 | Strong | — |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | 3–5 | Strong | — |
| **D** | Neutral | > 5 | Any | Any (overcast day/night) |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | 3–5 | — | Thinly overcast or ≥ 4/8 low cloud |
| **F** | Moderately/Strongly Stable | < 3 | — | Clear or ≤ 3/8 cloud |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU memory hook:** A → F progresses from **Unstable → Stable**. Classes A, B, C are **daytime unstable**; E, F are **night-time stable**; D is the **all-purpose default**.

## 2.4 Mixing Layer Height (MLH)

The **Mixing Layer (ML)** is the atmospheric depth through which pollutants are uniformly stirred by turbulence. Its top acts as an **invisible lid**.

- Daytime ML ≈ 1–2 km (convective boundary layer)
- Night-time ML ≈ 100–300 m (stable boundary layer, often capped by a nocturnal inversion)

**Significance:** When σz (vertical dispersion) approaches MLH/2, the plume reflects off the lid — pollutant concentration **doubles**. This is why tall stacks (above MLH) are preferred in industrial zones.

## 2.5 Temperature Inversions — The EIA "Trap"

An **inversion** is a layer where temperature *increases* with height (Γₑ < 0). Inversions are the **worst-case scenario** for pollutant dispersion.

| Inversion Type | Formation Mechanism | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiation (Nocturnal)** | Ground cools by longwave radiation on clear nights | Few hours (sunrise breaks it) |
| **Subsidence** | Large-scale sinking of air in high-pressure systems | Days to weeks (e.g., Delhi winter smog) |
| **Advection** | Warm air moves over cold surface (e.g., sea breeze over cold ocean) | Hours to days |
| **Frontal** | Cold air mass pushes under warm air at weather fronts | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fumigation** occurs when a nocturnal inversion breaks up after sunrise. The mixed layer rises, suddenly engulfing the elevated plume, and slams it to the ground → **spike GLC**. This is why NAAQS monitoring data shows morning peaks.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` instead of `|x|` inside table cells to avoid markdown conflicts.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Gamma_d = g / c_p$ | Dry adiabatic lapse rate (≈ **9.8 K/km**) | K/km |
| 2 | $H_{eff} = h_s + \Delta h$ | Effective stack height = physical + plume rise | m |
| 3 | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | **Gaussian Plume Model** (steady-state, point source, ground reflection) | g/m³ |
| 4 | $C_{max}(x) = \dfrac{2Q}{e \pi u H_{eff}^2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$ | Maximum ground-level concentration | g/m³ |
| 5 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3}}{u}$ | Briggs plume rise (general) — F = buoyancy flux | m |
| 6 | $F = g V_s d_s^2 (T_s - T_a)/(4 T_s)$ | Buoyancy flux parameter (hot gas) | m⁴/s³ |
| 7 | $u(z) = u_r (z / z_r)^p$ | **Power-law wind profile** | m/s |
| 8 | $u(z) = (u_* / k) [\ln(z/z_0) - \psi]$ | **Logarithmic wind profile** (neutral) | m/s |
| 9 | $\sigma_y(x), \sigma_z(x)$ | Pasquill–Gifford dispersion coefficients (from curves/tables) | m |
| 10 | $C_{crosswind,max} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Centerline ground-level concentration (y=0, z=0) | g/m³ |

**Engineering Utility:** Every formula above is used by **regulatory consultants** to prepare EIA reports and predict PM₂.₅, SO₂, NOₓ levels around proposed power plants, highways, or industrial clusters before clearance. Software like **ISCST3, AERMOD, CALPUFF** are industrial implementations of these exact equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

We start from the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a dry adiabatic parcel**:

$$dQ = c_p \, dT - \frac{1}{\rho} dp$$

For an **adiabatic** process, $dQ = 0$. Therefore:

$$c_p \, dT = \frac{1}{\rho} dp$$

Using the **hydrostatic equation** (pressure decreases with height):

$$dp = -\rho g \, dz$$

Substituting:

$$c_p \, dT = \frac{1}{\rho} (-\rho g \, dz) = -g \, dz$$

Rearranging for the lapse rate $\Gamma_d = -dT/dz$:

$$\Gamma_d = -\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{g}{c_p}$$

Numerically:

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2}{1004 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}} \approx 0.00976 \ \text{K/m} = 9.76 \ \text{K/km} \approx 9.8 \ \text{K/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As a dry parcel rises 100 m, it expands against lower pressure, does work on surroundings, and its internal energy decreases → temperature drops by ~1 °C. This is purely geometric (gravity + heat capacity) — independent of altitude.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gaussian Plume Model (Steady-State, Point Source)

### Step 1 — Starting Point: Fick's Law of Diffusion
Mass flux by turbulence:

$$\vec{J} = -K \nabla C$$

### Step 2 — Conservation of Mass (Advection-Diffusion Equation)
For steady state ($\partial/\partial t = 0$), with mean wind $u$ in x-direction:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 3 — Assume Constant K (simplification) and Seek Gaussian Solution
Try the ansatz:

$$C(x,y,z) = A(x) \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Substituting into the PDE and matching coefficients gives:

$$\sigma_y^2 = 2 K_y \frac{x}{u}, \quad \sigma_z^2 = 2 K_z \frac{x}{u}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions
- **(i)** Emission rate Q from stack at height H, mass conservation requires $\int u C \, dA = Q$ at x → 0.
- **(ii)** Ground reflection: image source of equal strength at $z = -H$.

The resulting concentration field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \, &\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \\
\times \bigg[ &\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) \bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Centerline Ground-Level Concentration (y = 0, z = 0)
Plugging z = 0, y = 0:

$$C(0,0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot 2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Maximum Ground-Level Concentration
Taking $dC/dx = 0$ along the centerline yields the **critical downwind distance** where $\sigma_z = H/\sqrt{2}$, and substituting:

$$C_{max} = \frac{2 Q}{e \pi u H_{eff}^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A power plant emits SO₂ at Q = 800 g/s from a stack of physical height $h_s = 100$ m. Effluent temperature $T_s = 400$ K, exit velocity $V_s = 12$ m/s, stack diameter $d_s = 3$ m. Wind speed at stack top $u = 4$ m/s$, ambient $T_a = 293$ K. Stability class C.

**Solution Step 1 — Buoyancy flux F:**

$$F = g V_s d_s^2 \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{4 T_s} = 9.81 \times 12 \times 9 \times \frac{(400-293)}{4 \times 400}$$

Computing numerator:

$$9.81 \times 12 \times 9 = 1059.48$$

$$F = 1059.48 \times \frac{107}{1600} = 1059.48 \times 0.0669 \approx 70.87 \ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2 — Plume rise (Briggs for buoyant, unstable/neutral):**

$$\Delta h = 1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3} / u$$

Assuming final rise distance $x_f = 100 \times u = 400$ m (Briggs rule of thumb for buoyancy-dominated plume):

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \times (70.87)^{1/3} \times (400)^{2/3} / 4$$

$$(70.87)^{1/3} \approx 4.137; \quad (400)^{2/3} \approx 54.29$$

$$\Delta h = 1.6 \times 4.137 \times 54.29 / 4 \approx 89.8 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective stack height:**

$$H_{eff} = 100 + 89.8 = 189.8 \ \text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This number (~190 m) is realistic — Indian thermal power plants (e.g., NTPC) routinely have 220–275 m stacks to ensure buoyancy lift takes the plume above the winter mixing layer.

**Step 4 — Centerline GLC at x = 2 km, stability C:**

From Pasquill–Gifford curves for Class C, at x = 2000 m:
- $\sigma_y \approx 120$ m
- $\sigma_z \approx 220$ m

$$C = \frac{800}{\pi \times 4 \times 120 \times 220} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(189.8)^2}{2 \times (220)^2}\right)$$

$$C = \frac{800}{331,776} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{36,033}{96,800}\right) \approx 2.41 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp(-0.372)$$

$$C \approx 2.41 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.689 \approx 1.66 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{g/m}^3 = 1660 \ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

This exceeds NAAQS SO₂ limit (80 µg/m³ annual) → mitigation needed (tall stack, ESP, FGD).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Atmospheric Stability Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure ELR via radiosonde] --> B{Gamma_e compared to Gamma_d 9.8 K per km}
    B -->|Gamma_e greater than Gamma_d| C[Absolutely Unstable - Class A B]
    B -->|Gamma_e equals Gamma_d| D[Neutral - Class D]
    B -->|Gamma_e between 0 and Gamma_d| E[Stable - Class E F]
    B -->|Gamma_e less than 0| F[Temperature Inversion - Trapping]
    C --> G[Plume Loops Vigorous Mixing]
    D --> H[Plume Coning Moderate Spread]
    E --> I[Plume Fanning Restricted Vertical]
    F --> J[Fumigation on Inversion Breakup]
    G --> K[Low GLC - Good Dispersion]
    H --> L[Moderate GLC]
    I --> M[High GLC Downwind]
    J --> N[Spike GLC at Sunrise - Worst Case]
```

## 4.2 Plume Behavior Matrix (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stack emits plume] --> B{Wind Speed and Stability}
    B -->|Low wind unstable| C[Looping - Large sigma_y sigma_z]
    B -->|Moderate wind neutral| D[Coning - Symmetric spread]
    B -->|Light wind stable| E[Fanning - Lateral spread only]
    B -->|Inversion then breakup| F[Fumigation - Ground slam]
    B -->|Plume trapped under inversion| G[Trapping - Slug along ground]
    B -->|Tall stack inversion aloft| H[Lofting - Effective dispersion]
    C --> I[Sampling: mobile air monitoring van]
    D --> I
    E --> J[High GLC at short distance]
    F --> K[Peak GLC at sunrise - critical]
    G --> L[Maximize stack height]
    H --> M[Verify NAAQS compliance]
```

## 4.3 Gaussian Plume Cross-Section (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Stack at height H] --> P[Plume travels downwind x]
    P --> X1[At x1: sigma_y small sigma_z small - concentrated]
    P --> X2[At x2 mid-field: sigma_z equal to H by sqrt 2 - PMGC location]
    P --> X3[At x3 far-field: sigma_z capped by mixing layer - reflection]
    X1 --> C1[High peak low areal extent]
    X2 --> C2[Maximum ground level concentration PMGC]
    X3 --> C3[Reflected plume - concentration doubles near ground]
    C1 --> R1[Receptor A - safe]
    C2 --> R2[Receptor B - critical design distance]
    C3 --> R3[Receptor C - validate NAAQS]
```

## 4.4 Inversion Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunset - Ground loses longwave radiation] --> B[Ground cools faster than air above]
    B --> C[Nocturnal Radiation Inversion forms 50 to 300 m thick]
    C --> D{Time of day}
    D -->|Midnight to 4 AM| E[Strongest inversion - low MLH]
    D -->|Sunrise 6 AM| F[Ground warms - inversion erodes from below]
    F --> G[ML rises - engulfs elevated plume]
    G --> H[FUMIGATION - Downward mixing to ground]
    H --> I[Peak morning GLC at NAAQS monitoring station]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define atmospheric stability. Explain the Pasquill–Gifford stability classes.**

**Model Answer:**
Atmospheric stability refers to the atmosphere's tendency to resist or enhance vertical motion of air parcels. It is determined by comparing the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR) to the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR).

Pasquill–Gifford classes divide stability into six categories (A–F):
- **A** — Very unstable (strong insolution, low wind)
- **B** — Moderately unstable
- **C** — Slightly unstable
- **D** — Neutral (high wind or overcast)
- **E** — Slightly stable (clear night)
- **F** — Moderately/strongly stable (clear, low wind night)

A, B, C are daytime unstable conditions; E, F are nocturnal stable; D is the universal default.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between dry adiabatic lapse rate and environmental lapse rate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DALR (Γd) | ELR (Γₑ) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Rate at which unsaturated parcel cools on adiabatic ascent | Actual temperature profile of ambient atmosphere |
| Value | **9.8 K/km** (fixed by g/cp) | Variable (typically 6.5 K/km average) |
| Depends on | Gravity and specific heat of dry air only | Local weather, season, altitude, surface conditions |
| Use | Reference to determine parcel stability | Measured by radiosonde |

If Γₑ > Γd → unstable; Γₑ < Γd → stable; Γₑ < 0 → inversion.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Gaussian Plume Model** for dispersion of air pollutants from a continuous elevated point source. State all assumptions and define each term in the equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A cement plant emits particulate matter (PM) at a rate Q = 200 g/s from a stack of effective height 80 m. Wind speed u = 5 m/s, stability class D. At a downwind distance x = 1 km, σy = 68 m, σz = 33 m. Calculate the **centerline ground-level concentration** of PM. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The Gaussian Plume Model is a steady-state dispersion model that assumes:
1. Continuous, constant emission rate Q.
2. Wind speed u is uniform with height.
3. Diffusion in x-direction is negligible compared to advection.
4. Turbulent diffusion follows Fick's law with constant eddy diffusivities Ky, Kz.
5. The pollutant is chemically inert (conservative).
6. Ground acts as a perfect reflector (image source at z = −H).

The governing equation:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Term definitions:**
- Q = pollutant emission rate (g/s)
- u = mean wind speed at stack height (m/s)
- σy, σz = horizontal and vertical standard deviations of plume concentration (m)
- H = effective stack height (m)
- x, y, z = coordinates: downwind, crosswind, vertical (m)

**Limitation:** Valid for distances 0.1–10 km in flat, open terrain.

#### Model Solution for (b):
Given: Q = 200 g/s, H = 80 m, u = 5 m/s, σy = 68 m, σz = 33 m, x = 1 km.

For centerline GLC, y = 0, z = 0:

$$C = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Compute the exponential argument:

$$-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = -\frac{(80)^2}{2 \times (33)^2} = -\frac{6400}{2178} \approx -2.939$$

$$\exp(-2.939) \approx 0.0531$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi \times 5 \times 68 \times 33 = \pi \times 11,220 \approx 35,250$$

Therefore:

$$C = \frac{200}{35,250} \times 0.0531 \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0531 \approx 3.01 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 301 \ \mu\text{g/m}^3}$$

This exceeds NAAQS PM10 limit (100 µg/m³ 24-hr average), indicating need for additional controls like electrostatic precipitators or taller stacks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **types of temperature inversions** and their role in trapping pollutants near the ground. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **six characteristic plume behaviors** (looping, coning, fanning, fumigation, lofting, trapping) observed under different atmospheric stability conditions. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Temperature Inversions** are layers where temperature *increases* with altitude (Γₑ < 0). They act as **lids** that prevent vertical dispersion of pollutants.

| Type | Mechanism | Duration | EIA Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radiation Inversion** | Ground radiates longwave heat on clear, calm nights; surface cools rapidly | Hours (sunrise breaks) | Winter Delhi smog |
| **Subsidence Inversion** | Large-scale sinking of air in high-pressure systems; adiabatic warming of descending air | Days to weeks | Persistent winter haze in Indo-Gangetic plain |
| **Advection Inversion** | Warm air mass moves over a cooler surface (e.g., sea breeze) | Hours to days | Coastal refineries in summer |
| **Frontal Inversion** | Cold front undercuts warm air at a frontal boundary | Hours | Pre-monsoon thunderstorm events |

**Role in pollution trapping:**
- Inversions **suppress turbulence** → σz becomes very small.
- Pollutants accumulate below the inversion base.
- Morning inversion breakup causes **fumigation**, where the mixed layer rises rapidly and slams the elevated plume to the ground.
- GLC can rise 5–10× the design value.

**Mitigation in EIA:** Taller stacks (above inversion base) and not locating receptors within typical fumigation zones.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Plume Type | Stability / Wind | Description | Schematic (textual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Looping** | Very unstable (A, B), low wind (< 2 m/s) | Plume traces large vertical loops due to strong convective eddies | ∿∿∿ vertical serpentine |
| **Coning** | Neutral (D), moderate wind | Plume spreads symmetrically as a horizontal cone | ────► ▽ shape |
| **Fanning** | Stable (E, F), light wind | Plume spreads only laterally; vertical spread negligible | ───► ════════ |
| **Fumigation** | Inversion breakup at sunrise | Mixed layer engulfs elevated plume; pollutant slammed to ground | ╲╲╲ vertical fall |
| **Lofting** | Inversion above plume height; unstable below | Plume disperses upward freely; clean air near ground | ↑ plume escapes ↑ |
| **Trapping** | Ground-level inversion (multiday) | Plume trapped below 200 m; travels as elongated slug | ═══════ near-ground |

**Engineering takeaway:** The **most dangerous** for human receptors are **trapping and fumigation** (worst-case for GLC); **lofting** is the most benign for ground-level exposure.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the GLC formula due to ground reflection — many write Q/(2πuσyσz) instead of Q/(πuσyσz) when z = 0.
> 2. **Confusing σz with σy** — σz (vertical) governs ground impact; σy (horizontal) governs crosswind spread.
> 3. **Using physical stack height (hs) instead of effective stack height (H = hs + Δh)** in the Gaussian equation. The KTU board expects you to compute plume rise Δh using Briggs' formula and ADD it.
> 4. **Mixing up Pasquill classes** — students often classify night conditions as A, B, C (those are daytime only).
> 5. **Units mismatch** — Q in g/s but answer expected in µg/m³ → forgetting to convert (×10⁶).
> 6. **No mention of assumptions** in Gaussian model — KTU examiner explicitly awards 2 marks just for listing the 5–6 key assumptions.
> 7. **Writing DALR = 10 K/km (rounded)** is acceptable, but writing "10 °C/km" with a *minus* sign is a classic error. DALR is positive by convention.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DALR = g/cp = 9.8 K/km**; **SALR ≈ 4–6 K/km**.
- ✅ Stability: **Γₑ > Γd → unstable**; **Γₑ < Γd → stable**; **Γₑ < 0 → inversion**.
- ✅ Pasquill classes: **A (most unstable) → F (most stable)**; D = neutral default.
- ✅ **Mixing Layer Height** caps dispersion — tall stacks must clear it.
- ✅ **Inversions** trap pollutants — radiation, subsidence, advection, frontal.
- ✅ **Fumigation** at sunrise = worst-case morning GLC spike.
- ✅ **Effective stack height** $H = h_s + \Delta h$ — ALWAYS use this in Gaussian equation.
- ✅ **Briggs plume rise** for buoyancy: $\Delta h = 1.6 F^{1/3} x_f^{2/3} / u$.
- ✅ **Gaussian Plume formula** is the *single most important* equation in EIA air modeling.
- ✅ **Centerline GLC**: $C = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Wind profile** follows power law: $u(z) = u_r (z/z_r)^p$.
- ✅ Software: **AERMOD** (current EPA standard), ISCST3 (older), CALPUFF (long-range).
- ✅ NAAQS (India, 2009) limits for SO₂: **80 µg/m³ annual**, **120 µg/m³ 24-hr**.
- ✅ Sigma values depend on **stability class AND downwind distance** — read from Pasquill–Gifford curves/tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
