topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Examples" in the EIA Context?

In **Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA)**, the term *"Examples"* under Module 3 refers to **worked-out sample problems, case study illustrations, and structured application demonstrations** of the EIA methodologies covered previously. These examples demonstrate how theoretical tools (Matrices, Checklists, Networks, Overlays, and Mathematical Modeling) are deployed in real-world projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Examples in EIA pedagogy are structured, documented demonstrations of impact identification, prediction, evaluation, and mitigation planning applied to representative development projects (e.g., thermal power plants, highways, dams, industrial estates, mining operations, airports, and urban developments). They serve as **template frameworks** that students must master to apply the EIA process to unfamiliar projects in examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of EIA examples like **"solved blueprint problems"** in engineering drawing:

- Just as a solved blueprint teaches a student how to convert 3D imagination into 2D orthographic projections, an **EIA example** teaches how to convert a raw project proposal into a structured impact assessment document.
- A solved math problem teaches the *method* — an EIA example teaches the *systematic thought process* of converting environmental concerns into quantifiable and mitigable impacts.
- Examples essentially act as **decision-making templates** that allow future EIA practitioners to recognize patterns (e.g., "If a thermal plant is proposed, then SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter, thermal discharge, and ash disposal are automatically the priority impacts").

## 1.3 Why Are Examples Critical in KTU Examinations?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Significance:**
> 1. Part A (3-mark) questions frequently test students on **"Give an example of a project requiring EIA"** or **"Explain with example the application of Leopold Matrix."**
> 2. Part B (14-mark) questions often present a **specific project scenario** (e.g., "An industrial estate with 50 hectares is proposed near a river... apply appropriate EIA methodology") and ask students to perform an example assessment.
> 3. **15% of total marks** in KTU ESE are typically derived from example/case-study-based questions testing application-level (Bloom Level 3) and analysis-level (Bloom Level 4) skills.

## 1.4 Categories of EIA Examples Covered in Module 3

| Category | Description | Typical Project Type |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Matrix Method Examples** | Leopold Matrix applications | Highway, Industrial plant |
| **Checklist Examples** | Sectoral & environmental checklists | Mining, Thermal power |
| **Overlay Examples** | Geographic Information System (GIS) based | Dam, Reservoir, Land use |
| **Network/Cause-Effect Examples** | Impact chain diagrams | Industrial complex |
| **Mathematical Model Examples** | Box Model, Gaussian Plume | Air pollution from stack |
| **Case Study Examples** | Real documented EIAs | Aswan Dam, Silent Valley |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How EIA Examples Are Structured

Every EIA example in the KTU syllabus follows a **standard 7-step structure**:

1. **Project Identification** — Define the nature, scale, and location of the proposed activity.
2. **Baseline Data Collection** — Enumerate the existing environmental, ecological, and socio-economic conditions.
3. **Impact Identification** — Use one or more methodologies (matrix/checklist/overlay/network) to identify potential impacts.
4. **Impact Prediction** — Quantify magnitude, duration, and spatial extent of impacts.
5. **Impact Evaluation** — Assign significance ratings (e.g., Low / Medium / High).
6. **Mitigation Measures** — Propose engineering or management controls.
7. **Monitoring Plan & EMP** — Outline an Environmental Management Plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This 7-Step Structure Matters:**
> Examiners award marks strictly on whether a student demonstrates a *systematic* approach. Random listing of impacts without structure typically attracts only **40-50% marks** in a 14-mark Part B question.

## 2.2 Standard Mathematical Tools Used in EIA Examples

While many EIA examples are qualitative, certain impact predictions require quantitative formulations. The most common in KTU examinations are:

### 2.2.1 Air Quality — Gaussian Plume Model (Box Form)

For ground-level concentration downwind of a stack:

$$
C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right]
$$

Where:
- $C$ = ground-level pollutant concentration (µg/m³)
- $Q$ = emission rate (g/s)
- $u$ = wind speed (m/s) — **typically assumed as 1 m/s or 5 m/s in KTU problems**
- $\sigma_y$ = horizontal dispersion coefficient (m)
- $\sigma_z$ = vertical dispersion coefficient (m)
- $H$ = effective stack height (m)

### 2.2.2 Noise Attenuation Model

For line source (highway) noise prediction:

$$
L_2 = L_1 - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)
$$

### 2.2.3 Leopold Matrix Scoring

Each cell carries two values:
$$
\text{Significance} = \text{Magnitude (1–10)} \times \text{Importance (1–10)}
$$

Total Project Impact Score:
$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij} \cdot I_{ij}
$$

### 2.2.4 Impact Significance Score (KTU Adopted Formula)

$$
\text{Significance} = (\text{Magnitude} + \text{Extent} + \text{Duration}) \times \text{Probability}
$$

Where each parameter is rated on a **1 to 5 scale**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} e^{-H^2/2\sigma_z^2}$ | Gaussian plume ground concentration | µg/m³ |
| 2 | $L_2 = L_1 - 10 \log_{10}(d_2/d_1)$ | Point-source noise attenuation | dB(A) |
| 3 | $S = (M + E + D) \times P$ | Leopold/Consequence scoring | Unitless (1-100) |
| 4 | $E_{stack} = Q \cdot (1 - \eta_{control})$ | Emission rate after control | g/s |
| 5 | $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H$ | Effective stack height | m |
| 6 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{1.6 \, v_s \, d \, (F + 0.04 \, T)}{u}$ | Buoyancy rise (Briggs equation) | m |
| 7 | $A_{eq} = \dfrac{N \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot 10^{L_{eq}/10}$ | Equivalent sound level | m² |
| 8 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_i - DO_f}{1 - e^{-k t}}$ | Stream BOD impact estimation | mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important KTU Pitfall:** In the Gaussian Plume equation, students often forget that $H$ is the **effective stack height**, not the physical height. Always compute $\Delta H$ first and add it to $H_s$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Examples

- **Project Proponents** use these example templates as reference structures for actual EIA reports submitted to **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), India**.
- **Consulting Firms** like ERM India, IL&FS, and WAPCOS use Leopold Matrix and Checklists as their baseline tools.
- **Regulatory Bodies** such as the **State Environmental Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)** use the same significance-rating framework for granting Environmental Clearance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Gaussian Plume Ground Concentration

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A thermal power plant emits SO₂ at a rate of **800 g/s** from a stack of **physical height 120 m** with **stack exit velocity 18 m/s** and **stack diameter 4 m**. The flue gas temperature is **160 °C**, ambient temperature is **30 °C**, and wind speed at stack height is **6 m/s**. Determine the **maximum ground-level concentration** at Pasquill-Gifford stability class C (daytime conditions). Assume neutral stability: $\sigma_y = 0.22x(1+0.0001x)^{-1/2}$ and $\sigma_z = 0.20x$.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Buoyancy Flux Term $F$

The buoyancy flux $F$ is defined as:

$$
F = g \cdot v_s \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F = 9.81 \cdot 18 \cdot \frac{4^2}{4} \cdot \frac{(160+273) - (30+273)}{(160+273)}
$$

$$
F = 9.81 \cdot 18 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{433 - 303}{433}
$$

$$
F = 9.81 \cdot 18 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{130}{433}
$$

$$
F = 9.81 \cdot 18 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.3002
$$

$$
F = 9.81 \cdot 21.61
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 212.02 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^3}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Briggs Equation for Plume Rise $\Delta H$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \, v_s^2 \, d \, (T_s - T_a)}{u \, T_s}
$$

Wait — let us use the **standard Briggs form** taught in KTU:

$$
\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \, v_s \, d \, (F + 0.04 \, T)}{u}
$$

where $T = T_s - T_a$ in Kelvin. So:

$$
T = 433 - 303 = 130 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{1.6 \cdot 18 \cdot 4 \cdot (212.02 + 0.04 \cdot 130)}{6}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{115.2 \cdot (212.02 + 5.2)}{6}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{115.2 \cdot 217.22}{6}
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{25023.7}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H = 4170.6 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This extremely high plume rise is unrealistic — it indicates a **strongly buoyant plume**. In practice, plume rise is **capped at 3–5 times the stack height** for downwash and stability considerations. We will adopt $\Delta H = 5 \times H_s = 5 \times 120 = 600$ m as a realistic engineering cap.

$$
H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H = 120 + 600 = 720 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Distance $x_{max}$ for Maximum Concentration

For maximum ground concentration under neutral conditions, an empirical approximation is:

$$
x_{max} = \frac{H_{eff}}{1.6}
$$

$$
x_{max} = \frac{720}{1.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{x_{max} = 450 \text{ m}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ at $x_{max}$

$$
\sigma_y = 0.22 \cdot 450 \cdot (1 + 0.0001 \cdot 450)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 99 \cdot (1 + 0.045)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 99 \cdot (1.045)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 99 \cdot 0.9783
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y = 96.85 \text{ m}}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = 0.20 \cdot 450 = 90 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Maximum Ground Concentration

$$
C_{max} = \frac{Q}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{H_{eff}^{\,2}}{2\sigma_z^2}\right]
$$

$$
C_{max} = \frac{800}{\pi \cdot 6 \cdot 96.85 \cdot 90} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{720^2}{2 \cdot 90^2}\right]
$$

Denominator:

$$
\pi \cdot 6 \cdot 96.85 \cdot 90 = \pi \cdot 52303.9 = 164345.5
$$

Exponent argument:

$$
-\frac{720^2}{2 \cdot 90^2} = -\frac{518400}{16200} = -32
$$

$$
e^{-32} \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-14}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{max} = \frac{800}{164345.5} \cdot 1.27 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
C_{max} = 4.87 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.27 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{max} \approx 6.18 \times 10^{-17} \text{ µg/m}^3}
$$

This is **physically negligible**, confirming that a **properly designed tall stack** virtually eliminates ground-level SO₂ impact.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Leopold Matrix Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A **cement manufacturing plant** of 2 MTPA capacity is proposed in a semi-arid region with 5 villages within a 5-km radius. Apply a partial Leopold Matrix (4 actions × 5 environmental components) to identify major impacts.

### Analytical Matrix (Scored Excerpt)

| Action → Component ↓ | Site Preparation | Quarrying | Kiln Operation | Transport |
|----------------------|------------------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Air Quality** | -3, 4 | -5, 6 | **-9, 10** | -4, 5 |
| **Water Resources** | -2, 3 | -4, 5 | -3, 4 | -1, 2 |
| **Soil** | -5, 6 | -7, 8 | -2, 3 | -3, 3 |
| **Flora & Fauna** | -6, 7 | -4, 5 | -1, 3 | -2, 2 |
| **Socio-Economic** | +4, 5 | -3, 4 | +5, 6 | +3, 4 |

(Format: Magnitude, Importance → Cell values; negative = adverse, positive = beneficial)

### Step 1 — Calculate Aggregate Scores per Component

$$
S_{Air} = (-3)(4) + (-5)(6) + (-9)(10) + (-4)(5)
$$

$$
S_{Air} = -12 - 30 - 90 - 20 = -152
$$

Similarly:

$$
S_{Water} = (-2)(3) + (-4)(5) + (-3)(4) + (-1)(2) = -6-20-12-2 = -40
$$

$$
S_{Soil} = (-5)(6) + (-7)(8) + (-2)(3) + (-3)(3) = -30-56-6-9 = -101
$$

$$
S_{Flora} = (-6)(7) + (-4)(5) + (-1)(3) + (-2)(2) = -42-20-3-4 = -69
$$

$$
S_{SocEco} = (4)(5) + (-3)(4) + (5)(6) + (3)(4) = 20-12+30+12 = +50
$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation

- **Air Quality** has the **highest adverse impact (-152)** — kiln emissions are critical.
- **Socio-Economic** shows **net benefit (+50)** — jobs and infrastructure.
- **Priority Mitigation Order:** Air > Soil > Flora > Water > Socio-Economic (positive).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to **rank mitigation priorities** based on the computed matrix scores. Listing impacts without prioritization loses up to **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 — Cause-Effect Network for Highway Project

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Construct a partial cause-effect network for a 20-km highway project passing through an agricultural zone.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Primary Action:** Highway Construction  
↓  
**Secondary Causes (Level 1):**
1. Land acquisition (50 hectares)
2. Earthwork excavation (200,000 m³)
3. Crusher operation
4. Bitumen hot-mix plant
5. Vehicular movement during operation

**Level 2 Effects:**
- Land acquisition → Loss of agricultural land → Loss of livelihood → Socio-economic stress
- Excavation → Soil erosion → Sedimentation in nearby stream → Aquatic habitat loss
- Crusher → Dust emission → Crop yield reduction (within 500 m)
- Hot-mix plant → H₂S, PAH emissions → Air quality deterioration
- Vehicular movement → Noise (70–85 dB) → Wildlife disturbance, Human stress

**Level 3 (Cumulative Effects):**
- Combined dust + noise → Ambient Air Quality Index (AAQI) reduction
- Cumulative stream sedimentation → Reduced downstream irrigation capacity

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Students should always draw **three levels** (Action → Cause → Effect → Cumulative Effect) for full marks in network-based questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *"Give one example of a project requiring EIA and list four major environmental impacts associated with it."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **500 MW coal-based thermal power plant** is a typical example.

Four major environmental impacts:
1. **Air Pollution** — Emission of SO₂, NOₓ, PM, and CO₂ from stack.
2. **Water Pollution** — Thermal discharge and ash pond leachate into nearby river.
3. **Land Degradation** — 1500 tonnes/day of fly ash disposal.
4. **Socio-Economic Impact** — Displacement of nearby villages and resettlement issues.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only generic impacts like "pollution." Always specify the **type of pollution** (air/water/noise) and the **specific pollutant** for full marks.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *"Illustrate with an example the application of the Leopold Matrix."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Leopold Matrix is a **bidimensional impact identification tool** developed in 1971.

**Example Application:** Quarrying operation (action) × Air Quality (component).

- Cell entry: Magnitude = -7 (high severity), Importance = 8 (high relevance to project).
- Product Score = -7 × 8 = **-56** (significant adverse impact).
- Magnitude ranges from **-10 to +10**; Importance from **1 to 10**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
> *"A 200-hectare industrial estate is proposed on the banks of River Periyar near a reserved forest. As an EIA consultant, prepare a sample EIA illustrating:*
> *(a) The application of Leopold Matrix for 4 actions and 4 components, with computed scores (7 marks).*
> *(b) A cause-effect network showing at least 3 levels of impact propagation, and propose 5 mitigation measures (7 marks)."*

#### Model Solution (a) — Leopold Matrix Application (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Actions (A) and Components (C):**

Actions:
- A1: Land clearing and grading
- A2: Industrial construction
- A3: Operation of chemical units
- A4: Effluent discharge

Components:
- C1: River water quality
- C2: Air quality
- C3: Reserved forest flora & fauna
- C4: Local community health

**Step 2 — Construct Matrix:**

| | A1 | A2 | A3 | A4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | -3, 4 | -5, 6 | -6, 8 | **-9, 10** |
| **C2** | -4, 5 | -6, 7 | **-8, 9** | -3, 4 |
| **C3** | **-8, 9** | -5, 6 | -4, 5 | -6, 7 |
| **C4** | -2, 4 | -3, 5 | -5, 6 | -4, 5 |

**Step 3 — Compute Aggregate Component Scores:**

$$
S_{C1} = (-3)(4) + (-5)(6) + (-6)(8) + (-9)(10) = -12-30-48-90 = -180
$$

$$
S_{C2} = (-4)(5) + (-6)(7) + (-8)(9) + (-3)(4) = -20-42-72-12 = -146
$$

$$
S_{C3} = (-8)(9) + (-5)(6) + (-4)(5) + (-6)(7) = -72-30-20-42 = -164
$$

$$
S_{C4} = (-2)(4) + (-3)(5) + (-5)(6) + (-4)(5) = -8-15-30-20 = -73
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret and Rank:**

1. **Water Quality (C1)** — most impacted (-180) → effluent discharge critical.
2. **Forest Ecosystem (C3)** — second priority (-164).
3. **Air Quality (C2)** — third (-146).
4. **Community Health (C4)** — lowest adverse (-73).

#### Model Solution (b) — Cause-Effect Network & Mitigation (7 marks)

**Three-Level Cause-Effect Network:**

**Level 0:** Industrial Estate (200 ha)  
↓  
**Level 1:** A1 Land Clearing, A2 Construction, A3 Operation, A4 Effluent Discharge  
↓  
**Level 2:** Dust, Noise, Chemical Release, Effluent Plume, Habitat Fragmentation  
↓  
**Level 3:**  
- River Periyar: BOD rise, dissolved oxygen depletion, fish mortality  
- Forest: Canopy loss, wildlife displacement, soil erosion  
- Community: Respiratory ailments, livelihood loss, traffic congestion  
↓  
**Level 4 (Cumulative):** Long-term ecological degradation of downstream Periyar Basin.

**Five Mitigation Measures:**

1. **Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP)** with tertiary treatment for chemical unit discharge.
2. **Green belt** of 50-m width around the boundary with native species.
3. **Stack height of 30 m** with **electrostatic precipitator (99.9% efficiency)** for particulate matter.
4. **Rainwater harvesting** and zero-discharge policy to protect Periyar.
5. **Continuous online monitoring** of effluent quality with regulatory public dashboard.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> *"With reference to a proposed 1000 MW hydroelectric project across River Cauvery:*
> *(a) Apply a Checklist Method (with at least 15 items across physical, biological, and socio-economic categories) to identify environmental impacts (7 marks).*
> *(b) Predict the downstream water quality impact using a steady-state mass-balance model and propose a monitoring plan (7 marks)."*

#### Model Solution (a) — Checklist Application (7 marks)

**Environmental Checklist (15 Items):**

**Physical Environment (5):**
- ☑ Submergence of agricultural land (hectares)
- ☑ Reservoir-induced seismicity
- ☑ Micro-climatic change (humidity rise)
- ☑ Sediment trapping and downstream silt reduction
- ☑ Reservoir stratification and anoxic bottom water

**Biological Environment (5):**
- ☑ Loss of riparian flora
- ☑ Disruption of fish migration (especially Mahseer)
- ☑ Submergence of forest patches
- ☑ Avian habitat fragmentation
- ☑ Aquatic weed proliferation (Eichhornia)

**Socio-Economic Environment (5):**
- ☑ Displacement of 5,000 families
- ☑ Loss of cultural heritage sites
- ☑ Increased vector-borne diseases (malaria)
- ☑ Positive: Flood control downstream
- ☑ Positive: Hydropower generation and irrigation

#### Model Solution (b) — Mass-Balance Prediction & Monitoring (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance Equation:**

$$
C_{down} = \frac{Q_r C_r + Q_e C_e}{Q_r + Q_e}
$$

Where:
- $Q_r$ = river flow (m³/s)
- $C_r$ = upstream river concentration (mg/L)
- $Q_e$ = effluent discharge (m³/s)
- $C_e$ = effluent pollutant concentration (mg/L)

**Step 2 — Assume:**
- $Q_r = 500$ m³/s, $C_r = 2$ mg/L BOD
- $Q_e = 2$ m³/s, $C_e = 200$ mg/L BOD (treated sewage)

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
C_{down} = \frac{500 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 200}{500 + 2}
$$

$$
C_{down} = \frac{1000 + 400}{502}
$$

$$
C_{down} = \frac{1400}{502}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{down} = 2.79 \text{ mg/L BOD}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Downstream BOD rises modestly from 2 to 2.79 mg/L — within permissible CPCB limits (3 mg/L).

**Monitoring Plan (5 Elements):**

1. **Monthly grab sampling** at 4 downstream stations.
2. **Continuous DO probe** with real-time data logging.
3. **Quarterly bioassessment** using macroinvertebrate indices.
4. **Annual third-party audit** by empanelled NABL lab.
5. **Public disclosure** of monitoring data on a project website.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write the mass-balance equation but **omit the units** in their final answer. Always specify mg/L, m³/s, or whichever units apply. Missing units → **1 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **EIA examples are structured, not free-form.** Always follow the 7-step framework: Project → Baseline → Identification → Prediction → Evaluation → Mitigation → Monitoring.
- **Leopold Matrix** is the most commonly tested quantitative example — memorize the scoring formula and always **rank components** by aggregate score.
- **Gaussian Plume Model** is the standard for air quality examples. Use **Briggs equation** for plume rise; remember $\Delta H$ is **capped at 3–5× stack height**.
- **Cause-Effect Networks** must show **three levels minimum** — Action → Cause → Effect → Cumulative Impact.
- **Checklist Method** requires **categorization** (Physical/Biological/Socio-Economic) for full marks.
- **Significance Rating** formula: $S = (M + E + D) \times P$, each parameter 1–5.
- **Effective Stack Height** $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H$, never use physical height alone in plume equations.
- **Mitigation Hierarchy** = Avoid → Minimize → Restore → Offset → Compensate.
- **Real-world case studies** that frequently appear: Silent Valley, Narmada Dam, Tehri Dam, Delhi Metro.
- **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** must contain 4 elements: Mitigation, Monitoring, Reporting, Contingency.
- **CPCB standards** are the Indian benchmark for permissible limits in numerical examples.
- **Public Consultation** is a mandatory component of any EIA example since the 2006 EIA Notification of India.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** Always draw the **diagram first** (matrix, network, or flowchart), then write the **explanation**. Visually organized answers consistently score 2–3 marks higher than text-only answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Quality Impact Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Quality Impact Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Quality Impact Analysis (WQIA)** is the systematic, multidisciplinary process of identifying, predicting, evaluating, and mitigating the probable physico-chemical and biological alterations in natural water bodies (surface and groundwater) caused by a proposed developmental project, industrial discharge, or anthropogenic activity, measured against established ambient water quality standards (IS 10500:2012, WHO Guidelines, CPCB Norms).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Water Quality Impact Analysis forms the **hydro-environmental spine** of the EIA notification (EIA Notification 2006, amended 2020) under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. It quantifies how a project alters the **dissolved oxygen (DO) regime**, **nutrient loading**, **trophic state**, **pH balance**, **heavy metal distribution**, and **microbial load** of receiving water bodies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Blood Test 🩸**
> Think of a river or lake as the *circulatory system* of the Earth. Just as a doctor takes a *blood sample* to check **pH (acidity)**, **oxygen (O₂)**, **BOD (organic load)**, **bacteria (microbial count)**, and **toxic metals (lead, mercury)** to assess a patient's health, an EIA consultant takes a *water sample* from a river near a proposed factory/plant and runs identical categories of tests.
> - **Healthy river ≈ Healthy blood** (rich DO, neutral pH, low BOD, no toxic metals)
> - **Polluted discharge ≈ Infected blood** (low DO, acidic, high BOD, heavy metals)
> - **WQIA = The medical report** that tells the regulator (State Pollution Control Board / Ministry of Environment) whether the project will *poison* the river or *leave it stable*.

The objective is to produce a **measurable, defensible, numeric verdict** that can be challenged, audited, or accepted by the **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) saturation at 20 °C, 1 atm** = **9.17 mg/L** (freshwater)
> - **Specific gravity of water** = **1.0** (reference fluid)
> - **Universal gas constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Acceptable BOD₅ (IS 10500 drinking water)** = **2 mg/L (max)**
> - **Acceptable pH range (IS 10500)** = **6.5 – 8.5**
> - **Reaeration rate constant (k₂) range** = **0.1 – 5.0 day⁻¹** (river-dependent)
> - **Deoxygenation constant (k₁) range** = **0.1 – 1.0 day⁻¹** (base-e)

### 1.4 Why This Module Matters in KTU 2024

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically mandates that students master the **mathematical modelling** of water quality because project consultants are required to attach quantified predictions in **EIA Reports (Form-1, Form-1A, EMP, and EIA Main Report)**. Board questions routinely test:
1. Streeter-Phelps DO sag derivation (14-mark favourite).
2. Weighted Arithmetic Water Quality Index computation.
3. Identification of water quality parameters and standards.
4. Mitigation and EMP for water pollution control.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Framework of WQIA

A scientifically defensible WQIA under KTU/EIA Notification 2020 follows a **seven-stage causal chain**:

- **Step 1 — Scoping & Screening:** Identify *Valued Environmental Components (VECs)* of the aquatic system — e.g., specific river stretch, wetland, downstream estuary, or aquifer.
- **Step 2 — Baseline Data Acquisition:** Minimum **one full seasonal cycle (pre-monsoon, monsoon, post-monsoon)** of sampling. KTU standard requires 4 seasons × 4 stations = 16 samples minimum.
- **Step 3 — Impact Identification:** Sources include *point sources* (effluent outfalls), *non-point sources* (agricultural runoff), *land-use change*, and *deforestation-driven siltation*.
- **Step 4 — Impact Prediction:** Through **mathematical models** (Streeter-Phelps for DO, QUAL2E/QUAL2K for river, MODFLOW for groundwater).
- **Step 5 — Evaluation & Significance:** Compare predicted concentrations with **IS 2296 (surface water classification), IS 10500 (drinking), CPCB designated best-use criteria**.
- **Step 6 — Mitigation:** Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD), Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP), Constructed Wetlands, Storm Water Management Plans.
- **Step 7 — Monitoring & Audit:** Post-project monitoring via **Continuous Water Quality Monitoring Stations (CWQMS)** under the National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP).

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why baseline?** Without *before* data, you cannot prove *after* impact. Courts reject unbaselined EIAs.
- **Why math models?** They convert a *qualitative fear* ("river will get dirty") into a *quantitative guarantee* ("DO at 8 km downstream will drop to 4.2 mg/L").
- **Why standards?** They convert *scientific values* into *legal liability*. Crossing IS 10500 limits = **environmental non-compliance**, which is punishable under Section 15 of the EPA, 1986.

### 2.3 Classification of Water Quality Parameters

| Category | Parameters | Significance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Physical** | Temperature, Turbidity, Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Colour, Odour | Affects light penetration, aquatic photosynthesis |
| **Chemical (General)** | pH, Electrical Conductivity (EC), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Alkalinity, Hardness | Indicates geochemical nature, suitability for irrigation/drinking |
| **Chemical (Organic)** | Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | Measures organic pollution load |
| **Chemical (Inorganic/Nutrient)** | Nitrate (NO₃⁻), Nitrite (NO₂⁻), Ammonia (NH₃), Phosphate (PO₄³⁻), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) | Triggers **eutrophication** and **algal blooms** |
| **Chemical (Toxic)** | Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) | **Carcinogenic**, bioaccumulative — priority pollutants |
| **Biological** | Total Coliform, Faecal Coliform, E. coli, MPN Index, Fecal Streptococci | Indicates **pathogenic contamination** (sewage origin) |
| **Radiological** | Gross Alpha, Gross Beta, Radium-226, Strontium-90 | Required for nuclear/thermal projects |
| **Microbiological (Aquatic)** | BOD₅, DO Sag, Biotic Index, Saprobic Index | Ecosystem health, fishery viability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always present at least **one parameter from each category** in 14-mark answers. Most students lose marks by only listing 4–5 parameters; the board expects **8–12 parameters** with units and standards.

### 2.4 Water Quality Standards — India Reference (Memorise for KTU)

| Parameter | IS 10500 (Drinking) | IS 2296 (Class A — Drinking Source) | CPCB Inland Surface Water (Best Use) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | 6.5 – 8.5 | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| DO (mg/L) | — | ≥ 6 | ≥ 4 (Class C) |
| BOD₅ (mg/L) | — | ≤ 2 | ≤ 3 |
| Total Coliform (MPN/100 mL) | 0 (must be absent) | ≤ 50 | ≤ 5000 (Class E) |
| TDS (mg/L) | 500 (max 2000) | 500 | 1500 |
| Nitrate (mg/L) | 45 (max) | 20 | — |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.0 (max 1.5) | 1.5 | — |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.5.A — Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Model (Steady-State, Single Point Source Discharge)**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $D_0$ | $D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{0,upstream}$ | mg/L | Initial oxygen deficit at mixing point |
| $L_0$ | $L_0 = L_{river} + \frac{Q_w L_w}{Q_r + Q_w}$ | mg/L | Mixed ultimate BOD at outfall |
| $L_t$ | $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$ | mg/L | BOD remaining at time *t* |
| $D_t$ | $D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | mg/L | Oxygen deficit at time *t* (downstream) |
| $t_c$ | $t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$ | days | Critical (worst) time |
| $D_c$ | $D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | mg/L | Critical oxygen deficit (minimum DO) |
| $DO_{min}$ | $DO_{min} = DO_{sat} - D_c$ | mg/L | Minimum dissolved oxygen reached |
| $k_1$ | (Deoxygenation rate constant) | day⁻¹ (base-e) | BOD decay rate |
| $k_2$ | (Reaeration rate constant) | day⁻¹ (base-e) | Atmospheric O₂ absorption rate |
| $f$ | $f = k_2 / k_1$ | dimensionless | Self-purification factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.5.B — Water Quality Index (WQI) — Weighted Arithmetic Method (Brown et al., 1970)**

| Step | Formula | Notation |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1. Quality Rating $q_i$ | $q_i = \dfrac{V_i}{S_i} \times 100$ | $V_i$ = observed value, $S_i$ = standard (IS 10500) |
| 2. Unit Weight $w_i$ | $w_i = \dfrac{K}{S_i}$, where $K = \dfrac{1}{\sum (1/S_i)}$ | Proportionality constant |
| 3. Sub-index $SI_i$ | $SI_i = q_i \cdot w_i$ | Per-parameter score |
| 4. Overall WQI | $WQI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Aggregate score |
| 5. Sub-index aggregation | $WQI = \dfrac{\sum SI_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Equivalent form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.5.C — WQI Classification Table (Standard Brown Rating Scale)**

| WQI Range | Water Quality Status | Intended Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0 – 25 | Excellent | Drinking, Irrigation, Aquatic life |
| 26 – 50 | Good | Drinking (after treatment), Irrigation |
| 51 – 75 | Poor | Irrigation, Industrial cooling |
| 76 – 100 | Very Poor | Restricted use, restricted irrigation |
| > 100 | Unsuitable for drinking | Not for any use without treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.5.D — Other Essential KTU Equations**

| Concept | Formula | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Streeter-Phelps Saturation DO | $DO_{sat} = 14.652 - 0.41022 T + 0.007991 T^2 - 0.000077774 T^3$ | Approx. (T in °C) |
| DO deficit → DO conversion | $DO = DO_{sat} - D$ | Whenever deficit is given |
| Mixing BOD | $L_0 = \dfrac{Q_r L_r + Q_w L_w}{Q_r + Q_w}$ | Confluence of stream + waste |
| Mixing DO | $DO_0 = \dfrac{Q_r DO_r + Q_w DO_w}{Q_r + Q_w}$ | Confluence mixing |
| BOD₅ to L₀ | $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5}}$ | Convert 5-day BOD to ultimate |
| Critical DO distance | $X_c = v \cdot t_c$ | Where *v* = stream velocity (km/day) |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Civil Engineers** use WQI to design **Water Treatment Plants (WTP)** and **Sewage Treatment Plants (STP)**.
- **Environmental Engineers** use the Streeter-Phelps model to compute the **assimilation capacity** of rivers for EIA clearance.
- **Chemical Engineers** use it to design **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)** for industries (textile, pharma, distillery, refinery).
- **Mining Engineers** evaluate **Acid Mine Drainage (AMD)** impacts.
- **Town Planners** evaluate cumulative impacts of SEZs, smart cities, and ring-road projects on receiving rivers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is the KTU examiner's favourite zone. Board valuations allot **7–10 marks per sub-part** for complete, labelled, logical steps. *No shortcuts are tolerated.*

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — The Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Equation (Full Derivation)

#### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions

Consider a flowing river receiving a single point-source discharge of municipal/industrial waste at section **x = 0**. Let:
- $L$ = ultimate carbonaceous BOD at location $x$ (mg/L)
- $D$ = dissolved oxygen deficit at location $x$ (mg/L) → $D = DO_{sat} - DO$
- $k_1$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹, base-*e*)
- $k_2$ = reaeration rate constant (day⁻¹, base-*e*)
- $t$ = flow time from outfall (days)
- $D_0$ = initial DO deficit at outfall (just after mixing)
- $L_0$ = mixed ultimate BOD at outfall (just after mixing)

#### 3.1.2 Governing Mass-Balance Differential Equations

**Equation 1 — BOD Decay (deoxygenation):**

The rate of decrease of BOD along the river is proportional to the remaining BOD:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L$$

**Equation 2 — Oxygen Deficit Rate:**

The rate of change of DO deficit has two contributions: deoxygenation *adds* to deficit, reaeration *subtracts* from deficit:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D$$

#### 3.1.3 Solution of Equation 1 (BOD Profile)

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $L = L_0$) to arbitrary $t$:

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = \int_{0}^{t} -k_1 \, dt$$

$$[\ln L]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k_1 t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t$$

$$L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$$

> This is the **residual BOD** at any downstream time *t*. As $t \to \infty$, $L_t \to 0$ (organic matter fully oxidised).

#### 3.1.4 Solution of Equation 2 (DO Deficit Profile)

Substitute $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$ into the deficit equation:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 D = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. The integrating factor is $e^{k_2 t}$. Multiplying both sides:

$$e^{k_2 t} \frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 e^{k_2 t} D = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

The left side is the derivative of $\left( D \cdot e^{k_2 t} \right)$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left( D \cdot e^{k_2 t} \right) = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$\left[ D \cdot e^{k_2 t} \right]_{0}^{t} = k_1 L_0 \int_{0}^{t} e^{(k_2 - k_1) \tau} \, d\tau$$

$$D_t e^{k_2 t} - D_0 = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left( e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} - 1 \right)$$

Dividing throughout by $e^{k_2 t}$:

$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

> **This is the famous STREETER–PHELPS DO SAG EQUATION** (1925). ✔

#### 3.1.5 Derivation of Critical Time (Maximum Deficit)

At the critical point, the deficit is at its maximum → $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = 0$. From Equation 2:

$$0 = k_1 L_t - k_2 D_c$$

At the critical time, $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t_c}$, so:

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

Substituting into the sag equation at $t = t_c$ and simplifying yields:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$$

When $D_0$ is very small (clean upstream river, $D_0 \to 0$), this simplifies to:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

#### 3.1.6 Critical Deficit (Minimum DO)

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

The **minimum dissolved oxygen** downstream of the outfall is:

$$DO_{min} = DO_{sat}(T) - D_c$$

If $DO_{min} \geq 4$ mg/L, the river sustains aquatic life (CPCB Class C). If $DO_{min} < 4$ mg/L → **fish kill** predicted → EIA rejection.

---

### 3.2 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 1 — DO Sag Computation

> **Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark):**
> A river has flow 8 m³/s, velocity 0.5 m/s, BOD₅ = 2 mg/L, DO = 8 mg/L, $k_1$ = 0.23 day⁻¹, $k_2$ = 0.43 day⁻¹. A waste discharge of 1 m³/s with BOD₅ = 150 mg/L, DO = 1 mg/L mixes in. Saturation DO at 20 °C is 9.17 mg/L. Find: (a) Initial mixed BOD (L₀), (b) Initial mixed DO deficit (D₀), (c) Critical time $t_c$, (d) Critical deficit $D_c$, (e) Minimum DO. Use base-*e* system.

#### Step 1 — Convert BOD₅ to L₀ for both streams

$$L_{0,river} = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-k_1 \times 5}} = \frac{2}{1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}}$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$L_{0,river} = \frac{2}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{2}{0.6834} = 2.927 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$L_{0,waste} = \frac{150}{0.6834} = 219.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

#### Step 2 — Mixed ultimate BOD (L₀)

$$L_0 = \frac{Q_r L_{0,river} + Q_w L_{0,waste}}{Q_r + Q_w} = \frac{(8)(2.927) + (1)(219.5)}{8 + 1}$$

$$L_0 = \frac{23.42 + 219.5}{9} = \frac{242.92}{9} = 26.99 \text{ mg/L}$$

#### Step 3 — Mixed Initial DO

$$DO_0 = \frac{Q_r DO_r + Q_w DO_w}{Q_r + Q_w} = \frac{(8)(8) + (1)(1)}{9} = \frac{65}{9} = 7.22 \text{ mg/L}$$

#### Step 4 — Initial Deficit D₀

$$D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_0 = 9.17 - 7.22 = 1.95 \text{ mg/L}$$

#### Step 5 — Critical Time $t_c$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.43 - 0.23}\ln\left[\frac{0.43}{0.23}\left(1 - \frac{1.95 \times 0.20}{0.23 \times 26.99}\right)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.20}\ln\left[1.870 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.39}{6.208}\right)\right]$$

$$= 5 \times \ln[1.870 \times (1 - 0.0628)]$$

$$= 5 \times \ln[1.870 \times 0.9372] = 5 \times \ln[1.7526]$$

$$= 5 \times 0.5609 = 2.805 \text{ days}$$

#### Step 6 — Critical Deficit $D_c$

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c} = \frac{0.23 \times 26.99}{0.43} e^{-0.23 \times 2.805}$$

$$= \frac{6.208}{0.43} \times e^{-0.6452}$$

$$= 14.437 \times 0.5247 = 7.577 \text{ mg/L}$$

#### Step 7 — Minimum DO

$$DO_{min} = DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.17 - 7.58 = 1.59 \text{ mg/L}$$

> **Interpretation:** Since $DO_{min} = 1.59$ mg/L < 4 mg/L (CPCB Class C limit), the river **cannot sustain aquatic life** downstream of the outfall. **EIA must recommend either (a) pre-treatment of waste, (b) reducing waste BOD, or (c) increasing river flow by releasing reservoir water** to bring $DO_{min}$ above 4 mg/L.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 2 — Water Quality Index (Weighted Arithmetic, Brown 1970)

#### Step 1 — Quality Rating $q_i$

The quality rating expresses the observed value as a percentage of the standard:

$$q_i = \frac{V_i}{S_i} \times 100$$

> *Example:* If observed nitrate is 20 mg/L and standard (IS 10500) is 45 mg/L → $q_i = (20/45) \times 100 = 44.4$.

#### Step 2 — Unit Weight $w_i$

The weight of a parameter is inversely proportional to its standard. To make weights sum to 1, a proportionality constant $K$ is introduced:

$$w_i = \frac{K}{S_i}$$

where $K$ is determined from the constraint $\sum w_i = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K}{S_i} = 1 \implies K = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/S_i)}$$

#### Step 3 — Sub-Index

$$SI_i = q_i \times w_i$$

#### Step 4 — Aggregate WQI

$$WQI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

Since $\sum w_i = 1$ (by normalisation), this simplifies to:

$$WQI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SI_i$$

---

### 3.4 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 2 — Full WQI Calculation

> **Problem:** Compute the Water Quality Index of a groundwater sample using the data below. (KTU 14-Mark sub-part)

| Parameter | Standard $S_i$ (IS 10500) | Observed $V_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| pH | 8.5 (max) | 7.2 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 500 | 380 |
| Nitrate (mg/L) | 45 | 22 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.0 | 0.6 |
| Chloride (mg/L) | 250 | 120 |

#### Step 1 — Compute $1/S_i$ and $K$

| Parameter | $S_i$ | $1/S_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| pH | 8.5 | 0.1176 |
| TDS | 500 | 0.0020 |
| Nitrate | 45 | 0.0222 |
| Fluoride | 1.0 | 1.0000 |
| Chloride | 250 | 0.0040 |
| **Sum** | — | **1.1458** |

$$K = \frac{1}{1.1458} = 0.8727$$

#### Step 2 — Compute $w_i = K / S_i$

| Parameter | $w_i = 0.8727 / S_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 0.1027 |
| TDS | 0.00175 |
| Nitrate | 0.01939 |
| Fluoride | 0.8727 |
| Chloride | 0.00349 |
| **Sum** | **1.0000** ✔ |

#### Step 3 — Compute $q_i = (V_i/S_i) \times 100$

| Parameter | $q_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 84.71 |
| TDS | 76.00 |
| Nitrate | 48.89 |
| Fluoride | 60.00 |
| Chloride | 48.00 |

#### Step 4 — Compute $SI_i = q_i \cdot w_i$

| Parameter | $SI_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 8.701 |
| TDS | 0.133 |
| Nitrate | 0.948 |
| Fluoride | 52.362 |
| Chloride | 0.168 |

#### Step 5 — Aggregate WQI

$$WQI = \sum SI_i = 8.701 + 0.133 + 0.948 + 52.362 + 0.168 = 62.31$$

> **Interpretation:** WQI = 62.31 falls in the **51 – 75 → "Poor"** band. The water is **suitable for irrigation** but **not for drinking** without treatment. The fluoride sub-index (52.36) is the dominant contributor; the regulator should demand a defluoridation unit in the project EMP.

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step BOD₅ ↔ L₀ Conversion (Frequently Tested)

> **Problem:** A wastewater sample has BOD₅ = 250 mg/L. $k_1$ = 0.25 day⁻¹ (base-e). Find the ultimate BOD ($L_0$).

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5}} = \frac{250}{1 - e^{-0.25 \times 5}} = \frac{250}{1 - e^{-1.25}}$$

$$e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$$

$$L_0 = \frac{250}{1 - 0.2865} = \frac{250}{0.7135} = 350.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

> **Reverse:** If $L_0$ = 350.4 mg/L and $k_1$ = 0.25 day⁻¹, BOD₅ = $L_0 (1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5}) = 350.4 \times 0.7135 = 250$ mg/L ✔ (Consistency check passes.)

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Traps

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfalls (Read Twice):**
> 1. **Units of $k_1, k_2$:** Always check whether the problem states *base-e* or *base-10*. If $k_1$ is given as $0.25 \text{ day}^{-1}$ (base-10), multiply by 2.303 → $k_1 = 0.576 \text{ day}^{-1}$ (base-e) before using in Streeter-Phelps.
> 2. **Time $t$:** The model uses $t$ in **days** since discharge. Convert hours → days (÷ 24).
> 3. **Saturation DO temperature dependence:** Always state the temperature. A 5 °C rise cuts $DO_{sat}$ by ~1 mg/L — this *alone* can tip a river below 4 mg/L.
> 4. **D₀ and $L_0$ mixing:** Many students forget to apply the *flow-weighted* mixing equation — they simply add. ❌ Use $Q_r, Q_w$ as weights.
> 5. **WQI normalisation:** $\sum w_i$ must equal exactly 1.00 — if not, the arithmetic is wrong.
> 6. **Sign convention:** $D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{0,measured}$. A *positive* $D_0$ means the river is *under*-saturated (a deficit exists).
