## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Water Quality Impact Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flow diagram maps the **complete procedural architecture** of WQIA as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus and the EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proposal Submission] --> B[Screening & Scoping Stage]
    B --> C{Is Water Body within 10 km Impact Zone?}
    C -->|Yes| D[List Valued Environmental Components VECs]
    C -->|No| E[Cat B - Minimal Water Impact]
    D --> F[Baseline Data Collection]
    F --> G[Pre-Monsoon Sampling]
    F --> H[Monsoon Sampling]
    F --> I[Post-Monsoon Sampling]
    F --> J[Winter Sampling]
    G --> K[Lab Analysis of 25 plus Parameters]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Water Quality Index WQI]
    K --> M[Run Streeter Phelps DO Sag Model]
    L --> N{Predicted WQI within Standards?}
    M --> O{Predicted DO min above 4 mg per Litre?}
    N -->|Yes| P[Proceed to EMP Design]
    N -->|No| Q[Revise Project Layout]
    O -->|Yes| P
    O -->|No| Q
    Q --> F
    P --> R[Design Effluent Treatment Plant ETP]
    P --> S[Design Storm Water Management]
    P --> T[Design Zero Liquid Discharge ZLD]
    P --> U[Submit EIA Report to SEIAA]
    U --> V{SEIAA Public Hearing}
    V -->|Approved| W[Install Continuous Water Quality Monitoring CWQMS]
    V -->|Rejected| X[Re-design and Re-submit]
    W --> Y[Six Monthly Compliance Reports to SPCB CPCB]
    Y --> Z[Long-term Environmental Audit]
```

### 4.2 Process-Flow Diagram — Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[River Upstream DO_r and BOD_r] --> M[Mixing with Wastewater]
    B[Waste Q_w BOD_w DO_w] --> M
    M --> C[Compute L0 and D0]
    C --> D[Compute Critical Time t_c]
    D --> E[Compute Critical Deficit D_c]
    E --> F[Compute DO_min = DO_sat minus D_c]
    F --> G{DO_min above 4 mg per Litre?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Acceptable - Proceed]
    G -->|No| I[Critical - Recommend ETP or Reduced Discharge]
```

### 4.3 Causal Loop — Sources of Water Quality Impact in EIA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Effluent Discharge] --> B[Increased BOD and COD]
    C[Agricultural Runoff] --> D[Increased Nutrient Load NO3 and PO4]
    E[Domestic Sewage] --> F[Increased Coliform and BOD]
    G[Deforestation Catchment] --> H[Increased Siltation and Turbidity]
    B --> I[River Self Purification Failure]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Aquatic Ecosystem Collapse Fish Kill]
    J --> K[EIA Recommends Mitigation]
    K --> L[ETP plus Constructed Wetland]
    K --> M[ZLD plus Recycling]
    K --> N[Catchment Afforestation]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Decision Matrix — Modelling Tools for WQIA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Quality Model Selection] --> B{Stream Type}
    B -->|Single Discharge Steady| C[Streeter Phelps Model]
    B -->|Multiple Diffuse Sources| D[QUAL2E or QUAL2K Model]
    B -->|Groundwater Aquifer| E[MODFLOW or MT3D Model]
    B -->|Coastal Estuary| F[MIKE 11 or Delft3D Model]
    C --> G[Output DO Sag Curve]
    D --> H[Output Spatially Distributed Concentrations]
    E --> I[Output Plume Migration]
    F --> J[Output Salinity and DO Field]
```

### 4.5 Interpretive Notes on the Diagrams

- **Diagram 4.1** captures the *legal-procedural* chain: from project proposal to long-term audit. Every step maps to a *Form* in the EIA Notification (Form-1, Form-1A, EIA Report, EMP, Compliance Report).
- **Diagram 4.2** is the *engineering-mathematical* heart: it shows that if $DO_{min}$ is below 4 mg/L, the project must redesign — this is the single most-cited reason for EIA rejection of thermal power plants, distilleries, and textile units in Kerala.
- **Diagram 4.3** identifies the **four primary anthropogenic sources** an EIA consultant must investigate.
- **Diagram 4.4** ensures students choose the *correct model* — KTU examiners often pose a "model selection" question (e.g., "Which model would you use for the backwaters near Kochi?" — answer: *MIKE 11 / Delft3D* for estuarine dynamics).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions (Direct Concepts)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Water Quality Index (WQI). List the four mathematical steps in the Weighted Arithmetic method of WQI computation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Water Quality Index (WQI) is a single dimensionless number (0 – 100+) that expresses the composite influence of multiple individual water quality parameters on the overall suitability of water for a designated use (drinking, irrigation, aquaculture, industrial cooling), by converting each parameter's measured value into a quality rating and aggregating them through a weighted arithmetic mean.

**Four Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Step 1 — Quality Rating:** $q_i = \dfrac{V_i}{S_i} \times 100$
2. **Step 2 — Unit Weight:** $w_i = \dfrac{K}{S_i}$, with $K = \dfrac{1}{\sum (1/S_i)}$
3. **Step 3 — Sub-Index:** $SI_i = q_i \cdot w_i$
4. **Step 4 — Aggregate WQI:** $WQI = \dfrac{\sum q_i w_i}{\sum w_i}$

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of BOD and DO as water quality parameters. What is the minimum DO required to sustain aquatic life as per CPCB classification?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) (1.5 marks):** BOD measures the *amount of dissolved oxygen* consumed by aerobic microorganisms while decomposing organic matter in water over a specified period (usually 5 days at 20 °C, denoted BOD₅). It is a direct indicator of *organic pollution* — higher BOD means more organic load, more oxygen consumption, and a greater threat to aquatic life. Domestic sewage typically has BOD₅ of 200–400 mg/L; clean river water has BOD₅ < 2 mg/L.

> **DO (Dissolved Oxygen) and CPCB Limit (1.5 marks):** DO is the amount of molecular oxygen ($O_2$) dissolved in water, essential for fish, invertebrates, and aerobic bacteria. Saturation at 20 °C is **9.17 mg/L**. As per **CPCB Surface Water Classification (IS 2296):** Class A (Drinking source) requires DO ≥ 6 mg/L; Class B (Outdoor bathing) ≥ 5 mg/L; Class C (Drinking after conventional treatment, aquatic life) ≥ 4 mg/L; Class D (Propagation of fish, wildlife) ≥ 4 mg/L. **The minimum DO threshold for sustaining aquatic life is 4 mg/L.**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question must offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives that test the *same Course Outcome (CO)* but at slightly *different cognitive levels* (Bloom's Level 3 vs. Level 4). The questions below strictly follow this pattern.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Paper]

**Question:**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **methodology of Water Quality Impact Analysis** in an EIA study. Discuss baseline data collection, identification of impacts, prediction models, and evaluation criteria. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A river has a flow of 10 m³/s, BOD₅ = 3 mg/L, DO = 7.5 mg/L, $k_1$ = 0.25 day⁻¹ (base-e), and velocity $v$ = 30 km/day. A wastewater discharge of 2 m³/s with BOD₅ = 200 mg/L, DO = 0 mg/L enters the river. Saturation DO at 25 °C is 8.38 mg/L. Calculate: **(i)** Mixed ultimate BOD ($L_0$); **(ii)** Mixed initial DO deficit ($D_0$); **(iii)** Critical time ($t_c$); **(iv)** Critical distance (km). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A **block diagram** (use Diagram 4.1 from Section 4 of these notes) showing the seven-stage methodology:

1. **Screening & Scoping (1 mark):** Identify whether the project (e.g., new township, industrial plant) lies within the impact zone (typically 10 km radius) of any notified water body. The Valued Environmental Components (VECs) — fish, aquatic flora, downstream users — are listed.

2. **Baseline Data Collection (2 marks):** Samples are collected across **four seasons** (pre-monsoon, monsoon, post-monsoon, winter) at a minimum of **4 stations** (upstream control, mixing zone, midstream impact, downstream recovery) per the CPCB **NWMP (National Water Quality Monitoring Programme)** protocol. Parameters analysed include pH, EC, TDS, BOD₅, COD, DO, nitrate, phosphate, sulphate, chlorides, total coliform, and heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr).

3. **Impact Identification & Prediction (2 marks):** Sources (point, non-point, land-use) are identified, and their **magnitude and extent** predicted using:
   - **Streeter-Phelps** (single point source, DO sag)
   - **QUAL2E / QUAL2K** (multi-source, river reach)
   - **MODFLOW / MT3D** (groundwater)
   - **MIKE 11 / Delft3D** (estuary)
4. **Evaluation & Significance (1 mark):** Predicted concentrations are compared with **IS 2296** (surface water classification), **IS 10500** (drinking), and **CPCB** designated best-use criteria. Significance is judged as Negligible / Minor / Moderate / Major.

5. **Mitigation & EMP (1 mark):** ETP, ZLD, constructed wetlands, rainwater harvesting, green belt along river bank, monitoring network (CWQMS).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $Q_r = 10$ m³/s, $BOD_{5,r} = 3$ mg/L, $DO_r = 7.5$ mg/L, $Q_w = 2$ m³/s, $BOD_{5,w} = 200$ mg/L, $DO_w = 0$ mg/L, $k_1 = k_2 = 0.25$ day⁻¹ (assume $k_2 = k_1$ in worst case; better to assume $k_2 = 2k_1$ = 0.5 for typical river; we use $k_2 = 0.5$ day⁻¹).

> **Note for the student:** When $k_2$ is not given, KTU convention is to assume $k_2 = 2k_1$ for moderately flowing rivers. We adopt $k_2 = 0.5$ day⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Convert BOD₅ to L₀ (using $e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$):**
$$L_{0,river} = \frac{3}{0.7135} = 4.205 \text{ mg/L}$$
$$L_{0,waste} = \frac{200}{0.7135} = 280.31 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Mixed $L_0$:**
$$L_0 = \frac{(10)(4.205) + (2)(280.31)}{12} = \frac{42.05 + 560.62}{12} = \frac{602.67}{12} = 50.22 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Mixed $DO_0$:**
$$DO_0 = \frac{(10)(7.5) + (2)(0)}{12} = \frac{75}{12} = 6.25 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Initial Deficit $D_0$:**
$$D_0 = 8.38 - 6.25 = 2.13 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Time $t_c$:**
$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{0.5 - 0.25}\ln\left[\frac{0.5}{0.25}\left(1 - \frac{2.13 \times 0.25}{0.25 \times 50.22}\right)\right]$$
$$= 4 \times \ln\left[2 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.5325}{12.555}\right)\right]$$
$$= 4 \times \ln[2 \times (1 - 0.04241)]$$
$$= 4 \times \ln[2 \times 0.9576] = 4 \times \ln[1.9152]$$
$$= 4 \times 0.6496 = 2.598 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Critical Distance:**
$$X_c = v \cdot t_c = 30 \text{ km/day} \times 2.598 \text{ days} = 77.95 \text{ km}$$

> **Conclusion:** The river's DO minimum occurs ~78 km downstream of the outfall. If the river is shorter than 78 km before entering a reservoir, estuary, or another town, the impact is **catastrophic**. EIA must mandate pre-treatment or partial flow augmentation.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Model Paper]

**Question:**
> **(a)** What is **Water Quality Index (WQI)**? Explain the Weighted Arithmetic method with formulas. Compute the WQI for the following groundwater data using **IS 10500** standards: **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Standard $S_i$ | Observed $V_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| pH | 8.5 | 7.4 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 500 | 600 |
| Nitrate (mg/L) | 45 | 50 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.0 | 1.4 |
| Chloride (mg/L) | 250 | 200 |

> **(b)** Discuss the **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Model** with assumptions, governing equations, and the significance of critical time and critical deficit. What is the practical importance of this model in EIA? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Quality Rating $q_i = (V_i / S_i) \times 100$:**

| Parameter | $V_i$ | $S_i$ | $q_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| pH | 7.4 | 8.5 | 87.06 |
| TDS | 600 | 500 | 120.00 |
| Nitrate | 50 | 45 | 111.11 |
| Fluoride | 1.4 | 1.0 | 140.00 |
| Chloride | 200 | 250 | 80.00 |

**Step 2 — Unit Weight (compute $K$ first):**

| Parameter | $1/S_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 0.1176 |
| TDS | 0.0020 |
| Nitrate | 0.0222 |
| Fluoride | 1.0000 |
| Chloride | 0.0040 |
| **Sum** | **1.1458** |

$$K = \frac{1}{1.1458} = 0.8727$$

| Parameter | $w_i = K / S_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 0.1027 |
| TDS | 0.00175 |
| Nitrate | 0.01939 |
| Fluoride | 0.8727 |
| Chloride | 0.00349 |

**Step 3 — Sub-Index $SI_i = q_i \cdot w_i$:**

| Parameter | $SI_i$ |
|:---|:---:|
| pH | 8.94 |
| TDS | 0.21 |
| Nitrate | 2.15 |
| Fluoride | 122.18 |
| Chloride | 0.28 |

**Step 4 — WQI:**
$$WQI = \sum SI_i = 8.94 + 0.21 + 2.15 + 122.18 + 0.28 = 133.76$$

> **Conclusion:** WQI = 133.76 → **> 100 → "Unsuitable for drinking"**. The fluoride sub-index (122.18) dominates due to its low standard (1.0 mg/L). **Recommendation:** EIA should mandate a *defluoridation plant* and reject the project layout if remediation is unfeasible.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Assumptions of Streeter-Phelps (1.5 marks):**
1. Steady-state, single point-source discharge.
2. Constant river flow, velocity, and cross-section.
3. One-dimensional flow (longitudinal mixing only).
4. BOD decays as first-order reaction ($\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L$).
5. Reaeration is first-order ($\frac{dD}{dt}_{reaeration} = -k_2 D$).
6. Only carbonaceous BOD is considered (no nitrification).
7. No longitudinal dispersion.

**Governing Equations (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L \implies L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D$$

$$\implies D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

**Critical Time & Deficit (1.5 marks):**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right] \quad \text{(when } D_0 = 0\text{)}$$

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

The critical point is the **lowest DO** in the river, occurring at distance $X_c = v \cdot t_c$ downstream of the outfall. This is the *worst-case* impact location.

**Practical Importance in EIA (2.5 marks):**
- Determines the **assimilation capacity** of a river — how much waste it can absorb without breaching the **4 mg/L DO** threshold mandated by CPCB.
- Helps decide **effluent BOD limits** in the **Consent to Establish / Consent to Operate** issued by the **State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB in Kerala)**.
- Predicts **aquatic ecosystem collapse** (fish kills) at specific downstream distances — used to design **mixing zones** and **buffer zones**.
- Forms the scientific basis for **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** recommendations: pre-treatment, flow augmentation, ZLD.
- For Kerala-specific projects (distilleries, rubber, coir, seafood processing near Periyar, Chalakudy, Pamba rivers), the model is *mandatory* in EIA submissions.
- Limitations: ignores nitrification, sediment oxygen demand, and algal photosynthesis. Refined models like **QUAL2E** and **WASP** address these.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in WQIA Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert BOD₅ → L₀:** Most students plug BOD₅ directly into the Streeter-Phelps equation. The model requires *ultimate* BOD ($L_0$). Examiner deducts **2 marks** for this single error.
> 2. **Mixing flow weights wrong:** Some students *add* the BODs of river and waste instead of computing a *flow-weighted average*. Loss: **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing base-e vs. base-10:** $k_1, k_2$ must be in **base-e (natural log)** for the Streeter-Phelps equation as derived. If the problem gives base-10, convert: $k_{e} = 2.303 \times k_{10}$.
> 4. **Not stating units:** Always write mg/L for concentrations, day⁻¹ for rate constants, and km (or days) for distance. Loss: **0.5 marks per missing unit**.
> 5. **WQI normalisation error:** $\sum w_i$ should equal **1.000**. If your sum is 1.05 or 0.92, the examiner knows arithmetic is wrong.
> 6. **Ignoring saturation DO temperature:** Using 9.17 mg/L when the problem states 25 °C (where saturation is 8.38 mg/L) is a 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Not interpreting the result:** Every numerical answer must end with a **commentary sentence** — "DO is above 4 mg/L, hence the river can sustain aquatic life" or "WQI = 133 indicates water is unsuitable for drinking." Loss if missing: **1 mark**.
> 8. **Diagrams without labels:** In 7-mark sub-parts, any diagram must have *labelled arrows* and a *legend*. Unlabelled flowcharts lose up to **2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Memorise Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **WQIA** quantifies project impact on water using parameters (physical, chemical, biological) compared to **IS 10500**, **IS 2296**, and **CPCB** standards.
- **BOD₅ → L₀ conversion:** $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k_1}}$ (base-e).
- **Streeter-Phelps Sag Equation:** $D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$.
- **Critical Time:** $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left[\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right]$ (when $D_0 \to 0$).
- **Critical Deficit:** $D_c = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$.
- **Minimum DO Threshold (CPCB):** 4 mg/L for Class C/D (aquatic life).
- **WQI Quality Rating:** $q_i = \dfrac{V_i}{S_i} \times 100$.
- **WQI Unit Weight:** $w_i = \dfrac{K}{S_i}$ where $K = \dfrac{1}{\sum(1/S_i)}$.
- **WQI Aggregate:** $WQI = \sum q_i w_i$ (since $\sum w_i = 1$).
- **WQI Classification:** 0–25 Excellent, 26–50 Good, 51–75 Poor, 76–100 Very Poor, >100 Unsuitable.
- **Flow-weighted mixing** is mandatory for both BOD and DO at outfall confluences.
- **Fluoride standard (IS 10500):** 1.0 mg/L — it carries the highest unit weight, dominating WQI whenever exceeded.
- **Nitrate standard (IS 10500):** 45 mg/L — exceeding this causes **methaemoglobinemia (blue baby syndrome)** in infants.
- **Heavy metals** (Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr⁶⁺) are **non-degradable**; WQI must include them for any industrial EIA.
- **Groundwater projects** → use **MODFLOW**; **Estuarine / coastal projects** (Kerala backwaters) → use **MIKE 11 / Delft3D**.
- **Mitigation hierarchy:** Avoidance → Minimisation → Restoration → Offset.
- **EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)** mandates WQIA for Category A and B1 projects within **5 km of any notified water body**.
- **Continuous Water Quality Monitoring Stations (CWQMS)** are now mandatory at every major outfall under the **CEMS programme**.
- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** is the *default mitigation recommendation* for new thermal power plants and distilleries in Kerala.

> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In the 14-mark numerical, always show **at least 6–8 explicit steps**, **state units**, **convert BOD₅ to L₀**, and **end with an interpretation sentence**. This single discipline can lift your score by **6–8 marks** out of 14.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Quality Criteria and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Quality Criteria and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Quality Criteria** are scientifically derived numerical limits or qualitative statements describing the quality of water required to support a designated use (drinking, bathing, irrigation, aquatic life, industrial cooling, etc.). **Water Quality Standards** are the legally enforceable permissible limits of various physico-chemical and biological parameters in water, established by statutory bodies to protect human health and aquatic ecosystems.

In the context of **Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA)**, water quality criteria and standards form the *baseline* against which the **predicted impacts** of a proposed project (e.g., effluent discharge, thermal pollution, sedimentation) are compared during the *scoping*, *impact prediction*, and *mitigation* stages.

The principal statutory frameworks relevant to India are:
- **IS 10500 (BIS, 2012)** — Drinking Water Specification
- **IS 2296 (BIS, 1982, amended 2014)** — Surface Water Quality Standards
- **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th Edition, 2017)**
- **CPCB Schedule VI, The Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986** — Effluent Discharge Standards
- **EPA, 1986 (India)** — umbrella legislation empowering the Central Government to set standards

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a hospital blood-test report. Your doctor does not simply say "your blood is fine" — she lists dozens of parameters (sugar, cholesterol, RBC count) with permissible *ranges*. If glucose is 250 mg/dL, that's a red flag. Water bodies function exactly the same way for an ecosystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a river as the *circulatory system of a landscape*. Water Quality Standards are the "lab report reference ranges" for this living system. Just as human blood cannot have >200 mg/dL cholesterol without illness, river water cannot have **BOD > 3 mg/L** in Class A stretches (IS 2296) without harming aquatic life.

When an EIA consultant predicts that a new textile mill will discharge effluent with **BOD = 350 mg/L**, the standards immediately tell us *how far* this is from acceptable, and therefore, how much treatment (mitigation) is required before discharge.

## 1.3 Categories of Water Quality Parameters

Water quality parameters are broadly classified into three families:

| Category | Examples | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Turbidity, Colour, Odour, Taste, Temperature, **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**, **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | Affect aesthetics and usability |
| **Chemical** | **pH**, Hardness, Chloride, Fluoride, Nitrate, Sulphate, Heavy Metals (Pb, As, Hg, Cd), COD, BOD | Govern health & toxicity |
| **Biological** | Total Coliforms, Faecal Coliforms, E. coli, Total Plate Count | Indicate pathogenic risk |

> [!NOTE]
> The **most heavily tested parameters** in any KTU numerical problem are **pH, Total Hardness, TDS, Chloride, Fluoride, Nitrate, Sulphate, BOD, COD, Dissolved Oxygen (DO), and Coliforms**. Memorise the BIS limits for these — they recur every year.

## 1.4 Designated Best Uses & Classification (IS 2296)

The Bureau of Indian Standards classifies inland surface waters into **five classes** based on the *Best Designated Use* (BDU):

| Class | Designated Best Use |
|---|---|
| **A** | Drinking water source without conventional treatment (but after disinfection) |
| **B** | Outdoor bathing (organoleptic-free) |
| **C** | Drinking water source with conventional treatment |
| **D** | Propagation of fish and aquatic life |
| **E** | Irrigation, industrial cooling, controlled waste disposal |

Each class has its own **mandatory primary water quality criteria** (see SECTION 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Standards Different for Different Uses?

The same parameter has **different permissible limits** depending on intended use. For example:
- **Fluoride** ≤ **1.0 mg/L** for drinking (IS 10500); but unlimited for industrial cooling.
- **Dissolved Oxygen** ≥ **6 mg/L** for Class A drinking; ≥ **4 mg/L** for Class D fisheries.
- **Faecal Coliforms** ≤ **50 MPN/100 mL** for Class A; but *zero tolerance* for treated drinking water.

The reason is **risk-based science**: drinking water enters the human gut, hence microbial limits are extremely tight. Industrial water only enters boilers/heat exchangers, so hardness and silica limits dominate.

## 2.2 Critical Parameters — Engineering Logic & Standards

### 2.2.1 pH
- **Definition:** Negative logarithm of hydrogen ion activity, pH = −log[H⁺].
- **Range in natural waters:** **6.5 – 8.5** (IS 10500).
- **Why it matters:** Below 6.5, water becomes corrosive (leaches Pb, Cu from pipes). Above 8.5, it tastes soapy and reduces disinfection efficiency of chlorine.

### 2.2.2 Total Hardness (TH)
- **Definition:** Concentration of divalent cations (mainly Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺), expressed as **mg/L CaCO₃**.
- **Hardness equation:**

$$
\text{TH (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} \;=\; 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] \;+\; 4.1 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]
$$

where ion concentrations are in mg/L.
- **IS 10500 limit:** **200 mg/L (desirable), 600 mg/L (permissible max).**

### 2.2.3 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)
- **Definition:** Amount of dissolved oxygen (mg/L) consumed by microorganisms while oxidising biodegradable organic matter over **5 days at 20 °C**.
- **Standard test:** BOD₅ at 20 °C, 3-day seed, etc.
- **Drinking limit:** **< 2 mg/L**. Surface water (Class A): **< 2 mg/L**.

### 2.2.4 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)
- **Definition:** Oxygen equivalent of organic matter oxidised by strong chemical oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇) in acidic medium.
- **Relation:** **COD > BOD always**, because COD oxidises both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics.
- **Typical ratio for municipal sewage:** COD/BOD ≈ **2.0 – 2.5**.

### 2.2.5 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)
- **Saturation value at 20 °C and 1 atm:** **9.2 mg/L**.
- **Class D fisheries:** **≥ 4 mg/L (minimum).** Below 4 mg/L → fish mortality.

### 2.2.6 Total Coliforms & Faecal Coliforms
- Measured as **MPN/100 mL** (Most Probable Number) or CFU/100 mL.
- **IS 10500:** Total coliforms — **shall not be detectable in any 100 mL sample** (essentially 0 MPN/100 mL).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula and standard value you must memorise for KTU ESE Part B numericals. **Replace the vertical bar `|` with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | IS 10500 Limit (Drinking) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | $-\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | **6.5 – 8.5** | — |
| Total Hardness | $2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ (mg/L) | **200 (D), 600 (P)** | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| TDS | $\sum (\text{cations} + \text{anions})$ | **500 (D), 2000 (P)** | mg/L |
| Chloride | $\text{AgNO}_3$ titration | **250 (D), 1000 (P)** | mg/L |
| Fluoride | SPADNS / Ion-selective electrode | **1.0 (D), 1.5 (P)** | mg/L |
| Nitrate (as NO₃⁻) | UV spectrophotometry | **45 (P)** | mg/L |
| Sulphate (as SO₄²⁻) | Barium chloride turbidity | **200 (D), 400 (P)** | mg/L |
| Iron (Fe) | Phenanthroline | **0.3 (P)** | mg/L |
| Lead (Pb) | AAS | **0.01 (P)** | mg/L |
| Arsenic (As) | AAS / SDDC | **0.01 (P), 0.05 (relaxed)** | mg/L |
| BOD₅ | 5-day, 20 °C, Winkler | **2 (P)** | mg/L |
| COD | K₂Cr₂O₇ reflux | No specific limit | mg/L |
| DO | Winkler / DO meter | No specific limit; ≥ 6 desired | mg/L |
| Total Coliforms | MPN / Membrane filtration | **0 MPN / 100 mL** | MPN/100 mL |

*(D = Desirable; P = Permissible in absence of alternate source)*

## 2.4 Surface Water Classification (IS 2296) — Primary Criteria

| Class | DO (min) | BOD₅ (max) | Total Coliforms (max) | pH |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 6 mg/L | 2 mg/L | 50 MPN/100 mL | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **B** | 5 mg/L | 3 mg/L | 500 MPN/100 mL | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **C** | 4 mg/L | 3 mg/L | 5000 MPN/100 mL | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **D** | 4 mg/L | — | — | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **E** | — | — | — | 6.5 – 8.5 |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in EIA

In a typical EIA report for a **thermal power plant** discharging condenser cooling water:
- ΔTemperature ≤ **5 °C** at mixing zone boundary (CPCB guideline).
- DO must remain ≥ **4 mg/L** downstream even under worst-case low-flow.
- Free chlorine residual ≤ **0.5 mg/L** (toxicity to aquatic life).

For a **distillery**, BOD of spent wash can be **40,000 – 50,000 mg/L**. Standards force installation of biomethanation + RO — otherwise the receiving river's DO crashes to zero, causing massive fish kill.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Water Quality Index (WQI) — Weighted Arithmetic Mean Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Water Quality Index (WQI)** is the single most frequent 14-mark numerical question in KTU Module 4. It condenses 9–11 parameters into a single number (0–100) describing overall fitness for drinking.

### 3.1.1 Definition

$$
\text{WQI} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Each Component

**Step 1 — Quality Rating (Qi):** Dimensionless number indicating *how badly* parameter i deviates from its standard.

$$
Q_i \;=\; 100 \times \left[\frac{V_i - I_i}{S_i - I_i}\right]
$$

- $V_i$ = measured value of $i$-th parameter
- $S_i$ = BIS standard (desirable limit) of $i$-th parameter
- $I_i$ = ideal value (= 0 for most parameters; for pH, $I_i = 7$)

**Step 2 — Unit Weight (Wi):** Inverse proportionality to standard, giving stricter parameters higher weight.

$$
W_i \;=\; \frac{K}{S_i}
$$

where $K$ is the proportionality constant (typically chosen as $K = 1$).

**Step 3 — Aggregation:** Substitute $Q_i$ and $W_i$ into the master WQI equation.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{WQI} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K}{S_i} \cdot 100 \cdot \left[\dfrac{V_i - I_i}{S_i - I_i}\right]}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{K}{S_i}}\;}
$$

The constant $K$ and the factor $100$ cancel out numerically if consistent units are used, but are retained for dimensional clarity.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

A groundwater sample analysis gives the following data:

| Parameter | Measured $V_i$ | Standard $S_i$ (IS 10500) | Ideal $I_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.8 | 8.5 | 7 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 850 | 500 | 0 |
| TH (mg/L) | 320 | 200 | 0 |
| Cl⁻ (mg/L) | 220 | 250 | 0 |
| F⁻ (mg/L) | 1.2 | 1.0 | 0 |
| NO₃⁻ (mg/L) | 50 | 45 | 0 |
| SO₄²⁻ (mg/L) | 180 | 200 | 0 |

**Compute Wi (with K = 1):**

$$
W_{\text{pH}} = \frac{1}{8.5} = 0.118
$$
$$
W_{\text{TDS}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002
$$
$$
W_{\text{TH}} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005
$$
$$
W_{\text{Cl}} = \frac{1}{250} = 0.004
$$
$$
W_{\text{F}} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1.000
$$
$$
W_{\text{NO3}} = \frac{1}{45} = 0.0222
$$
$$
W_{\text{SO4}} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005
$$

$$
\sum W_i = 0.118 + 0.002 + 0.005 + 0.004 + 1.000 + 0.0222 + 0.005 = 1.1562
$$

**Compute Qi:**

$$
Q_{\text{pH}} = 100 \times \frac{7.8 - 7}{8.5 - 7} = 100 \times \frac{0.8}{1.5} = 53.33
$$
$$
Q_{\text{TDS}} = 100 \times \frac{850 - 0}{500 - 0} = 170.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{TH}} = 100 \times \frac{320 - 0}{200 - 0} = 160.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{Cl}} = 100 \times \frac{220 - 0}{250 - 0} = 88.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{F}} = 100 \times \frac{1.2 - 0}{1.0 - 0} = 120.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{NO3}} = 100 \times \frac{50 - 0}{45 - 0} = 111.11
$$
$$
Q_{\text{SO4}} = 100 \times \frac{180 - 0}{200 - 0} = 90.00
$$

**Compute Wi × Qi:**

| Parameter | Wi | Qi | Wi × Qi |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 0.118 | 53.33 | 6.293 |
| TDS | 0.002 | 170.00 | 0.340 |
| TH | 0.005 | 160.00 | 0.800 |
| Cl⁻ | 0.004 | 88.00 | 0.352 |
| F⁻ | 1.000 | 120.00 | 120.000 |
| NO₃⁻ | 0.0222 | 111.11 | 2.467 |
| SO₄²⁻ | 0.005 | 90.00 | 0.450 |
| **Σ** | **1.1562** | — | **130.702** |

**Final WQI:**

$$
\text{WQI} = \frac{130.702}{1.1562} = 113.04
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Scale (Brown, 1970 / ICMR):**
> WQI < 50 → Excellent; 50–100 → Good; 100–200 → Poor; 200–300 → Very Poor; > 300 → Unsuitable for drinking.
>
> Here **WQI = 113.04 → "Poor"**. Fluoride is the dominant pollutant (Wi = 1.0, Qi = 120) — exactly as the unit-weight logic predicted.

### 3.2 Hardness Calculation — Sample Derivation

A water sample has Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L and Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L. Compute total hardness as CaCO₃.

$$
\text{TH} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]
$$

$$
\text{TH} = 2.5 \times 80 + 4.1 \times 24 = 200 + 98.4 = 298.4 \; \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3
$$

The factors **2.5 and 4.1** come from:
- Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50; Atomic weight of Ca = 40 → 50/40 = 1.25. But Ca²⁺ is reported as Ca, so factor = 100/(2 × 40) = 1.25. Standard hardness in mg/L CaCO₃ uses 100/40 = **2.5** because $\text{CaCO}_3$ equivalent weight per mg Ca = 100/40.
- For Mg: 100/(2 × 24.3) ≈ **2.06**, but the classical convention in environmental engineering uses **4.12** (≈ 4.1) when converting mg/L of Mg²⁺ to CaCO₃-equivalent, accounting for the divalent stoichiometry: 100/24.3 × ½ × 2 = ... — actually the formula in textbooks is **4.1** for Mg when both ion and hardness are reported as the element/CaCO₃ respectively.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Standard

| Project Type | Primary Governing Standard | Most Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Drinking-water treatment plant | IS 10500 / WHO | Total Coliforms, Fluoride |
| Textile effluent discharge | CPCB Schedule VI, Part A | BOD, COD, Colour, TDS |
| Thermal power (cooling water) | CPCB thermal norms | ΔT, free Cl₂ residual |
| Coastal aquaculture | IS 2296 Class D | DO, Salinity |
| Irrigation project | FAO / CPCB irrigation norms | SAR, EC, Residual Sodium Carbonate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Indian Water-Quality Governance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Water Resources in India"] --> nodeB["EPA 1986 Umbrella Act"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CPCB Central Board"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["SPCB State Boards"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Effluent Standards Schedule VI"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["IS 10500 Drinking Water"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["IS 2296 Surface Water Classes"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Class A Drinking"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["Class B Bathing"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["Class C Drinking with Treatment"]
    nodeG --> nodeG3["Class D Fisheries"]
    nodeG --> nodeG4["Class E Irrigation"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Industry Specific Norms"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["Textile"]
    nodeH --> nodeH2["Distillery"]
    nodeH --> nodeH3["Pulp and Paper"]
    nodeH --> nodeH4["Thermal Power"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Local Monitoring and Enforcement"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the WQI Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Field Sampling] --> nodeB[Lab Analysis of Parameters]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Database of Measured Values Vi]
    nodeD[BIS Standards Si Database] --> nodeE[Compute Unit Weight Wi]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Compute Quality Rating Qi]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG[Compute Wi times Qi]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Sum Numerator and Denominator]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Final WQI Value]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Interpretation}
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Less than 50 Excellent"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["50 to 100 Good"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["100 to 200 Poor"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["200 to 300 Very Poor"]
    nodeJ --> nodeO["Greater than 300 Unsuitable"]
```

## 4.3 Inter-Parameter Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Temperature"] --> nodeB["DO Saturation"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["BOD Reaction Rate"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Aquatic Life Survival"]
    nodeE["pH"] --> nodeF["Heavy Metal Speciation"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Chlorine Disinfection Efficiency"]
    nodeH["TDS"] --> nodeI["Taste Threshold"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Boiler Scaling"]
    nodeK["Hardness"] --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeL["Soap Consumption"]
    nodeM["Coliforms"] --> nodeN["Epidemic Risk"]
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeO["Fluoride"] --> nodeP["Skeletal Fluorosis"]
    nodeO --> nodeQ["Dental Fluorosis"]
    nodeR["Nitrate"] --> nodeS["Methemoglobinemia Blue Baby"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each arrow above is a *cause-effect pathway* the KTU examiner expects you to mention when explaining *why* a parameter has a particular limit. For instance, "Fluoride ≤ 1.5 mg/L because beyond this, skeletal fluorosis sets in" scores higher than rote memorisation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Water Quality Criteria and Water Quality Standards.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Water Quality Criteria | Water Quality Standards |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Scientific recommendations based on toxicological and epidemiological data | Legally enforceable numerical limits |
| Source | Research bodies, WHO, ICMR | Statutory bodies (CPCB, BIS, SPCB) |
| Authority | Advisory only | Mandatory; non-compliance attracts penalty under EPA 1986 |
| Flexibility | Revised periodically with new research | Requires gazette notification to amend |
| Example | "Fluoride ≤ 1.5 mg/L prevents skeletal fluorosis" | "Fluoride ≤ 1.0 mg/L desirable, ≤ 1.5 mg/L permissible — IS 10500" |

(Any 3 well-articulated points × 1 mark = 3 marks.)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What are the designated best uses of water as per IS 2296? List the five classes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 2296) classifies inland surface waters into **five classes (A–E)** based on designated best use:
1. **Class A** — Drinking water source without conventional treatment (only disinfection required).
2. **Class B** — Outdoor bathing (organoleptic parameters controlled).
3. **Class C** — Drinking water source with conventional treatment (coagulation, filtration, disinfection).
4. **Class D** — Propagation of fish and aquatic life (DO ≥ 4 mg/L mandatory).
5. **Class E** — Irrigation, industrial cooling, controlled waste disposal.

(½ mark each for naming + ½ mark each for correct use description × 5 classes = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE of the two]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the BIS classification of surface water (IS 2296). Tabulate the primary water quality criteria for each class. Discuss how this classification drives the **mitigation measures** specified in an EIA report for an industrial project on a river bank.

**(b) [7 marks]** A groundwater sample was analysed and the following results were obtained:

| Parameter | Measured $V_i$ | Standard $S_i$ | Ideal $I_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.6 | 8.5 | 7 |
| TH (mg/L) | 280 | 200 | 0 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 720 | 500 | 0 |
| Cl⁻ (mg/L) | 180 | 250 | 0 |
| F⁻ (mg/L) | 0.9 | 1.0 | 0 |

Compute the **Water Quality Index (WQI)** using the weighted arithmetic mean method. Comment on the suitability of this water for drinking.

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** standard **IS 2296:1982 (with amendments up to 2014)** classifies inland surface waters into **5 classes (A–E)** based on the *Designated Best Use (DBU)* approach. The philosophy is that **the highest intended use governs the quality required** for the entire stretch — there is no averaging between classes.

**Primary Water Quality Criteria (Tabular — 3 marks):**

| Class | DBU | DO min (mg/L) | BOD max (mg/L) | Total Coliforms max (MPN/100 mL) | pH |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Drinking (no treatment) | 6 | 2 | 50 | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **B** | Outdoor bathing | 5 | 3 | 500 | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **C** | Drinking (with treatment) | 4 | 3 | 5000 | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **D** | Fisheries | 4 | — | — | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **E** | Irrigation/Cooling | — | — | — | 6.5 – 8.5 |

**Role in EIA Mitigation (4 marks):**
1. The EIA consultant first establishes the *baseline* DBU of the receiving river (e.g., "Class B — Bathing").
2. The consultant then computes the **assimilative capacity** using DO sag curve (Streeter–Phelps) — how much BOD load the river can absorb without dropping DO below the Class B limit of 5 mg/L.
3. The required **effluent BOD at discharge point** is back-calculated from assimilative capacity, and the **treatment plant efficiency** is specified (e.g., "Effluent BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, so STP must achieve ≥ 95% reduction from raw 600 mg/L").
4. If baseline is Class A (drinking), the mitigation is far more stringent, often forcing *zero liquid discharge (ZLD)*.
5. Monitoring frequency, location of sampling, and emergency action plans are all referenced back to the IS 2296 class.

#### Part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Unit Weight Wi = K/Si (with K = 1):**

$$
W_{\text{pH}} = 1/8.5 = 0.1176
$$
$$
W_{\text{TH}} = 1/200 = 0.0050
$$
$$
W_{\text{TDS}} = 1/500 = 0.0020
$$
$$
W_{\text{Cl}} = 1/250 = 0.0040
$$
$$
W_{\text{F}} = 1/1.0 = 1.0000
$$

$$
\sum W_i = 0.1176 + 0.0050 + 0.0020 + 0.0040 + 1.0000 = 1.1286
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Quality Rating Qi:**

$$
Q_{\text{pH}} = 100 \times \frac{7.6 - 7}{8.5 - 7} = 100 \times \frac{0.6}{1.5} = 40.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{TH}} = 100 \times \frac{280 - 0}{200 - 0} = 140.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{TDS}} = 100 \times \frac{720 - 0}{500 - 0} = 144.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{Cl}} = 100 \times \frac{180 - 0}{250 - 0} = 72.00
$$
$$
Q_{\text{F}} = 100 \times \frac{0.9 - 0}{1.0 - 0} = 90.00
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Wi × Qi and Sum:**

| Parameter | Wi | Qi | Wi × Qi |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 0.1176 | 40.00 | 4.704 |
| TH | 0.0050 | 140.00 | 0.700 |
| TDS | 0.0020 | 144.00 | 0.288 |
| Cl⁻ | 0.0040 | 72.00 | 0.288 |
| F⁻ | 1.0000 | 90.00 | 90.000 |
| **Σ** | **1.1286** | — | **95.980** |

**Step 4 — Compute WQI:**

$$
\text{WQI} = \frac{\sum W_i Q_i}{\sum W_i} = \frac{95.980}{1.1286} = 85.05
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- WQI in the range **50–100 = "Good"** water quality.
- However, individual parameters TH (140) and TDS (144) have Qi > 100 — meaning they exceed BIS desirable limits and require attention (softening for TH, RO/membrane for TDS).
- Fluoride is within limits (Qi = 90 < 100).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (i) Many students forget the **ideal value** for pH ($I = 7$), treating it as 0 — *lose 1 mark*.
> (ii) Some forget to convert Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ to **CaCO₃-equivalent** when computing hardness — *lose 2 marks*.
> (iii) Using *permissible* limits (e.g., TH 600) instead of *desirable* limits (200) when computing WQI — both approaches are accepted but must be declared in the answer.
> (iv) **Always show the WQI formula explicitly before substitution** — evaluators award partial credit on the framework even if the arithmetic is wrong.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the methodology adopted by **CPCB for setting effluent discharge standards** in India. List at least **five industry-specific effluent standards** with key parameters and limits.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (2017)**. Compare the WHO guideline values with **IS 10500 (2012)** for at least six parameters. Highlight any significant deviations.

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Methodology Flowchart (textual description — 2 marks):**
1. **Hazard Identification** — List toxic substances in each industry (e.g., chromium in tanneries).
2. **Exposure Assessment** — Determine ingestion/inhalation/dermal exposure routes for downstream users.
3. **Toxicological Database** — Refer to NOAEL/LOAEL data, EPA IRIS, WHO documents.
4. **Setting Maximum Acceptable Concentration (MAC)** — Apply safety factors (typically 100× for non-cancer, 1000× for cancer endpoints).
5. **Technology-Based Limits** — Consider what is achievable with Best Available Treatment Technology (BAT) at reasonable cost.
6. **Risk Characterisation & Public Consultation** — Stakeholder inputs from industry, NGOs, state boards.
7. **Notification** — Published in the *Gazette of India* under **Schedule VI, EP Rules 1986**.

**Industry-Specific Standards (5 marks):**

| Industry | Key Parameters | Discharge Limits |
|---|---|---|
| **Common Effluent Treatment Plant** | pH, BOD, COD, TSS, Oil & Grease | pH 6.5–7.5; BOD ≤ 30 mg/L; COD ≤ 250 mg/L |
| **Distillery (spent wash)** | BOD, COD, TDS, Colour | BOD ≤ 100 mg/L; TDS ≤ 2100 mg/L |
| **Pulp & Paper** | BOD, COD, Colour, AOX | BOD ≤ 30 mg/L; Colour ≤ 200 Pt-Co |
| **Tannery** | pH, Cr (total), TDS, Sulphide | pH 6.5–9.0; Cr ≤ 2 mg/L; Sulphide ≤ 2 mg/L |
| **Thermal Power** | ΔT, free Cl₂, Suspended Solids | ΔT ≤ 5 °C; Free Cl₂ ≤ 0.5 mg/L |

#### Part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

The **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality, 4th Edition (2017)** are a science-based reference for setting national standards worldwide. They are *not legally binding* — countries adapt them. **IS 10500:2012** is India's legally enforceable adaptation.

**Comparative Table (6 marks):**

| Parameter | WHO Guideline (2017) | IS 10500 (2012) | Significant Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead (Pb) | 0.01 mg/L (provisional) | 0.01 mg/L (permissible) | Aligned |
| Arsenic (As) | 0.01 mg/L (provisional) | 0.01 mg/L (permissible); 0.05 mg/L (relaxed) | **IS 10500 relaxed to 0.05 in absence of alternate source** |
| Fluoride | 1.5 mg/L | 1.0 mg/L desirable, 1.5 mg/L permissible | India is stricter due to endemic fluorosis zones |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | 50 mg/L (short-term) | 45 mg/L (permissible) | Aligned within rounding |
| Cadmium (Cd) | 0.003 mg/L | 0.003 mg/L (permissible) | Aligned |
| Mercury (Hg) | 0.006 mg/L | 0.001 mg/L (permissible) | **India is stricter** |
| E. coli / Thermotolerant coliforms | 0 CFU/100 mL (acute) | 0 MPN/100 mL | Aligned |

**Key Observations (1 mark):**
- India is **stricter** for mercury, fluoride, and lead because of localised contamination hotspots (Kerala's Endosulfan legacy, Rajasthan fluorosis belt).
- IS 10500 allows *two-tier* values (desirable + permissible max), giving regulatory flexibility during supply scarcity — a feature WHO does not have.
- WHO emphasises *risk-based* approach with health-based targets; BIS retains a more *prescriptive* format.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- **Five classes of IS 2296** — A (drinking-no treatment), B (bathing), C (drinking-with treatment), D (fisheries), E (irrigation). DO requirements: **6, 5, 4, 4, no-spec** respectively.
- **BIS drinking water standard** — IS 10500 (2012). Two-tier: **Desirable** vs **Permissible** limits.
- **WHO Guideline 4th Edn (2017)** — reference for global standards; not legally binding.
- **WQI formula** — **WQI = Σ Wi Qi / Σ Wi**, with Wi = K/Si and Qi = 100 × (Vi − Ii) / (Si − Ii).
- **Interpretation of WQI**: <50 Excellent; 50–100 Good; 100–200 Poor; 200–300 Very Poor; >300 Unsuitable.
- **pH ideal = 7**; for all other parameters, **ideal value = 0**. Do not mix this up!
- **Hardness formula**: TH = **2.5[Ca²⁺] + 4.1[Mg²⁺]** (both ions in mg/L, result as CaCO₃).
- **Total coliforms in drinking water = 0 MPN/100 mL** (absolute zero tolerance).
- **Class D fisheries DO ≥ 4 mg/L** is the most critical aquatic life threshold (below this → fish kill).
- **Effluent discharge governed by CPCB Schedule VI** of EP Rules 1986; industry-specific tables for **textile, distillery, tannery, paper, thermal power**.
- **EPA 1986** is the umbrella legal authority; CPCB/SPCBs are implementing agencies.
- **ΔT limit for thermal power cooling water** ≤ 5 °C at mixing zone boundary.
- **BOD₅ test conditions**: 5 days, **20 °C**, dark, seeded if needed.
- **COD/BOD ratio** for municipal sewage ≈ **2 to 2.5**; for industrial effluents can exceed **5**.
- **EIA stage at which standards are used**: Scoping (define baseline) → Impact Prediction (modelling) → Mitigation (treatment design) → Monitoring (compliance verification).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Modelling** in the context of EIA is the systematic use of mathematical, physical, or logical representations of environmental systems to simulate, predict, and evaluate the probable impacts of a proposed project, policy, or activity on the surrounding physical, chemical, biological, and socio-economic environment.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for EIA, modelling is the third stage of the EIA process (after Screening and Scoping), where qualitative observations are converted into **quantitative predictions** using established scientific equations, numerical simulations, or computer-based tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"A model is a simplified representation of a real system, constructed to understand, predict, and manage the behaviour of that system under various natural and anthropogenic conditions."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Map" Intuition

Imagine you are a civil engineer deciding where to place the **air intake vent** of a new building near a busy highway. Before construction, you need to know: *"Where will the polluted air from vehicles actually go?"*

You obviously cannot wait 5 years, build the building, and observe the pollution. Instead, you create a **mathematical model** — like the Gaussian Plume Model — that behaves like an invisible "smoke trail calculator." It tells you, *digitally*, how CO, PM₂.₅, and NO₂ will spread based on:
- Wind speed
- Wind direction
- Stack height
- Emission rate

**That calculator = the Model.** It is a "**simulation shortcut**" of reality.

### 1.3 Why Modelling is Indispensable in EIA

| Purpose | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Prediction** | Forecast future impacts before they occur |
| **Quantification** | Convert "high pollution" into "150 µg/m³" |
| **Comparison** | Compare alternatives (e.g., coal vs. gas plant) |
| **Mitigation Design** | Decide optimal stack height, buffer zone |
| **Compliance** | Prove to regulators that CPCB/SPCB norms are met |
| **Decision Support** | Provide scientific basis for public hearings |

### 1.4 Classification of Models — The Master Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Classification Framework:**

```
ENVIRONMENTAL MODELS
├── 1. Physical (Scale) Models
├── 2. Mathematical (Analytical) Models
├── 3. Numerical (Computational) Models
├── 4. Statistical (Empirical) Models
└── 5. Conceptual / Graphical Models
```

Each is further sub-classified by their internal behaviour:

| Basis of Classification | Types | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **By Randomness** | Deterministic vs. Stochastic | No randomness vs. includes probability |
| **By Time** | Static vs. Dynamic | No time vs. time-dependent |
| **By Space** | Lumped vs. Distributed | Single value vs. spatial variation |
| **By Linearity** | Linear vs. Non-Linear | Direct proportionality vs. complex |
| **By Process** | Mechanistic vs. Empirical | Based on physics vs. based on data |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Modelling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of a Good Model:**
> - **Accuracy:** Deviation < **5%** is desirable
> - **Precision:** Repeatability of output
> - **Calibration R²:** Must be > **0.85** for air models
> - **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error):** Lower is better
> - **Mass Balance Closure Error:** Must be **< 1%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Environmental Model — The 5 Building Blocks

Every environmental model — whether simple or complex — consists of these mandatory components:

1. **Inputs (Forcing Functions):** External drivers like emission rate $Q$, wind speed $u$, temperature, flow rate $Q_w$.
2. **State Variables:** Quantities the model tracks — concentration $C$, dissolved oxygen $DO$, temperature $T$.
3. **Parameters:** Coefficients that characterise the system — dispersion coefficient $\sigma$, reaction rate $k$.
4. **Mathematical Framework:** The governing equation (PDE, ODE, or algebraic).
5. **Outputs (Predictions):** Spatial/temporal distribution of pollutants.

### 2.2 Why and How of Each Model Type

**A. Physical (Scale) Models**
- *Why:* Validate mathematical predictions visually.
- *How:* Build a 1:10 or 1:50 scale replica (e.g., river basin) and observe tracer dye behaviour.
- *Limitation:* Expensive, time-consuming, subject to scaling laws errors.

**B. Mathematical Models**
- *Why:* Fast, repeatable, and parameter-efficient.
- *How:* Apply conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) → derive governing equations → solve analytically.

**C. Numerical/Computational Models**
- *Why:* Handle complex geometries and non-linearities.
- *How:* Discretise space/time (FDM, FEM, FVM) and solve on computers (e.g., AERMOD, MIKE, MODFLOW).

**D. Statistical Models**
- *Why:* When physical understanding is weak but data is abundant.
- *How:* Regress observed data → develop empirical equations.

### 2.3 The Big Four Models in EIA — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Print This & Memorise for KTU Exams:**

| Model Name | Domain | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian Plume** | Air Dispersion | See Section 3.1 | $Q, u, H, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ | Stack emissions |
| **Box Model** | Urban Airshed | $C = \dfrac{Q}{u \cdot A \cdot h}$ | $Q, u, A, h$ | City-wide pollution |
| **Streeter-Phelps** | River Water Quality | See Section 3.3 | $D_0, L_0, k_1, k_2, u, x$ | BOD-DO sag curve |
| **ISO 9613-2** | Industrial Noise | $L_p = L_W - 20\log(r) - 11 - \alpha r$ | $L_W, r, \alpha$ | Plant noise propagation |

### 2.4 The Step-by-Step Modelling Procedure (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 7-Stage EIA Modelling Protocol (used by CPCB, MOEFCC):**

```
Stage 1 → Conceptual Model      (What are the key processes?)
Stage 2 → Data Collection       (Baseline + Source data)
Stage 3 → Mathematical Set-up   (Governing equation selection)
Stage 4 → Parameter Estimation  (k, σ, u values)
Stage 5 → Calibration          (Tune to match observations)
Stage 6 → Validation            (Test with independent dataset)
Stage 7 → Scenario Simulation   (Predict future impacts)
```

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Gaussian Plume used to design stack height (MPL formula).
- **Sewage Discharge:** Streeter-Phelps sets the minimum distance from water intake.
- **Highways:** Noise models decide sound barrier specifications.
- **Industries:** Box models estimate worst-case ground-level concentrations.
- **Mining:** Water quality models predict acid mine drainage downstream.

### 2.6 Criteria for Model Selection in EIA

| Criterion | Question to Ask |
|---|---|
| **Spatial Scale** | Local (m) or Regional (km)? |
| **Temporal Scale** | Hours, days, years? |
| **Data Availability** | Are input parameters measurable? |
| **Regulatory Acceptance** | Is it approved by CPCB/SPCB? |
| **Computational Cost** | Desktop or workstation? |
| **Uncertainty** | Are stochastic methods required? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule:** *"The best model is the simplest one that adequately answers the regulatory question."* — EPA Modelling Guideline
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — The Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model

**Starting Principle:** Conservation of mass applied to a continuous point source emitting pollutant at rate $Q$ (g/s) into a steady wind of speed $u$ (m/s) from an effective stack height $H$ (m).

#### Step 1: Establish the Concentration Distribution Assumption

Assume the pollutant disperses in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions following a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** with standard deviations $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.

#### Step 2: Write the 3-D Concentration Distribution

$$
C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

*Logic:* The numerator $Q/u$ gives the mass per unit length of plume; the denominator $(2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ normalises the bivariate Gaussian such that integration over all $y,z$ recovers the total mass.

#### Step 3: Add Ground Reflection (Image Source)

The ground is a reflecting boundary. To honour the no-flux condition at $z = 0$, add an **image source** at $z = -H$.

$$
C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[ \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) \right]
$$

#### Step 4: Special Case — Ground-Level Concentration (z = 0)

Setting $z = 0$, the two exponentials become equal:

$$
C(x, y, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

#### Step 5: Special Case — Centreline Concentration (y = 0)

$$
C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

#### Step 6: Maximum Ground-Level Concentration Distance

Use Pasquill-Gifford stability curves where $\sigma_y \propto x^{0.9}$ and $\sigma_z \propto x^{0.8}$ approximately. Differentiating the centreline equation with respect to $x$ and setting $dC/dx = 0$ gives the maximum concentration location, typically:

$$
x_{\max} \approx \frac{H \cdot \sigma_z(x)}{\text{some function of }H}
$$

In practice, stability-class lookup tables are used.

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Box Model for Urban Airshed

#### Step 1: Control Volume Definition

Consider a city as a rectangular box of:
- Base area $A$ (m²)
- Mixing height $h$ (m)
- Wind speed $u$ (m/s) advecting pollutant along $x$.

#### Step 2: Mass Balance (Steady State)

$$
\text{In} + \text{Source} = \text{Out} + \text{Sink}
$$

$$
u \cdot A \cdot h \cdot C_b + Q = u \cdot A \cdot h \cdot C + 0
$$

where $C_b$ = background concentration entering the box, $C$ = concentration leaving.

#### Step 3: Solve for Concentration

$$
C = C_b + \frac{Q}{u \cdot A \cdot h}
$$

*Logic:* $Q$ is the emission rate (g/s); $uAh$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s); ratio gives added concentration (g/m³).

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Streeter-Phelps BOD-DO Model

#### Step 1: System Definition

A river receives a wastewater discharge with initial BOD $= L_0$ and initial oxygen deficit $= D_0 = (DO_{sat} - DO_0)$.

#### Step 2: Governing Differential Equations

Two simultaneous processes occur:

**Deoxygenation (BOD decay by bacteria):**
$$
\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 \cdot L \quad \Rightarrow \quad L(x) = L_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{k_1 x}{u}\right)
$$

**Reaeration (oxygen absorption from atmosphere):**
$$
\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D
$$

where $k_1$ = deoxygenation rate (day⁻¹), $k_2$ = reaeration rate (day⁻¹).

#### Step 3: Solve the Oxygen Deficit Equation

Substituting $L(x)$ and solving with initial condition $D(0) = D_0$:

$$
D(x) = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left[ \exp\left(-\frac{k_1 x}{u}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{k_2 x}{u}\right) \right] + D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{k_2 x}{u}\right)
$$

*Logic:* The deficit is a balance between ongoing deoxygenation (first term) and initial deficit being reaerated (second term).

#### Step 4: Critical Point (Maximum Deficit)

Setting $dD/dx = 0$ yields the critical distance $x_c$:

$$
x_c = \frac{u}{k_2 - k_1} \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{k_2}{k_1} \left( 1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} \right) \right]
$$

#### Step 5: Critical Deficit Magnitude

$$
D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{k_1 x_c}{u}\right)
$$

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — ISO 9613-2 Noise Propagation Model

#### Step 1: Point Source Sound Power

A point source with sound power level $L_W$ (dB) radiates equally in all directions.

#### Step 2: Geometric Divergence (Spherical Spreading)

At distance $r$ (m), the sound pressure level reduces by $20 \log_{10}(r)$:

$$
L_p(r) = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11
$$

The **11 dB** constant comes from $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99$ for spherical spreading into free field.

#### Step 3: Atmospheric Absorption

Air absorbs sound, with coefficient $\alpha$ (dB/km):

$$
L_p(r) = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11 - \alpha \cdot r
$$

#### Step 4: Add Ground and Barrier Effects (Engineering Modifications)

For typical EIA, additional attenuation terms $A_{gr}$ (ground) and $A_{bar}$ (barrier) are subtracted.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Gaussian Plume

**Problem:** A stack emits SO₂ at $Q = 100$ g/s. Wind speed $u = 5$ m/s. Effective stack height $H = 50$ m. At a downwind distance $x = 1000$ m, the dispersion parameters are $\sigma_y = 35$ m and $\sigma_z = 25$ m. Find the centreline ground-level concentration.

**Solution:**

Using $C(x, 0, 0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$

Step 1: Compute $\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z$:

$$
\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 35 \cdot 25 = \pi \cdot 4375 \approx 13744.47 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Step 2: Compute exponent:

$$
-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = -\frac{(50)^2}{2 \cdot (25)^2} = -\frac{2500}{1250} = -2
$$

Step 3: Evaluate exponential:

$$
\exp(-2) \approx 0.1353
$$

Step 4: Final concentration:

$$
C = \frac{100}{13744.47} \cdot 0.1353 \approx 9.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3 = 984 \, \mu g/m^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** $1 \text{ g/m}^3 = 10^6 \, \mu g/m^3 = 10^3 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Always convert before comparing with CPCB standards (typically in $\mu g/m^3$).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define environmental modelling. List any four types of models used in EIA.**

**Model Answer:**

Environmental modelling is the use of mathematical, physical, or logical representations to simulate real environmental systems and predict their behaviour under various scenarios. It is a critical step in EIA where qualitative impacts are quantified.

**Four types of models:**

1. **Physical (Scale) Models** — Reduced-size replicas (e.g., hydraulic river model).
2. **Mathematical Models** — Equation-based (e.g., Gaussian Plume, Streeter-Phelps).
3. **Numerical/Computational Models** — Discretised PDE solvers (e.g., AERMOD, CFD).
4. **Statistical Models** — Data-driven empirical relationships (e.g., regression).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between deterministic and stochastic models with suitable EIA examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Deterministic Model | Stochastic Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Fixed output for a given input | Output varies randomly |
| **Randomness** | None | Includes probabilistic components |
| **Equations** | ODE/PDE with no random terms | Includes random variables & distributions |
| **EIA Example** | Gaussian Plume, Streeter-Phelps | Monte Carlo Risk Assessment, Probabilistic flood model |
| **Use Case** | When system is well-understood | When uncertainties are significant |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Gaussian Plume Model** for atmospheric dispersion of pollutants. State its assumptions and derive the centreline ground-level concentration formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal-based thermal power plant emits SO₂ at $Q = 200$ g/s from a stack with effective height $H = 80$ m. Wind speed $u = 4$ m/s. At downwind distance $x = 1500$ m, dispersion parameters are $\sigma_y = 45$ m and $\sigma_z = 30$ m. Calculate the centreline ground-level concentration. Comment on compliance with NAAQS (limit = 80 µg/m³). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a):**

The **Gaussian Plume Model** is the most widely used mathematical model for predicting the dispersion of non-reactive pollutants from a continuous elevated point source. It is the regulatory backbone of EIA for thermal power plants, cement industries, and refineries.

**Key Assumptions:**

1. Steady-state conditions (constant emission rate, wind speed, direction).
2. Pollutant is conservative (no chemical reaction or deposition).
3. Plume spreads in crosswind and vertical directions following **Gaussian (Normal) distribution**.
4. Wind speed is uniform with height (no wind shear).
5. The ground acts as a **perfect reflector** of the pollutant.
6. Top of the mixing layer does not affect dispersion.

**Derivation (Summary):**

Apply mass conservation to a steady plume. The mass flux through any downwind cross-section equals the emission rate $Q$.

$$
Q = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} u \cdot C(x,y,z) \, dy \, dz
$$

Substituting the Gaussian form $C(x,y,z) \propto \exp(-y^2/2\sigma_y^2) \cdot \exp(-(z-H)^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ and using the property that the total mass under a Gaussian curve is $\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}$, the final equation emerges:

$$
C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[ \exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) \right]
$$

At **centreline ($y=0$) and ground level ($z=0$)**, the two exponentials become equal:

$$
C_{GLC} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

---

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Plug in the given values:

$$
\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z = \pi \cdot 4 \cdot 45 \cdot 30 = \pi \cdot 5400 \approx 16964.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponential term:

$$
-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = -\frac{(80)^2}{2 \cdot (30)^2} = -\frac{6400}{1800} = -3.5556
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\exp(-3.5556) \approx 0.02857
$$

**Step 4:** Final concentration:

$$
C = \frac{200}{16964.6} \cdot 0.02857 \approx 3.37 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Convert to µg/m³:**

$$
C = 3.37 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 10^6 = 337 \, \mu g/m^3
$$

**Compliance Check:**
- Calculated = **337 µg/m³**
- NAAQS Limit = **80 µg/m³**
- Status: **Non-Compliant** ❌ (exceeds by ~4.2×)

**Conclusion:** Mitigation required — either increase stack height or install Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD) system.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **Streeter-Phelps model** for dissolved oxygen in a river. Derive the oxygen deficit equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A domestic sewage discharge has initial BOD $L_0 = 50$ mg/L, initial DO deficit $D_0 = 2$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.25$ /day, $k_2 = 0.40$ /day, and stream velocity $u = 0.5$ m/s. Calculate the **critical deficit** and the **distance at which it occurs**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a):**

The **Streeter-Phelps Model (1925)** is the foundational water quality model used in EIA for rivers receiving organic wastewater discharge. It predicts the **DO sag curve** downstream of a pollutant source.

**System Setup:** A river at saturation DO receives wastewater with BOD $= L_0$ and DO deficit $D_0$.

**Two Competing Processes:**

1. **Deoxygenation:** Microbial oxidation of organic matter consumes oxygen.

$$
\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L \implies L(x) = L_0 e^{-k_1 x/u}
$$

2. **Reaeration:** Oxygen absorption from the atmosphere replenishes DO.

$$
\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D
$$

Substituting $L(x)$ and solving the linear ODE:

$$
D(x) = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left[e^{-k_1 x/u} - e^{-k_2 x/u}\right] + D_0 \, e^{-k_2 x/u}
$$

**Critical Point:** Maximum deficit occurs when $dD/dx = 0$.

$$
x_c = \frac{u}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - \frac{D_0(k_2-k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]
$$

---

**Solution (b):**

**Given:**
$L_0 = 50$ mg/L, $D_0 = 2$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.25$ /day, $k_2 = 0.40$ /day, $u = 0.5$ m/s.

**Step 1: Compute $k_2 - k_1$:**

$$
k_2 - k_1 = 0.40 - 0.25 = 0.15 \text{ /day}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the bracketed term:**

$$
1 - \frac{D_0(k_2-k_1)}{k_1 L_0} = 1 - \frac{2 \times 0.15}{0.25 \times 50} = 1 - \frac{0.30}{12.5} = 1 - 0.024 = 0.976
$$

**Step 3: Compute critical distance:**

$$
x_c = \frac{0.5}{0.15} \ln\left[\frac{0.40}{0.25} \times 0.976\right] = 3.333 \times \ln(1.6 \times 0.976)
$$

$$
= 3.333 \times \ln(1.5616) = 3.333 \times 0.4459 = 1.486 \text{ m/day}
$$

Converting to days → multiply by $u = 0.5$ m/s:

$$
x_c = 1.486 \text{ day} \quad (\text{or } 1.486 \times 86400 \times 0.5 \approx 64.2 \text{ km if interpreting $x_c$ as time})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimension Note:** The formula $x_c$ yields **time in days**. To get distance in metres, multiply by $u$ (m/day) or convert units consistently.

**Step 4: Compute critical deficit:**

First, $e^{-k_1 x_c / u} = e^{-0.25 \times 1.486 / 0.5} = e^{-0.743} \approx 0.4758$

$$
D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \cdot e^{-k_1 x_c / u} = \frac{0.25 \times 50}{0.40} \cdot 0.4758
$$

$$
D_c = 31.25 \cdot 0.4758 \approx 14.87 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Interpretation:** The DO drops to $DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.0 - 14.87 = -5.87$ mg/L. Since negative DO is impossible, the river is **fully anaerobic** at this point — severe pollution, requiring aeration or wastewater pre-treatment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting the ground reflection term** in Gaussian Plume (image source) — costs **2 marks** out of 7.
> - **Unit errors:** Mixing g/m³ with µg/m³ in air quality questions. Always state conversions explicitly.
> - **Confusing $k_1$ and $k_2$** in Streeter-Phelps: $k_1$ = deoxygenation, $k_2$ = reaeration. $k_2 > k_1$ for natural recovery.
> - **Skipping assumptions** before derivation — the KTU Board deducts **1–2 marks** if assumptions of the model are not listed.
> - **Not commenting on compliance** with NAAQS/CPCB standards — the examiner expects a regulatory interpretation, not just a numerical answer.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Modelling** = simplified mathematical/physical representation of an environmental system.
- ✅ **5 Types:** Physical, Mathematical, Numerical, Statistical, Conceptual.
- ✅ **By Behaviour:** Deterministic vs. Stochastic; Static vs. Dynamic; Lumped vs. Distributed.
- ✅ **Gaussian Plume** uses Normal distribution in $y$ and $z$ with **ground reflection (image source)**.
- ✅ **Centreline GLC formula:** $C = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Box Model:** $C = C_b + \dfrac{Q}{u A h}$ — simple but limited to well-mixed urban airsheds.
- ✅ **Streeter-Phelps** has **two rate constants:** $k_1$ (deoxygenation) and $k_2$ (reaeration), with $k_2 > k_1$.
- ✅ **Oxygen deficit:** $D(x) = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left[e^{-k_1 x/u} - e^{-k_2 x/u}\right] + D_0 e^{-k_2 x/u}$.
- ✅ **ISO 9613-2** is the global standard for industrial noise propagation in EIA.
- ✅ **7-Stage Protocol:** Conceptual → Data → Mathematical → Parameter → Calibration → Validation → Application.
- ✅ **Calibration R²** must exceed **0.85** for air quality models.
- ✅ **NAAQS for SO₂** (24-hr) = **80 µg/m³**; **PM₂.₅** = **60 µg/m³**; **PM₁₀** = **100 µg/m³**.
- ✅ **Mandatory KTU Keywords to memorise:** *Mass balance, conservation, dispersion coefficient, Gaussian, Pasquill-Gifford, BOD, DO deficit, reaeration, image source, calibration, validation*.
- ✅ **Always:** state **assumptions**, state **units**, **convert** correctly, and **comment on regulatory compliance**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Quality Impact by Projects (Highways)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Water Quality Impact by Projects (Highways) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Water Quality Impact** in the context of highway projects refers to the systematic identification, prediction, evaluation, and mitigation of adverse changes in the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of surface water and groundwater resources — both during the **construction phase** and the **operational phase** of a road/bridge project — caused by the generation and migration of point and non-point source pollutants. In **KTU 2024 Scheme EIA terminology**, this is studied as a sub-component of the **Hydrology and Water Environment** sectoral assessment under the **Screening → Scoping → Impact Prediction → Mitigation → Monitoring** framework prescribed by the **EIA Notification, 2006 (as amended 2020)** and the **MoEFCC Guidelines for Highway Projects**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"Water quality impact assessment of highway projects evaluates the alteration in the physico-chemical and biological parameters of the receiving water bodies due to construction-related sediment runoff, operational stormwater carrying vehicular pollutants, and alteration of natural drainage patterns, with the objective of prescribing engineering and biological mitigation measures to maintain receiving water standards as per CPCB / KSPCB / IS:2296 specifications."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road as a Moving Pollutant Factory" Imagine a highway as a **long, linear, moving industrial belt** stretching across the landscape. Every vehicle crossing it acts like a small factory chimney depositing microscopic amounts of soot (PM), oil droplets (hydrocarbons), metal filings (brake dust containing Cu, Pb, Zn), and salt spray (deicing compounds). When it rains, the road behaves like the **roof of a building**: instead of clean rainwater running off a tiled roof, a "dirty rooftop" of asphalt sheds a toxic soup into the nearest storm drain, stream, or river. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > - A highway is like a **slanted kitchen counter with crumbs and oil spills** — rain is the "wash water" that carries the grease (oils) and crumbs (sediment, metals) straight into your sink (the receiving stream). > - The **construction phase** is like **renovating your house** — dust (sediment), paint chips (chemicals), and debris (construction waste) fall everywhere before the proper drainage is built. > - The **operational phase** is like **daily cooking** — small but constant oil, salt, and food particles (pollutants) wash down the drain with every rinse (rainfall event). ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Source / Authority | |---|---|---| | Threshold Rainfall for Runoff Generation | **2.5 mm** | CPCB Stormwater Guidelines | | Total Suspended Solids (TSS) in Highway Runoff | **150 – 1500 mg/L** | USGS, 2000 | | Lead (Pb) in Highway Runoff | **0.01 – 0.5 mg/L** | NCHRP Report 565 | | Zinc (Zn) in Highway Runoff | **0.05 – 1.0 mg/L** | NCHRP Report 565 | | Oil & Grease (O&G) in Highway Runoff | **5 – 50 mg/L** | CPCB | | pH of Runoff | **6.0 – 8.5** (Class C surface water) | IS:2296 | | Chloride (from deicing) | **100 – 1000 mg/L** peak | NCHRP | | BOD of Construction Runoff | **20 – 200 mg/L** | CPCB | > [!TIP] > **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners love asking you to *justify the study area* or *list parameters monitored*. Memorize the **6 high-yield parameters** for highway runoff: **TSS, O&G, Pb, Zn, BOD, Chloride**. ### 1.4 Classification of Highway Water Impacts Highway water impacts are classified by KTU/EIA literature into **three distinct temporal-physical categories**: 1. **Construction Phase (Temporary, 1–3 years)** — Sediment from earthworks, cement wash, fuel spills, alteration of drainage paths, siltation of streams during bridge/pile work. 2. **Operational Phase (Chronic, 30–100 years)** — Routine vehicular non-point source pollution (oils, PAHs, heavy metals, microplastics from tire wear, deicing salts). 3. **Accidental Phase (Episodic, low-probability)** — Tanker spills, hazardous material accidents, catastrophic bridge failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Always structure your answer using this **3-phase matrix** — the examiner expects it because it aligns with **CO4: "Assess sectoral environmental impacts"** of PEPOT636. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Sources of Highway Water Pollution — The "POLLUTE" Mnemonic For high-yield exam recall, highway water pollutants can be remembered using the **POLLUTE** mnemonic: - **P** – Petroleum hydrocarbons (oil, grease, PAHs) - **O** – Oils from engines and gearboxes - **L** – Lead (legacy), Lithium, and other heavy metals - **L** – Litter and Light-duty vehicle emissions - **U** – Used tire wear (microplastics, Zn, Cd) - **T** – Total Suspended Solids (TSS) from road surface wear - **E** – Engineering materials (cement, epoxy, anti-strip agents) and Excess salts ### 2.2 Detailed Mechanism of Pollutant Generation **Step 1 — Atmospheric Deposition (Pre-Construction Background Loading)** - Settled dust and aerosols contribute baseline heavy metal loads. Metals accumulate on the road surface during dry weather. **Step 2 — Surface Accumulation (Dry Days, Antecedent Period)** - Days without rain allow pollutant build-up. A common model: build-up follows an asymptotic curve, often expressed as: $$P_t = P_{max} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$ where $P_t$ = pollutant mass at time $t$ (kg/ha), $P_{max}$ = maximum build-up, $k$ = build-up rate constant (day⁻¹), $t$ = antecedent dry days. **Step 3 — Wash-off During Rainfall (The Critical Step)** - The first flush (typically first 20–30% of runoff volume) carries **70–80%** of the pollutant load. Wash-off is commonly modeled as: $$L_w = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_w \cdot R}$$ where $L_w$ = remaining load, $L_0$ = initial available load, $k_w$ = wash-off coefficient, $R$ = cumulative runoff depth (mm). **Step 4 — Transport via Drainage Network** - Pollutants travel via **roadside drains, culverts, grassed swales, and outfalls** into the receiving water body. **Step 5 — In-stream Fate Processes** - Settling of TSS, adsorption of metals onto sediments, photolysis of PAHs, biodegradation of hydrocarbons, dilution. ### 2.3 The High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Board Favorites) > [!NOTE] > **Table 2.1 — Essential Formulas for Highway Water Quality Assessment** | # | Formula | Description | Standard Use | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | **Rational Method** for peak runoff | Storm drain design | m³/s | | 2 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | SCS Potential Maximum Retention | Curve Number method | mm | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$ | SCS Runoff Depth | Event runoff volume | mm | | 4 | $L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$ | **Simple Method** for annual pollutant load | Highway runoff loading | kg/yr | | 5 | $EMC = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i}$ | Event Mean Concentration | Statistical water quality | mg/L | | 6 | $C_f = C_0 \cdot \exp\left(-k \cdot \dfrac{L}{U}\right)$ | Streeter-Phelps dissolved oxygen sag | Receiving water DO modeling | mg/L | | 7 | $C_{mix} = \dfrac{C_1 Q_1 + C_2 Q_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$ | Mixing zone concentration | Outfall discharge | mg/L | | 8 | $L_p = C \cdot Q \cdot T$ | Pollutant load from concentration × flow × time | Mass balance check | kg | | 9 | $A_{buffer} = 30 \cdot S + 0.5$ | Required buffer width from road to stream | IRC:SP:21 guideline | m | | 10 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's Equation for open channel | Drainage design | m/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition Box — Terms You Must Define for Full Marks:** > - **C (Rational)** = runoff coefficient (dimensionless), typically **0.7–0.95 for asphalt** and **0.1–0.3 for grassed medians**. > - **CN (Curve Number)** = SCS dimensionless parameter (0–100). For impervious asphalt: **CN = 98**. Forested area: **CN = 55–70**. > - **EMC** is the *flow-weighted average concentration* over an entire storm event.
