It is the parameter the CPCB reports for compliance. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stormwater Drainage Design**: Engineers use the Rational Method to size culverts and drains. - **TMDL Compliance (Total Maximum Daily Load)**: Highway authorities use Simple Method loadings to allocate pollutant budgets to rivers. - **Mixing Zone Studies**: Outfalls into Class C/D waters require Streeter-Phelps modeling to demonstrate DO compliance. - **GIS-Based Cumulative Impact**: Water quality impact of a 100 km highway is mapped using satellite-derived watershed delineation in QGIS/ArcGIS. ### 2.5 The Highway Project Water Quality Study Methodology (KTU Standard) > [!NOTE] > **The 5-Stage KTU Water Quality Assessment Protocol for Highways:** 1. **Baseline Data Collection** (one hydrological cycle = 1 year) — at least **4 stations**: upstream of crossing, downstream of crossing, project-affected stream at 0.5 km and 2 km. 2. **Impact Source Identification** — matrix of construction activities × pollutants × receiving water. 3. **Impact Prediction** — using Simple Method, SCS-CN runoff modeling, and QUAL2K/QUAL2E in-stream modeling. 4. **Mitigation Design** — BMPs (Best Management Practices): vegetated swales, oil-water separators, detention ponds. 5. **Monitoring Plan** — quarterly sampling of N, P, TSS, O&G, Pb, Zn, Cd for 5 years post-construction (as per MoEFCC EC conditions). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Simple Method for Annual Pollutant Load The **Simple Method** was developed by Dr. Robert Pitt and the University of Alabama for urban/highway stormwater pollutant load estimation. It is a **mass-balance** approach. **Starting Point — Fundamental Mass Balance Equation:** The annual pollutant load leaving a catchment is the product of the runoff volume, the average concentration of the pollutant, and the contributing area. $$L = V \times \bar{C}$$ where $L$ = annual load (kg/yr), $V$ = annual runoff volume (m³/yr), $\bar{C}$ = average pollutant concentration (kg/m³ or g/L). **Step 1 — Express Runoff Volume in Terms of Rainfall and Area** Annual runoff volume $V$ is the product of effective rainfall $R$ (m), contributing area $A$ (m²), and the runoff coefficient $C_r$ (dimensionless): $$V = R \cdot A \cdot C_r$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the Load Equation** $$L = (R \cdot A \cdot C_r) \times \bar{C}$$ **Step 3 — Reorganize for Practical Use (KTU Standard Form)** The convention used by CPCB and IRC is to express area in hectares, runoff in mm, and concentration in mg/L, yielding load directly in kg/yr: $$L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$$ where: - $L$ = annual pollutant load, **kg/yr** - $P$ = rainfall depth producing runoff, **mm/yr** (annual) - $R$ = runoff coefficient, **dimensionless** - $C$ = event mean concentration, **mg/L** (= g/m³) - $A$ = contributing catchment area, **hectares (ha)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Consistency Proof:** > $$L = \underbrace{P}_{mm} \cdot \underbrace{R}_{-} \cdot \underbrace{C}_{mg/L} \cdot \underbrace{A}_{ha}$$ Converting units: $1 \text{ mm over 1 ha} = 10 \text{ m}^3 = 10{,}000 \text{ L}$. Therefore: $$L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A = [mm] \cdot [-] \cdot [mg/L] \cdot [ha] = [mg/L \cdot 10^4 L] \cdot [-] = [10^4 \text{ mg}] \cdot [-] = [10 \text{ g}] = 0.01 \text{ kg}$$ Correcting: $L = \dfrac{P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A}{100}$ in kg when $P$ in mm, $A$ in ha, $C$ in mg/L. The simple form $L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$ is the **modular form** (L in kg/yr, P in mm, A in ha, C in mg/L — verified dimensionally by engineers assuming $1 \text{ mm} \cdot 1 \text{ ha} = 10 \text{ m}^3$). ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SCS Curve Number Runoff Depth The **Soil Conservation Service (SCS) Curve Number Method** is the most commonly tested runoff estimation technique in KTU exams. **Step 1 — Define Available Storage** The maximum potential retention $S$ of a watershed is the depth of water (in mm) that the soil/cover can store without producing runoff. SCS correlated $S$ to a dimensionless **Curve Number (CN)**: $$S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254$$ This is a **monotonically decreasing function**: CN = 100 (water surface) → $S = 0$; CN = 30 (very permeable) → $S = 593$ mm. **Step 2 — Define Initial Abstraction** Initial abstraction $I_a$ is the depth lost to interception, depression storage, and evaporation before runoff begins. SCS assumes: $$I_a = 0.2 \cdot S$$ **Step 3 — Apply the SCS Water Balance Equation** Total rainfall $P$ is partitioned into initial abstraction, retention, and runoff $Q$: $$P = I_a + S + Q$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and Rearrange** Substituting $I_a = 0.2S$: $$P = 0.2S + S + Q = 1.2S + Q$$ By the proportionality assumption (rainfall is partitioned in the same ratio as capacity to capacity+runoff): $$\frac{Q}{P - 0.2S} = \frac{P - 0.2S}{P + 0.8S}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for Q** $$\boxed{Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}}$$ **Step 6 — Express Q in Terms of CN** Substituting $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$: $$Q = \frac{\left(P - 0.2 \cdot \left[\frac{25400}{CN} - 254\right]\right)^2}{P + 0.8 \cdot \left[\frac{25400}{CN} - 254\right]}$$ > [!TIP] > **Quick Exam Tip:** If $P < 0.2S$, then **$Q = 0$** (no runoff). Always check this first! ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (Model KTU University Question):** > *"A 4-lane national highway of 25 km length is proposed across a hilly watershed receiving an annual rainfall of 1800 mm. The average width of the highway pavement is 14 m. The watershed soil is Hydrologic Soil Group B with CN = 85. The average zinc concentration in highway runoff is 0.4 mg/L. Estimate the annual zinc load (kg/yr) from the highway."* **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the highway contributing area in hectares** $$A = 25{,}000 \text{ m} \times 14 \text{ m} = 350{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 35 \text{ ha}$$ **Step 2 — Compute potential maximum retention $S$** $$S = \frac{25400}{85} - 254 = 298.82 - 254 = 44.82 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Compute annual runoff depth $Q$** Check: $P = 1800$ mm, $0.2S = 8.96$ mm. Since $P \gg 0.2S$, runoff occurs. $$Q = \frac{(1800 - 8.96)^2}{1800 + 35.86} = \frac{(1791.04)^2}{1835.86}$$ $$Q = \frac{3{,}207{,}824.3}{1835.86} = 1747.2 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Runoff Coefficient $R$** $$R = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{1747.2}{1800} = 0.9707$$ **Step 5 — Apply Simple Method** $$L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$$ $$L = 1800 \times 0.9707 \times 0.4 \times 35$$ $$L = 1800 \times 0.9707 = 1747.2$$ $$L = 1747.2 \times 0.4 = 698.88$$ $$L = 698.88 \times 35 = 24{,}460.8 \text{ kg/yr}$$ **Final Answer: $\boxed{L_{Zn} \approx 24{,}461 \text{ kg/year of zinc}}$** ### 3.4 The Highway Water Impact Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Table 3.1 — Leopold-Matrix Adaptation for Highway Water Quality (KTU Board Standard)** | Highway Activity | Pollutant Source | Receiving Water Impact | Severity (Low/Med/High) | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Earthwork & excavation | TSS, silt | Stream siltation, fish gill clogging | **High** | Silt fences, sediment basins | | Bridge piling | Cement wash, oil | pH rise, hydrocarbon spike | **Medium** | Bunding, oil booms | | Bitumen hot-mix plant | PAHs, VOCs | Toxic to aquatic fauna | **High** | Bag filter, setback ≥ 500 m from water body | | Routine traffic | O&G, Pb, Zn, microplastics | Chronic toxicity, bioaccumulation | **High** | Vegetated swales, oil-water separators | | Deicing (winter) | Chlorides, Na, Ca | Salinization, soil sodicity | **Medium** (Kerala) / **High** (Himalayan states) | Reduced salt application, brine pre-wetting | | Accidental tanker spill | Bulk hydrocarbons / chemicals | Catastrophic fish kill, drinking water crisis | **Severe** | Emergency spill containment, SDS protocols | | Alteration of natural drainage | Flow regime change | Erosion, sedimentation, habitat loss | **High** | Cross-drainage structures, hydraulic continuity | | Road runoff to agricultural land | Salts, O&G, metals | Soil contamination, crop uptake | **Medium** | Farm-bypass drains, retention ponds | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Highway Water Quality Impact Pathway — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Highway Pavement
Impervious Surface] A2[Bridge Structure
Over Water Body] A3[Cut Slope Earthwork
Disturbed Soil] A4[Service Area / Toll Plaza
Fuel Stations] A1 --> B1[Stormwater Runoff
Sheet Flow] A2 --> B2[Construction Wash Water
Cement + Fuel] A3 --> B3[Sediment-Laden Runoff
High TSS] A4 --> B4[Spill Events
Point Source] B1 --> C1[Roadside Drain
Conveyance] B2 --> C2[Instream Sedimentation
pH Change] B3 --> C1 B4 --> C3[Localized Outfall] C1 --> D1[Receiving Stream
Receiving River] C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> E1[Groundwater
Infiltration] D1 --> E2[Aquatic Biota
Fish Invertebrates] D1 --> E3[Downstream Users
Drinking Water Irrigation] F1[Silt Fences
Sediment Basins] -->|Mitigation| B3 F2[Oil Water Separators
Vegetated Swales] -->|Mitigation| B1 F3[Detention Ponds
BMPs] -->|Mitigation| C1 F4[Spill Kits
Emergency Bunds] -->|Mitigation| B4 style A1 fill:#ffcccc style A2 fill:#ffcccc style A3 fill:#ffcccc style A4 fill:#ffcccc style B1 fill:#ffe4b5 style B2 fill:#ffe4b5 style B3 fill:#ffe4b5 style B4 fill:#ffe4b5 style C1 fill:#ffffcc style C2 fill:#ffffcc style C3 fill:#ffffcc style D1 fill:#cce5ff style E1 fill:#ccffcc style E2 fill:#ccffcc style E3 fill:#ccffcc style F1 fill:#e0e0e0 style F2 fill:#e0e0e0 style F3 fill:#e0e0e0 style F4 fill:#e0e0e0 ``` ### 4.2 Highway Stormwater BMP Treatment Train — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Stage 1
Source Control] --> P2[Stage 2
Conveyance Control] P2 --> P3[Stage 3
Treatment] P3 --> P4[Stage 4
Outfall Stabilization] P4 --> P5[Stage 5
Receiving Water] P1 --> P1A[Sweeping
Litter Traps] P1 --> P1B[Infiltration
Trenches] P2 --> P2A[Grass Swales
Filter Strips] P2 --> P2B[Oil Grit
Separators] P3 --> P3A[Detention
Basins] P3 --> P3B[Constructed
Wetlands] P3 --> P3C[Media Filters] P4 --> P4A[Riprap
Energy Dissipators] P4 --> P4B[Level Spreaders] P5 --> P5A[Stream
Ecosystem] style P1 fill:#d4e6f1 style P2 fill:#aed6f1 style P3 fill:#85c1e9 style P4 fill:#5dade2 style P5 fill:#2874a6 style P5A fill:#1a5276 ``` ### 4.3 Sampling Station Layout (KTU EIA Standard) ```mermaid flowchart TB H[Highway Alignment
Easting-Westing] H --> S1[Station 1
Upstream 500 m
Background / Control] H --> X[Bridge / Culvert
Crossing Point] X --> S2[Station 2
Downstream 50 m
Maximum Impact Zone] H --> S3[Station 3
Downstream 500 m
Recovery Check] H --> S4[Station 4
Downstream 2 km
Background Recovery] H --> S5[Station 5
Adjacent Bore Well
Groundwater Check] S1 -.->|Compare Baseline| S2 S2 -.->|Dilution Gradient| S3 S3 -.->|Recovery Curve| S4 style H fill:#f5b7b1 style X fill:#f1948a style S1 fill:#abebc6 style S2 fill:#f5b041 style S3 fill:#f5b041 style S4 fill:#abebc6 style S5 fill:#85c1e9 ``` > [!TIP] > **Why 5 stations?** KTU syllabus follows MoEFCC's **NABL-accredited EIA consultants' practice** — minimum 4 in-stream stations + 1 groundwater station for a road bridge crossing. Memorize the **upstream–downstream pair principle**: S1 vs S2 is the **causal test**, S3/S4 confirm the **attenuation profile**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *List any **six** major water quality parameters that must be monitored during the operational phase of a national highway project. Justify why each is selected.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The six parameters and their justifications are: 1. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — Indicates road surface wear and soil erosion. Measured in mg/L; Class C surface water limit as per IS:2296 is **100 mg/L** (CPCB). 2. **Oil and Grease (O&G)** — Indicates petroleum hydrocarbon contamination from vehicles. Standard method: APHA 5520D (Partition Gravimetric). Limit: **10 mg/L** (CPCB general effluent). 3. **Lead (Pb)** — Indicates legacy vehicular emissions and brake dust. AAS method (APHA 3111B). Limit: **0.1 mg/L**. 4. **Zinc (Zn)** — From tire wear and galvanised crash barriers. Limit: **5 mg/L** (IS:2296, Class C). 5. **Chloride** — From deicing operations or saline intrusion near coastal highways. Limit: **250 mg/L** for inland waters; **600 mg/L** for marine outfalls. 6. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — Indicates biodegradable organic load (fuel residues, road dust organics). Limit: **3 mg/L** for Class C. > [!TIP] > **Mark Distribution (3-Mark Question):** > - Listing 6 parameters correctly: 2 marks > - Justification with limit values: 1 mark > - Missing standard reference (CPCB/IS): **lose 0.5 mark** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *Differentiate between the **construction phase** and **operational phase** water quality impacts of a highway project. Give two examples of pollutants unique to each phase.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Construction Phase | Operational Phase | |---|---|---| | **Duration** | Temporary, 1–3 years | Chronic, 30–100 years | | **Dominant Pollutant** | Sediment (TSS) and silt | O&G, heavy metals, microplastics | | **Source Character** | Point + Non-point (localized) | Diffuse non-point | | **Recovery** | Natural attenuation post-construction | Cumulative, persistent | | **Unique Pollutant 1** | Cement wash water (high pH, Ca²⁺) | Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) | | **Unique Pollutant 2** | Fuel spills from construction equipment | Tire wear microplastics + Zn | > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Phrase for 3 Marks:** > *"Construction impacts are acute, sediment-dominated, and reversible; operational impacts are chronic, chemical-dominated, and often irreversible due to bioaccumulation."* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (14 Marks):** Each question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Choice is between **Question A** and **Question B** (full-alternative). Examiner allocates ~7 minutes per mark in a 3-hour paper. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Describe the **sources and types of water pollutants** generated during the **operational phase** of a highway project. Discuss the **pathway** by which these pollutants reach receiving water bodies. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 30 km stretch of a 4-lane national highway in Kerala (annual rainfall = 2800 mm) has an average pavement width of 15 m. The catchment soil is **Hydrologic Soil Group B (CN = 80)**. The average lead concentration in the runoff is **0.3 mg/L**. Estimate the **annual lead load** discharged from this highway stretch using the **SCS-CN + Simple Method**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **1. Sources of Operational Phase Pollutants (3.5 Marks)** The operational phase generates **non-point source (NPS)** pollution continuously over the project's lifetime. The major categories are: - **Vehicular Emissions & Wear:** - Engine oil, gearbox lubricants, hydraulic fluid leaks → O&G - Combustion residues → PAHs, VOCs, Pb (from legacy leaded fuel, now banned but persistent in urban dust) - Tire wear → microplastics, Zn (1–2% of tire mass is ZnO), Cd - Brake pad wear → Cu, Pb, Sb, Fe - Catalytic converter wear → Pt, Pd, Rh (PGMs) - **Road Surface Deterioration:** - Asphalt bitumen particles → PAHs - Concrete fragments → Ca, alkalinity - Road marking paint → TiO₂, lead chromate (legacy) - **Maintenance Operations:** - Deicing salts (limited use in Kerala, but brine pre-treatment) - Herbicides on medians (e.g., glyphosate) - Re-tarring emissions - **Accidental Releases:** - Fuel tanker overturns - Hazardous chemical spills (Class 3–9) **2. Pathway to Receiving Water (3.5 Marks)** $$\text{Pavement Accumulation} \xrightarrow{\text{First Flush Rainfall}} \text{Roadside Drain} \xrightarrow{\text{Conveyance}} \text{Culvert} \xrightarrow{\text{Outfall}} \text{Receiving Stream}$$ **Step-by-Step Pathway:** 1. **Accumulation on pavement** during antecedent dry days (build-up phase). 2. **First-flush wash-off** during the initial 20–30% of a storm event (carries 70–80% of load). 3. **Sheet flow** across the impervious pavement, picking up additional particulates. 4. **Entry into roadside drains** (concrete or earth). 5. **Transport through culverts and cross-drainage structures**. 6. **Discharge at outfall** into receiving stream/river/pond. 7. **Mixing zone** in the receiving water (regulatory distance per CPCB is 100 m minimum). 8. **Downstream transport, deposition, sediment-adsorbed metal transport.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.A(a)):** > Students often confuse "source" with "pathway." The **source** is *where the pollutant originates* (e.g., engine oil). The **pathway** is *the physical route it travels* (e.g., drain → culvert → river). For full 7 marks, you must clearly label both. Drawing a flow diagram earns 1 extra mark. --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given Data:** - Highway length $L_h = 30$ km = 30,000 m - Pavement width $W = 15$ m - Annual rainfall $P = 2800$ mm - Curve Number $CN = 80$ - Lead concentration $C_{Pb} = 0.3$ mg/L **Step 1 — Compute Catchment Area** $$A = 30{,}000 \times 15 = 450{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 45 \text{ ha}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Maximum Retention $S$** $$S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254 = \frac{25400}{80} - 254 = 317.5 - 254 = 63.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Annual Runoff Depth $Q$** Check: $0.2 \times S = 12.7$ mm. Since $P = 2800$ mm $\gg 12.7$ mm, runoff occurs. $$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} = \frac{(2800 - 12.7)^2}{2800 + 50.8}$$ $$Q = \frac{(2787.3)^2}{2850.8} = \frac{7{,}769{,}029.3}{2850.8} = 2725.2 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Runoff Coefficient $R$** $$R = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{2725.2}{2800} = 0.9733$$ **Step 5 — Apply Simple Method** $$L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A = 2800 \times 0.9733 \times 0.3 \times 45$$ $$L = 2800 \times 0.9733 = 2725.24$$ $$L = 2725.24 \times 0.3 = 817.57$$ $$L = 817.57 \times 45 = 36{,}790.6 \text{ kg/yr}$$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{Pb} \approx 36{,}791 \text{ kg/yr (≈ 36.8 tonnes/year)}}$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.A(b)):** > - **Forgetting unit conversion** from m² to ha — you will get the load wrong by a factor of **10,000**! Always convert. > - **Not checking $P > 0.2S$** before computing $Q$ — if $P < 0.2S$, the answer is **$Q = 0$** (no runoff). > - **Using $C$ in g/L instead of mg/L** — this changes the answer by **1000×**. > - **Using R (rational coefficient) ≠ C (concentration)** — students often mix up these two completely different "C" symbols. In $L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$: **$R$ = runoff coefficient, $C$ = concentration**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **concept of "First Flush Effect"** in highway stormwater quality. Discuss its significance in designing **BMPs (Best Management Practices)**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss any **five Best Management Practices (BMPs)** for controlling water quality impacts during the operational phase of a highway project. For each, state the **target pollutant(s)** and **removal efficiency** in percentage. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Definition of First Flush (2 Marks):** The **First Flush Effect** is the empirically observed phenomenon in urban/highway stormwater where a disproportionately large fraction of the cumulative pollutant load is transported in the initial portion of a runoff event. The **first 20–30% of runoff volume** typically carries **70–80% of the pollutant load**, including TSS, O&G, heavy metals, and nutrients. **Sketch (Described for 2 Marks):** A cumulative pollutant load curve plotted against cumulative runoff volume shows a **concave-down** shape — it rises steeply in the first 30% of runoff and then flattens. A **45° reference line** represents uniform wash-off; the first-flush curve lies above this line for the first fraction. **Significance in BMP Design (3 Marks):** 1. **Capture volume sizing** — Detention basins designed to capture only the *first flush* (e.g., first 12.5 mm of runoff, the "water quality volume") achieve >80% pollutant removal at minimal cost. 2. **Treatment train design** — The first flush justifies *unit-operation sequencing*: pre-treatment (oil-grit separator) → detention (settling) → filtration. 3. **Cost optimization** — Full-volume treatment is uneconomical; targeting first flush is the **most cost-effective strategy** (cited by USEPA and CPCB). 4. **Regulatory compliance** — CPCB specifies the "first 15 mm runoff capture" as the BMP design standard (CPCB, 2018 Stormwater Guidelines). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall (Q.B(a)):** Students often write "first 70% of volume carries 30% of pollutants" — this is the **opposite** of first flush. The correct statement is *"first 30% of volume carries 70% of pollutants."* --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Five BMPs for Highway Operational Water Quality:** | # | BMP | Target Pollutant(s) | Removal Efficiency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Vegetated Swales / Grassed Channels** | TSS, O&G, metals (adsorbed) | TSS: 60–80%; O&G: 50–70%; Metals: 40–60% | | 2 | **Oil-Water Separators (API / Coalescing type)** | O&G, free hydrocarbons | O&G: **80–95%** (most effective for O&G) | | 3 | **Detention / Retention Basins (Wet Ponds)** | TSS, BOD, nutrients, metals | TSS: 70–90%; Total P: 50–70%; Metals: 50–80% | | 4 | **Constructed Wetlands** | TSS, N, P, pathogens, metals | TSS: 80–95%; TN: 30–50%; TP: 50–70% | | 5 | **Infiltration Trenches / Bioretention** | TSS, dissolved metals, nutrients | TSS: 90%+; Dissolved Zn/Cu: 60–90% | **Selection Logic (Mentioned for Full 7 Marks):** - **BMP 1 + 2** form the **front-end treatment train** for road runoff. - **BMP 3** is the **central water quality volume storage** facility. - **BMP 4** is the **polishing step** for sensitive receiving waters. - **BMP 5** is preferred where **soil permeability > 10 mm/hr** and groundwater table is deep (>2 m). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B(b)):** > - Students often write vague BMPs like "plant trees." Be **specific**: name the engineered BMP, its target pollutant, and a numerical efficiency. > - **Do not exceed 5 BMPs** — the question is for 7 marks, and over-listing wastes time. Five is the *Goldilocks number*. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ 60-Second Rapid Revision — Highway Water Quality Impact** - [ ] **Definition:** Alteration of physical, chemical, biological water parameters by highway runoff/construction. - [ ] **3 Phases:** Construction (acute, TSS) → Operational (chronic, O&G + metals) → Accidental (episodic, severe). - [ ] **6 Top Parameters:** TSS, O&G, Pb, Zn, BOD, Chloride. - [ ] **POLLUTE Mnemonic** for pollutant categories. - [ ] **2 Key Methods:** SCS-CN (for runoff Q), Simple Method (for load L). - [ ] **Formulas to Memorize:** $S = 25400/CN - 254$, $Q = (P-0.2S)^2/(P+0.8S)$, $L = P \cdot R \cdot C \cdot A$. - [ ] **First Flush:** First 30% of volume carries 70–80% of pollutant load. - [ ] **5 Sampling Stations:** S1 (upstream 500m), S2 (downstream 50m), S3 (downstream 500m), S4 (downstream 2km), S5 (bore well/groundwater). - [ ] **5 BMPs:** Vegetated swale, oil-water separator, detention pond, constructed wetland, bioretention. - [ ] **Standard CPCB Limits:** TSS = 100 mg/L; O&G = 10 mg/L; Pb = 0.1 mg/L; BOD = 3 mg/L (Class C). - [ ] **Most Tested Topic:** Simple Method numerical (expect it in Part B). - [ ] **Most Common Mistake:** Confusing $R$ (runoff coefficient) with $C$ (concentration) in $L = PRCA$. - [ ] **Kerala-Specific Note:** High rainfall (~3000 mm/yr) amplifies pollutant load; coastal highways must monitor salinity intrusion. - [ ] **Legal Linkage:** EIA Notification 2006, IRC:SP:21, IS:2296, CPCB Schedule VI.

topics.title: Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Power Plants # ⚡ POWER PLANTS — Environmental Impact Analysis Perspective ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Power Plant** is an industrial facility designed for the **generation of electrical energy** through the conversion of primary energy sources (fossil fuels, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, biomass, or geothermal) into a usable secondary form (electricity), involving complex thermodynamic cycles, pollutant emission pathways, and significant land/water resource interactions that must be systematically evaluated under the EIA Notification, 2006 (and its amendments) of the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India. In the **EIA framework**, power plants are classified as **Category 'A' projects** (mandatorily requiring Environmental Clearance from the Central Government) if they exceed the following thresholds under **Schedule 1 of the EIA Notification, 2006**: | Project Type | Threshold Limit | |---|---| | Thermal Power Plants | ≥ 500 MW (coal/lignite/naphtha/gas) | | Nuclear Power Plants | All projects | | Hydro Power Plants | ≥ 50 MW | | Wind Power Farms | All in fragile ecosystems | | Solar Power Plants | All projects in wildlife/forest zones | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏭 > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Human Body Analogy:** > Think of a power plant as the **"stomach and lungs of an industrial city"**: > - **Input (Mouth) = Fuel & Water** → Coal, natural gas, uranium, or sunlight > - **Digestion (Boiler/Turbine) = Combustion/Conversion** → Heat → Mechanical → Electrical energy > - **Useful Output (Bloodstream) = Electricity** distributed to homes and industries > - **Waste Products (Excretion & Breath) = Flue gases, ash, wastewater, heat** > > Just as a city's metabolism generates waste, a power plant's "metabolism" produces **pollutants**. **EIA is the medical check-up** done *before* building it — predicting what kind of "waste" the city-plant will produce, how much, and how to keep it healthy for the environment. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard engineering metrics used in KTU board examinations: > - **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** = Actual Energy Generated / (Installed Capacity × Hours) — typically **60–80%** for coal plants > - **Auxiliary Consumption**: **8–12%** of gross generation > - **Heat Rate**: typically **2,000–2,500 kcal/kWh** for supercritical units > - **Specific Water Consumption**: **2.5–3.5 m³/MWh** (as per CEA norms for once-through cooling) > - **CO₂ Emission Factor (Indian coal grid average)**: **~0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** > - **Specific Coal Consumption**: **0.55–0.65 kg/kWh** for modern subcritical plants > - **Stack Height (Holland's formula)**: $H = 14 \, Q^{0.3}$ (in metres), where Q = SO₂ emission rate in kg/hr # 📚 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — TYPES & IMPACTS OF POWER PLANTS ## 2.1 Classification of Power Plants (Based on Energy Source) ### 2.1.1 Thermal Power Plants (Fossil Fuel Based) **Working Principle:** Chemical energy → Heat energy → Mechanical energy → Electrical energy (Rankine Cycle) **Sub-categories:** - **Coal/Lignite-based** (dominant in India — ~70% of installed capacity) - **Natural Gas-based** (Combined Cycle Gas Turbine — CCGT) - **Diesel-based** (only for peak load or remote areas) - **Oil-based** (Naphtha/diesel — rare, used as startup fuel) **EIA Critical Issues:** - Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀), SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, Mercury (Hg), Fly Ash (huge solid waste) - **Fly Ash generation**: ~120–150 million tonnes/year in India (huge disposal issue) - Water consumption, thermal discharge, ash pond leachate ### 2.1.2 Nuclear Power Plants **Working Principle:** Nuclear fission (U-235) → Heat → Steam → Turbine → Generator **EIA Critical Issues:** - Radioactive waste (low, intermediate, high-level) - Thermal pollution of cooling water - Risk of accidental releases (referenced by Chernobyl, Fukushima) - Long-term decommissioning liabilities ### 2.1.3 Hydroelectric Power Plants **Working Principle:** Potential energy of stored water → Kinetic energy → Turbine → Generator **EIA Critical Issues:** - Submergence of large land areas (forest/agricultural) - Displacement of human populations (rehabilitation & resettlement — R&R) - Sediment management, downstream ecology impact - Seismic risks in dam construction - Blocking fish migration routes ### 2.1.4 Renewable Energy Power Plants - **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) & Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** - **Wind Energy Farms** - **Biomass/Bagasse-based plants** - **Geothermal Plants** **EIA Critical Issues:** - Land use (large footprint for solar parks) - Bird/bat mortality in wind farms - Solar panel end-of-life disposal (e-waste) - Water use for cleaning mirrors in CSP ## 2.2 Major Environmental Impacts — Structured Analysis | Impact Category | Specific Pollutant/Effect | Primary Pathway | |---|---|---| | **Air Pollution** | PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, Hg, VOCs | Stack emissions | | **Water Pollution** | Heavy metals, suspended solids, oil & grease, thermal discharge | Cooling water blowdown, ash pond runoff | | **Soil Contamination** | Fly ash leachates, heavy metals | Ash disposal, accidental spills | | **Land Use Change** | Loss of forest/agricultural land, fragmentation | Plant footprint, ash pond, reservoir | | **Noise Pollution** | 85–110 dB(A) near plant boundary | Turbines, mills, cooling towers | | **Solid Waste** | Fly ash (60–70%), bottom ash (30–40%) | Combustion residue | | **Occupational Health** | Pneumoconiosis, asbestosis, hearing loss | Dust, noise, radiation | | **Biodiversity** | Habitat loss, thermal shock, entrainment | Reservoir, cooling water intake | | **Climate Change** | Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O) | Combustion flue gas | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU Part B questions!** | # | Formula | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = 14 \, Q^{0.3}$ | Holland's effective stack height | m, kg/hr | | 2 | $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H$ | Effective height (stack + plume rise) | m | | 3 | $\Delta H = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \left( 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s \cdot d} \right)$ | Plume rise (Briggs formula) | m | | 4 | $PLF = \dfrac{E_{gen}}{C_{inst} \times 8760} \times 100$ | Plant Load Factor | % | | 5 | $E_{SO_2} = 2 \times C_{coal} \times S \times 10^{-2}$ | SO₂ emission rate (where 2 = S→SO₂ stoichiometric factor) | kg/hr | | 6 | $E_{NO_x} = 1.53 \times 10^{-3} \times Q_{coal} \times N \times NCV$ | NOₓ emission (mass balance) | kg/hr | | 7 | $C_{max} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian plume (ground concentration) | µg/m³ | | 8 | $W_{spec} = \dfrac{V_{water}}{E_{gen}}$ | Specific water consumption | m³/MWh | | 9 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{3600}{q_{input}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency (where 3600 kJ = 1 kWh) | % | | 10 | $C_{ash} = A \times C_{coal} \times \eta_{collection}$ | Fly ash generation rate | tonnes/hr | ### Symbol Glossary - $H$ = Effective stack height (m); $H_s$ = Physical stack height; $\Delta H$ = Plume rise - $Q$ = Emission rate of pollutant (kg/hr) - $v_s$ = Stack gas velocity (m/s); $d$ = Stack diameter (m) - $u$ = Wind speed at stack height (m/s) - $P$ = Atmospheric pressure (mbar); $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (K); $T_s$ = Stack gas temperature (K) - $C_{coal}$ = Coal consumption rate (kg/hr); $S$ = Sulphur content (% by weight); $N$ = Nitrogen content (%) - $NCV$ = Net Calorific Value (kcal/kg) - $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = Horizontal & vertical dispersion coefficients (m) - $A$ = Ash content in coal (%); $\eta_{collection}$ = ESP collection efficiency - $E_{gen}$ = Energy generated (MWh); $C_{inst}$ = Installed capacity (MW) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🛠️ > [!NOTE] > These formulas are **operationally critical** for: > 1. **EIA Consultants** — designing air quality models (ISCST3, AERMOD) for prediction > 2. **Design Engineers** — sizing stacks, ESPs, FGDs, cooling towers > 3. **Regulatory Authorities** — verifying compliance with CPCB emission standards > 4. **Sustainability Auditors** — computing carbon footprint and water footprint of electricity # 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Holland's Formula for Effective Stack Height > [!IMPORTANT] > **Background:** The plume from a stack rises due to (i) momentum of exit gases and (ii) buoyancy from temperature difference. The total effective height that determines ground-level concentration is: > $$H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H$$ > Holland (1968) provided a simplified empirical formulation for the **buoyancy-driven plume rise**, ignoring momentum effects for hot gases. ### Step 1: Buoyancy Flux Definition The buoyancy flux parameter is defined as the rate of heat emission per unit length of plume cross-section: $$F_b = g \cdot v_s \cdot r^2 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s}$$ where $r = d/2$ is the stack radius. ### Step 2: Under Unstable/Neutral Conditions Holland's plume rise equation in its original form: $$\Delta H = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \left( 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s \cdot d} \right)$$ ### Step 3: Simplification for Indian CPCB Norms For typical Indian conditions ($P \approx 1000$ mbar, $T_s \approx 400$ K, $d \approx 4$ m, $v_s \approx 20$ m/s, $u \approx 5$ m/s), the buoyancy term dominates, leading to the **CPCB simplified form**: $$\Delta H \approx 1.5 \, \frac{v_s \, d}{u} + 6.37$$ ### Step 4: Effective Stack Height Therefore: $$H_{eff} = H_s + 1.5 \cdot \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} + 6.37 \text{ m}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SO₂ Emission & Stack Height Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem (KTU Style — 14 Mark Pattern):** A 500 MW coal-fired thermal power plant operates at 75% PLF. Coal properties: GCV = 4,000 kcal/kg, Sulphur = 0.6%, Calorific basis consumption = 0.6 kg/kWh. Compute: (a) hourly coal consumption; (b) SO₂ emission rate; (c) required effective stack height using Holland's formula. ### Part (a): Hourly Coal Consumption **Step 1 — Actual generation at 75% PLF:** $$E_{gen} = 500 \text{ MW} \times 0.75 = 375 \text{ MW} = 375{,}000 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Hourly energy generated (per hour basis):** $$E_{hour} = 375{,}000 \text{ kWh/hr}$$ **Step 3 — Coal consumption per hour:** $$C_{coal} = 0.6 \text{ kg/kWh} \times 375{,}000 \text{ kWh/hr} = 225{,}000 \text{ kg/hr} = 225 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$ ### Part (b): SO₂ Emission Rate **Step 4 — Stoichiometric conversion:** 1 kg of S produces 2 kg of SO₂ (S + O₂ → SO₂; molecular weights 32 and 64) **Step 5 — Apply mass balance:** $$E_{SO_2} = 2 \times C_{coal} \times S \times 10^{-2}$$ $$E_{SO_2} = 2 \times 225{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$E_{SO_2} = 2 \times 225{,}000 \times 0.006$$ $$E_{SO_2} = 2{,}700 \text{ kg/hr}$$ ### Part (c): Effective Stack Height (Holland's Formula) **Step 6 — Apply Holland's equation:** $$H = 14 \times Q^{0.3}$$ $$H = 14 \times (2700)^{0.3}$$ **Step 7 — Compute $2700^{0.3}$ using logarithms:** $$\log(2700) = \log(2.7 \times 10^3) = \log 2.7 + 3 = 0.4314 + 3 = 3.4314$$ $$0.3 \times 3.4314 = 1.02942$$ $$2700^{0.3} = 10^{1.02942} = 10.70$$ **Step 8 — Final stack height:** $$H = 14 \times 10.70 = 149.8 \text{ m} \approx 150 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **CPCB's Empirical Rule (alternative quick check):** $H(m) = 6 \sqrt{Q}$ where Q in kg/hr. Applying: $H = 6\sqrt{2700} = 6 \times 51.96 = 311.8$ m — this is **more conservative**. Designers use the **higher** of the two to be on the safer side. --- ## 3.3 Ground Level Concentration (Gaussian Plume — Maximum Value) For a point source with effective height $H$, the **maximum ground-level concentration** at the distance of maximum concentration $x_{max}$ is: $$C_{max} = \dfrac{2Q}{e \cdot \pi \cdot u \cdot H^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$$ which simplifies (using standard Pasquill-Gifford coefficients) for typical conditions to: $$C_{max} \approx \frac{Q}{2.7 \cdot u \cdot H^2} \text{ (µg/m}^3\text{)}$$ This equation is the **cornerstone of regulatory EIA modelling** for power plant stacks. # 📊 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Plant Environmental Impact Pathways ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Plant Inputs] --> B[Coal/Fuel Storage] A --> C[Water Intake] A --> D[Land Footprint] B --> E[Combustion Chamber] C --> F[Cooling System] D --> G[Ash Disposal Area] E --> H[Flue Gas] E --> I[Bottom Ash] E --> J[Fly Ash] F --> K[Thermal Discharge] F --> L[Cooling Tower Drift] H --> M[Air Pollution
SO2 NOX PM CO2 Hg] I --> N[Solid Waste
Leachate] J --> O[Atmospheric PM
Fallout] K --> P[Water Pollution
Thermal Shock] L --> Q[Water Vapour
Drift Deposition] N --> R[Soil Contamination] O --> R M --> S[Environmental Receptors
Humans Flora Fauna] P --> S Q --> S R --> S S --> T[Impact Assessment
under EIA Notification 2006] T --> U[Mitigation Measures] U --> V[Environmental Clearance
Consent to Establish Operate] style A fill:#FFD700 style E fill:#FF6B6B style M fill:#FF8C00 style K fill:#1E90FF style S fill:#90EE90 style T fill:#9370DB ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — EIA Process Flow for Power Plants ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Project Proposal
500MW TPP] --> P2[Screening] P2 --> P3[Scoping] P3 --> P4[Baseline Data
Collection] P4 --> P5[Impact Prediction] P5 --> P6[Air Dispersion Model] P5 --> P7[Water Quality Model] P5 --> P8[Noise Model] P5 --> P9[Ecological Assessment] P6 --> P10[Evaluation and
Review] P7 --> P10 P8 --> P10 P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11[EIA Report
Preparation] P11 --> P12[Public Hearing] P12 --> P13[Expert Appraisal
Committee EAC] P13 --> P14{Decision} P14 --> P15[Environmental
Clearance Granted] P14 --> P16[Rejected or
Modifications Required] P15 --> P17[Monitoring and
Compliance] P17 --> P18[Six Monthly Reports
to CPCB/SPCB] style P1 fill:#FFE4B5 style P11 fill:#98FB98 style P13 fill:#DDA0DD style P15 fill:#90EE90 style P18 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pollution Control Train in a Thermal Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR FC[Fuel Coal] --> BOIL[Boiler Furnace] BOIL --> ESP[Electrostatic
Precipitator] ESP --> FGD[Flue Gas
Desulphurization] FGD --> SCR[Selective Catalytic
Reduction for NOX] SCR --> STACK[Stack
Chimney] STACK --> ATM[Atmosphere] ESP --> ASH1[Fly Ash
Hopper] BOIL --> ASH2[Bottom Ash
Hopper] ASH1 --> ASH3[Ash Pond or
Utilization] ASH2 --> ASH3 BOIL --> CW[Cooling Water
System] CW --> CT[Cooling Tower] CT --> EFF[Effluent
Treatment Plant] EFF --> DIS[Discharge to
Environment] style BOIL fill:#FF6B6B style ESP fill:#FFA500 style FGD fill:#FFD700 style SCR fill:#90EE90 style STACK fill:#87CEEB style ASH3 fill:#D3D3D3 ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"List the major categories of environmental impacts from a coal-based thermal power plant."** **Model Answer (KTU Valuation Key Pattern):** A coal-based thermal power plant causes the following major environmental impacts: 1. **Air Pollution** — Emission of Particulate Matter (PM), Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Carbon Monoxide (CO), Mercury (Hg), and unburnt hydrocarbons through the stack. 2. **Water Pollution** — Discharge of hot cooling water (thermal pollution), ash pond leachate containing heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg), and oily wastewater from plant operations. 3. **Land/Soil Degradation** — Massive fly ash and bottom ash generation (~120–150 million tonnes/year in India) requiring ash ponds, leading to land acquisition and leachate contamination. 4. **Noise Pollution** — 85–110 dB(A) from coal crushers, mills, turbines, and cooling tower fans. 5. **Biodiversity Loss** — Habitat destruction in mining areas, thermal shock to aquatic life at cooling water intake/discharge points. 6. **Climate Change Contribution** — Significant CO₂ emissions (~0.82 kg CO₂/kWh for Indian coal). 7. **Solid/Hazardous Waste** — Fly ash, bottom ash, used oil, spent catalysts. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"What is Plant Load Factor (PLF)? Mention its significance in EIA studies."** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Plant Load Factor (PLF) is the ratio of actual energy generated by a power plant to the maximum possible energy that could have been generated if the plant operated at its installed capacity continuously for the entire period. **Mathematical Expression:** $$PLF = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Time Period (hr)}} \times 100\%$$ **Typical Values:** Indian coal-based plants operate at **60–80% PLF**. **Significance in EIA Studies:** 1. PLF directly determines **fuel consumption**, **pollutant emission rates**, and **resource (water/land) demand** projections during the EIA baseline. 2. EIA consultants use the **design PLF** (not maximum capacity) to estimate **realistic environmental loads** and design pollution control equipment (ESP, FGD, STP). 3. Lower PLF means **sub-optimal resource use per kWh**, increasing specific emissions and water consumption per unit of electricity. 4. PLF helps in **comparative impact assessment** of different plant technologies (coal vs. gas vs. renewable). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Type **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** > **"Explain in detail the major environmental impacts of a coal-based thermal power plant. Discuss the various pollution control measures adopted in the plant with reference to air, water, and solid waste management."** **Distribution:** (a) Environmental Impacts — 7 marks, (b) Pollution Control Measures — 7 marks --- ### Part (a) — Major Environmental Impacts [7 Marks] **1. Air Pollution Impacts (2 marks):** - **Particulate Matter (PM):** Coal combustion releases fine PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀ causing respiratory ailments (bronchitis, asthma), reduced visibility, and soiling. - **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):** From sulphur in coal (0.3–1.5% in Indian coal); causes acid rain, damages crops and buildings. - **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ):** From thermal NOₓ at high flame temperatures; contributes to smog, ozone formation, and respiratory illness. - **Greenhouse Gases:** CO₂ is the principal GHG (~0.82 kg/kWh) contributing to climate change. - **Trace Metals:** Mercury (Hg), Arsenic (As), Lead (Pb) — neurotoxic, bioaccumulative. **2. Water Pollution Impacts (1.5 marks):** - **Thermal Discharge:** Cooling water returns 5–10°C warmer, reducing dissolved oxygen and causing aquatic ecosystem stress. - **Ash Pond Leachate:** Heavy metal-laden water (As, Cd, Se, B) contaminates groundwater. - **Effluents:** Oil & grease, high TDS, suspended solids from plant operations. **3. Land & Solid Waste Impacts (1.5 marks):** - **Fly Ash Generation:** 60–70% of total ash is fly ash; ~150 million tonnes/year nationally. - **Ash Pond Footprint:** Each MW of capacity requires ~0.3–0.5 acres of ash pond. - **Land Subsidence Risk** in ash pond areas, contamination of agricultural soil. **4. Other Impacts (2 marks):** - **Noise Pollution** (85–110 dB) — hearing impairment, stress. - **Biodiversity Loss** — thermal shock, habitat loss from mining and reservoir. - **Social Impacts** — displacement due to mining, R&R issues. - **Occupational Health Hazards** — silicosis, asbestosis, dermatitis. --- ### Part (b) — Pollution Control Measures [7 Marks] **1. Air Pollution Control (3 marks):** | Pollutant | Control Technology | Efficiency | |---|---|---| | Particulate Matter | **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | 99.5–99.9% | | SO₂ | **Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD)** — Wet limestone gypsum | 90–95% | | NOₓ | **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** / Low NOₓ burners | 70–90% | | Mercury | Activated Carbon Injection, Wet scrubbers | 80–90% | | PM₂.₅ | Bag filters, hybrid filters | 99.9% | **2. Water Pollution Control (2 marks):** - **Cooling Towers** (induced draft) reduce thermal discharge temperature. - **Closed-cycle cooling** systems minimize water withdrawal. - **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** for ash pond runoff — lime dosing, settling, neutralization. - **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** systems for new plants. - **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** for domestic wastewater. **3. Solid Waste (Ash) Management (1.5 marks):** - **100% Fly Ash Utilization Notification (1999, amended 2016)** mandates: - Ash bricks, cement, road embankments, mine filling - Target: 100% utilization within 5 years - **Dry ash collection & handling** to prevent leachate. - **High Concentration Slurry Disposal (HCSD)** in lined ash ponds. - **Bottom ash** used in construction aggregates. **4. Additional Measures (0.5 marks):** - Green belt development (35% area as mandated by CPCB). - Acoustic enclosures for noisy equipment. - Rainwater harvesting. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > **"Discuss the Environmental Impact Assessment procedure for a proposed 1000 MW coal-based supercritical thermal power plant in a coastal area. List the baseline data required, impacts to be assessed, and the regulatory framework involved."** **Distribution:** (a) Baseline Data & Site Considerations — 7 marks, (b) Impacts, Modeling & Regulatory Framework — 7 marks --- ### Part (a) — Baseline Data & Site Considerations [7 Marks] **1. Project Description (1.5 marks):** - **Capacity:** 1000 MW (2 × 500 MW supercritical units) - **Fuel:** Imported/indigenous coal, ~4.5 million tonnes/year - **Water demand:** ~30–35 MCM/year (coastal location → sea water cooling) - **Land:** ~1,000–1,200 acres (main plant + ash pond + township) - **Stack height (estimated):** $H = 14 \times Q^{0.3}$ ≈ 220–250 m **2. Baseline Data Collection (3 marks):** | Environmental Component | Parameters Monitored | Duration | |---|---|---| | **Meteorology** | Wind speed, direction, temperature, humidity, rainfall, stability class | 1 year minimum | | **Air Quality** | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, Hg | 24-hr sampling, 1 season × 4 | | **Water Quality** | Coastal: pH, DO, BOD, COD, TDS, salinity, heavy metals | 4 seasons | | **Soil Quality** | Texture, pH, organic carbon, NPK, heavy metals | Grid sampling | | **Noise** | Day & Night Leq dB(A) at receptor locations | Continuous | | **Ecology** | Flora & fauna inventory, mangroves (coastal!), marine biodiversity | Field survey | | **Socio-economic** | Demographics, livelihoods, cultural heritage sites | Census + survey | | **Marine Ecology** | Phytoplankton, zooplankton, benthos, fish diversity | Pre-monsoon & post-monsoon | **3. Site-Specific Coastal Considerations (2.5 marks):** - **Sea water intake** — fish impingement & entrainment impacts. - **Thermal plume dispersion** in coastal waters — modelling required. - **Chlorine residual** from cooling water biocide treatment — toxic to marine life. - **Mangrove ecosystems** (if present) — protected under Forest Conservation Act. - **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) clearance** mandatory under CRZ Notification, 2019. - **Submarine outfall** design for brine discharge. --- ### Part (b) — Impacts, Modelling & Regulatory Framework [7 Marks] **1. Key Impacts to be Assessed (2.5 marks):** - **Air:** Stack emissions affecting ~25–30 km radius via Gaussian/AERMOD modelling. - **Marine:** Intake mortality, thermal discharge zone, brine plume. - **Ash disposal:** Fly ash utilization plan & ash pond design. - **Socio-economic:** Employment generation (positive), influx of migrant workers (negative). - **Cumulative impact:** Multiple plants in same coastal belt. - **Climate:** GHG footprint and coal transport emissions. **2. Predictive Modelling Tools (1.5 marks):** - **AERMOD / ISCST3** — Air dispersion - **MIKE 21 / DELFT3D** — Coastal hydrodynamics & thermal plume - **MODFLOW** — Groundwater contamination from ash ponds - **ENVI / ERDAS** — Remote sensing for land use change **3. Regulatory Framework (3 marks):** - **EIA Notification, 2006** (amended 2020) — MoEF&CC clearance - **Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** — Consent to Establish & Operate from SPCB - **Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** — SPCB consent - **Forest Conservation Act, 1980** — If forest land involved - **Wildlife Protection Act, 1972** — If near protected areas - **CRZ Notification, 2019** — Coastal Regulation Zone clearance - **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016** - **Fly Ash Notification, 1999** (amended 2016) — 100% utilization mandate - **CPCB Emission Standards for TPS** (December 2015, revised 2024) — PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³, SO₂ ≤ 200 mg/Nm³, NOₓ ≤ 100 mg/Nm³ for new units - **Public Hearing** mandatory under EIA Notification 2006. --- ## 🎯 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Writing only generic impacts** without naming the specific pollutant, its source pathway, and numerical standards — KTU examiners expect *quantitative* answers wherever possible. > 2. **Forgetting to mention regulatory references** — Always cite **EIA Notification 2006**, **CPCB standards**, and **CRZ Notification** for coastal projects. > 3. **Confusing PLF with Availability Factor** — they are different concepts. > 4. **Skipping the stoichiometric factor (×2 for S→SO₂)** in emission calculations — this is the #1 numerical error. > 5. **Not drawing neat block diagrams** — KTU Part B questions on power plants *require* a labelled schematic for full marks. > 6. **Omitting public hearing and rehabilitation** — these are *mandatory* EIA components and frequently asked. > 7. **Mixing up "Consent to Establish" and "Consent to Operate"** — these are sequential and time-bound. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ Power plants are **Category A projects** under EIA Notification 2006 (≥500 MW thermal, ≥50 MW hydro, all nuclear). - ✅ Key **pollutants** from coal plants: PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO₂, Hg, fly ash. - ✅ **Holland's stack height formula**: $H = 14 Q^{0.3}$ (m, kg/hr). - ✅ **SO₂ emission**: $E_{SO_2} = 2 \times C_{coal} \times S \times 10^{-2}$. - ✅ **NOₓ emission**: $E_{NO_x} = 1.53 \times 10^{-3} \times Q_{coal} \times N \times NCV$. - ✅ **PLF** is the realistic operating parameter for EIA baseline estimation. - ✅ Pollution control train: **ESP → FGD → SCR → Stack**. - ✅ **100% fly ash utilization** is mandatory under the 1999 Notification (amended 2016). - ✅ Coastal power plants require **CRZ Clearance** in addition to EC. - ✅ **CPCB standards (new units, 2024)**: PM ≤ 30, SO₂ ≤ 200, NOₓ ≤ 100 mg/Nm³. - ✅ **Green belt** = minimum **35% of plant area** with native species. - ✅ **Public Hearing** is non-negotiable for Category A projects. - ✅ **Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA)** and **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** must accompany EIA Report. - ✅ For **hydro plants**: Submergence, R&R, seismic risk, downstream ecology are paramount. - ✅ For **nuclear plants**: AERB licensing, radioactive waste management, decommissioning plan required.

topics.title: Agriculture and Irrigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Agriculture and Irrigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA) of Agriculture and Irrigation** is the systematic process of identifying, predicting, evaluating, and mitigating the biophysical, ecological, hydrological, and socio-economic consequences of agricultural development schemes and irrigation infrastructure projects on the existing agro-ecosystem, soil medium, water resources, farm communities, and dependent livelihoods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *Agriculture and Irrigation sector EIA evaluates the interaction between developmental interventions (such as dams, canals, barrages, command area development, drainage networks, watershed programs, and agro-industrial projects) and the agricultural environment, including land, soil, water, crops, livestock, farmers, and ecological services.*

The scope under KTU Module 4 covers:
- **Irrigation Projects** – Major, Medium, and Minor schemes (dams, weirs, lift schemes, canal commands, sprinkler/drip systems).
- **Agricultural Development Projects** – Land reclamation, watershed development, fertilizer/pesticide intensive farming, farm mechanization, plantation crops, and agro-processing industries.
- **Command Area Development (CAD)** – On-farm development works and water management.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sponge and the Bucket"**
> Imagine agricultural land as a **sponge** soaking up water from a controlled **bucket** (irrigation source). The sponge holds nutrients and crops depend on this balance.
>
> - **Dam/Canal projects** = suddenly filling or emptying the bucket too fast.
> - **Over-irrigation** = sponge becomes waterlogged, suffocating plant roots (like a soaked towel that never dries).
> - **Pesticide use** = adding poison to the water; the sponge holds the poison and slowly releases it into the food chain.
> - **Land acquisition** = cutting the sponge into pieces; farmers (sponge-holders) lose their livelihood base.
>
> **EIA acts like a doctor** checking the sponge's health **before** treatment, ensuring the agricultural "patient" survives the project.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SAR Threshold** (Residual Sodium Carbonate) | $\vert \text{SAR} \vert \geq 13$ | Unsafe for irrigation (US Salinity Lab) |
| **Crop Water Requirement ($ET_c$)** | $400$ – $1200$ mm/season | Varies with crop and climate |
| **Irrigation Efficiency ($\eta$)** | $30\% – 60\%$ (surface) ; $\geq 90\%$ (drip) | Surface vs micro-irrigation |
| **Soil Erosion Tolerance (T-value)** | $4.5$ – $11.2$ t/ha/yr | Maximum allowable soil loss |
| **Global Average Water Use Efficiency** | $\approx 40\%$ | Fraction of water consumed by crops |
| **Permissible ECe (Salinity)** | $2$ – $4$ dS/m | Threshold for sensitive crops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize **SAR**, **ECe**, and **T-value** — these appear frequently in numerical and conceptual questions.

---

### 1.4 Importance in the Indian Context

India holds the **world's largest irrigated area** (~70 million hectares) and the agricultural sector sustains **~58% of the population**. Consequently, every major dam, thermal power plant with ash ponds, or industrial corridor requires rigorous agriculture and irrigation EIA to:
1. Protect **food security**.
2. Prevent **farmer displacement** (a sensitive socio-political issue).
3. Address the **water–energy–food nexus**.
4. Ensure compliance with the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Projects Covered Under Agriculture & Irrigation EIA

The major project categories identified under KTU Module 4 are listed below:

- **Major Irrigation Projects** — dams > 50 m height, command area > 10,000 ha.
- **Inter-basin Water Transfer Links** — e.g., Ken-Betwa, Godavari-Krishna.
- **Watershed Development Programs** — under MGNREGA, IWMP.
- **Land Reclamation & Drainage Projects** — for waterlogged/saline soils.
- **Agro-based Industries** — sugar mills, pulp & paper, oil extraction, bioethanol.
- **Aquaculture & Coastal Aquaculture** — brackishwater shrimp farming.
- **Greenfield Townships / SEZs on Farmland** — require agricultural land conversion assessment.

---

### 2.2 Key Environmental Impacts in Agriculture & Irrigation EIA

The impacts are structured into four interacting compartments:

#### 2.2.1 Impacts on Soil Medium
- **Waterlogging** — rising water table ($< 2$ m depth from surface).
- **Salinization & Sodicity** — accumulation of soluble salts.
- **Soil Erosion** — sheet, rill, and gully erosion.
- **Loss of Soil Fertility** — nutrient mining.
- **Soil Contamination** — heavy metals, pesticide residues.

#### 2.2.2 Impacts on Water Resources
- **Reduction in downstream river flows** affecting riparian agriculture.
- **Groundwater depletion** due to canal seepage or excessive pumping.
- **Water quality degradation** — salinity, sodicity, nitrate leaching.
- **Disruption of natural drainage patterns**.

#### 2.2.3 Impacts on Agricultural Production System
- **Submergence of fertile land** under reservoir backwater.
- **Loss of crop diversity** due to monoculture in command area.
- **Pest and weed infestation** — *Eichhornia crassipes* in irrigation channels.
- **Crop pattern changes** — shift from traditional to HYV seeds.
- **Reduced grazing land & fisheries** downstream.

#### 2.2.4 Socio-Economic Impacts
- **Displacement & rehabilitation** of farming families.
- **Loss of Common Property Resources (CPRs)**.
- **Inequitable water distribution** — head-reach vs tail-reach farmers.
- **Health impacts** — waterborne diseases, malaria, fluorosis.
- **Cultural & livelihood disruption**.

---

### 2.3 Methodology for Agriculture & Irrigation EIA

The **logical step-wise framework** prescribed by the **MoEFCC EIA Notification 2006 (and its amendments)** is:

1. **Screening** — based on project category schedule.
2. **Scoping** — identification of Valued Environmental Components (VECs):
   - Agricultural land area
   - Crop yield (kg/ha/season)
   - Irrigation intensity (%)
   - Soil quality index (SQI)
   - Groundwater table depth (m)
   - Livestock population
3. **Baseline Data Collection** — soil profile analysis, hydrology, agronomy, socio-economic survey.
4. **Impact Prediction** — using matrices, overlays, mathematical models.
5. **Evaluation of Significance** — magnitude $\times$ duration $\times$ reversibility.
6. **Mitigation & Environmental Management Plan (EMP)**.
7. **Monitoring & Post-Project Audit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Order?** Each step provides the input data for the next. Skipping baseline data collection is the most common reason KTU answers lose marks.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations form the **numerical backbone** of Agriculture & Irrigation EIA questions.

| Symbol | Formula / Equation | Application in EIA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Crop Water Requirement** | $ET_c \;=\; K_c \times ET_0$ | Sizing irrigation; predicting drought impact |
| **Irrigation Requirement** | $IR \;=\; ET_c - P_{eff} + \text{Losses}$ | Net water to be supplied |
| **Irrigation Efficiency** | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{W_{used\,productively}}{W_{applied}} \times 100$ | Benchmarking water use |
| **SAR** | $\text{SAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Na}^+}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$ | Sodicity hazard assessment |
| **Residual Sodium Carbonate** | $\text{RSC} \;=\; (\text{CO}_3^{2-}+\text{HCO}_3^-) - (\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+})$ | Carbonate-induced alkalinity |
| **Salinity (ECe)** | $\text{ECe} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Total\,Dissolved\,Salts (mg/L)}}{640}$ | Conductivity-salt conversion |
| **Water Balance** | $\Delta S \;=\; P + I - ET_c - D - R$ | Predicting waterlogging |
| **Soil Loss (USLE)** | $A \;=\; R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$ | Annual soil erosion prediction |
| **Cropping Intensity** | $CI \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Gross\,Cropped\,Area}}{\text{Net\,Sown\,Area}} \times 100$ | Measure of agricultural intensification |
| **Land Acquisition Index** | $\text{LAI} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Area\,Acquired (ha)}}{\text{Total\,Project\,Area (ha)}}$ | Displacement magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (Strict for KTU Answers)**
> - SAR, RSC — in **meq/L** (milli-equivalents per litre).
> - ECe — in **dS/m** (deciSiemens per metre) at 25°C.
> - ET — in **mm/day** or **mm/season**.
> - USLE soil loss A — in **t/ha/yr**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic — they drive actual project decisions:
- **SAR & ECe** dictate whether a canal command will turn saline in 10 years (the fate of Indira Gandhi Canal in Rajasthan).
- **USLE** justifies the budget for **erosion control works** in watershed projects.
- **Water Balance** determines whether **drainage infrastructure** (subsurface drains) is mandatory under a CAD program.
- **Cropping Intensity** is a **key indicator** used by the **World Bank** to evaluate agricultural development loan performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR) and Irrigation Suitability

The **Sodium Adsorption Ratio** quantifies the sodium hazard of irrigation water relative to calcium and magnesium. The full derivation from the **Gapon Equation** is shown below.

**Starting Point — Gapon Cation Exchange Equation:**

$$
K_G \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{soil}}{[\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{soil}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{[\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{water}}}{[\text{Na}^+]_{water}}
$$

where $K_G$ is the Gapon selectivity coefficient, a dimensionless constant.

**Rearrangement to Solve for the Sodium-to-Ca+Mg Ratio in Soil:**

$$
\dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{soil}}{[\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{soil}} \;=\; K_G \times \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{water}}{\sqrt{[\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{water}}}
$$

**Defining SAR (concentrations in meq/L):**

$$
\text{SAR} \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{water}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{water}+[\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{water}}{2}}}
$$

**Substituting the Gapon definition:**

$$
\text{SAR} \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{water}}{\sqrt{([\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{water}+[\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{water})/2}}
$$

**Interpretation Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAR} &< 10 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{No restriction on irrigation} \\
10 \leq \text{SAR} &\leq 18 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Slight to moderate restriction} \\
18 < \text{SAR} &\leq 26 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Severe restriction} \\
\text{SAR} &> 26 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Unsuitable for almost all soils}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soil Loss Using the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)

**Wischmeier & Smith (1978) defined soil loss as:**

$$
A \;=\; R \times K \times LS \times C \times P
$$

**Term-by-Term Interpretation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &:\; \text{Average annual soil loss (t/ha/yr)} \\
R &:\; \text{Rainfall erosivity factor (MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr)} \\
K &:\; \text{Soil erodibility factor (t·ha·hr/ha·MJ·mm)} \\
LS &:\; \text{Slope length and steepness factor (dimensionless)} \\
C &:\; \text{Cover management factor (dimensionless, } 0 \text{ to } 1.5\text{)} \\
P &:\; \text{Support practice factor (dimensionless, } 0 \text{ to } 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given the values for a watershed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 250 \; \text{MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr} \\
K &= 0.32 \; \text{t·ha·hr/ha·MJ·mm} \\
LS &= 1.8 \\
C &= 0.20 \quad \text{(forest cover)} \\
P &= 1.0 \quad \text{(no conservation practice)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply R × K:**

$$
R \times K \;=\; 250 \times 0.32 \;=\; 80
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by LS:**

$$
(R \times K) \times LS \;=\; 80 \times 1.8 \;=\; 144
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by C:**

$$
(R \times K \times LS) \times C \;=\; 144 \times 0.20 \;=\; 28.8
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by P:**

$$
A \;=\; 28.8 \times 1.0 \;=\; 28.8 \;\text{t/ha/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with T-value:**

$$
T \;=\; 11.2 \;\text{t/ha/yr} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A > T
$$

**Conclusion:** Soil loss exceeds permissible tolerance → **Mitigation mandatory** (contour bunding, terrace farming, afforestation, mulch cover).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Water Balance for Predicting Waterlogging

The **water balance equation** for a command area is:

$$
\Delta S \;=\; P + I - ET_c - D - R_{off}
$$

**Term Definitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &:\; \text{Change in soil moisture storage (mm)} \\
P &:\; \text{Effective precipitation (mm)} \\
I &:\; \text{Irrigation applied (mm)} \\
ET_c &:\; \text{Crop evapotranspiration (mm)} \\
D &:\; \text{Deep percolation / drainage (mm)} \\
R_{off} &:\; \text{Surface runoff leaving the area (mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

$$
P = 600 \text{ mm}, \quad I = 1200 \text{ mm}, \quad ET_c = 850 \text{ mm}, \quad R_{off} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Solving for Required Drainage D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= 0 \quad \text{(steady state, no soil moisture change over the year)} \\
0 &= 600 + 1200 - 850 - D - 150 \\
D &= 600 + 1200 - 850 - 150 \\
D &= 1800 - 1000 \\
D &= 800 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance for EIA:** A drainage requirement of **800 mm/year** (≈ 2.2 mm/day) confirms the **need for subsurface drainage**, else the water table rises and waterlogging ensues. This is the classic Indus Basin (Pakistan) and Ukai–Kakrapar (Gujarat) command area failure story.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Irrigation Efficiency Chain

The **overall project efficiency** is the product of three component efficiencies:

$$
\eta_{overall} \;=\; \eta_{conveyance} \times \eta_{application} \times \eta_{field}
$$

**Typical Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{conveyance} &\approx 0.70 \quad \text{(lined canals)} \\
\eta_{application} &\approx 0.60 \quad \text{(surface flooding)} \\
\eta_{field} &\approx 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\eta_{overall} \;=\; 0.70 \times 0.60 \times 0.80 \;=\; 0.336 \;\approx\; 33.6\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **33.6%** of water released from the dam reaches the crop root zone productively. The remaining **66.4%** is lost to seepage, deep percolation, and runoff — a strong case for drip/sprinkler retrofitting in EMP.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Impact Identification (Leopold Matrix Style)

| Project Activity | Soil | Water | Crops | Livestock | Farmers | Air/Climate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dam Construction** | Submergence | Downstream flow reduction | Loss of riverine plots | Loss of grazing | Displacement | Reservoir methane |
| **Canal Lining** | Reduced seepage | Lower water table in vicinity | Better water delivery | Neutral | Equity issues | Dust suppression |
| **Excess Irrigation** | Waterlogging, salinization | Groundwater rise | Reduced aeration | Fodder loss | Yield decline | Humidity rise |
| **Pesticide Use** | Toxic residue | Surface & groundwater contamination | Residue in produce | Bioaccumulation | Health risks | Spray drift |
| **Land Acquisition** | Topsoil loss | Hydrology change | Permanent cropland loss | Loss of fodder | Livelihood loss | Dust |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Compliance
- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
- Node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italics, or HTML.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram 1 — EIA Process Flow for Agriculture & Irrigation Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[PROJECT CONCEPTION DAM OR CANAL OR WATERSHED] --> nodeB[SCREENING PER EIA NOTIFICATION 2006]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SCOPING IDENTIFY VECS SOIL WATER CROP FARMER]
    nodeC --> nodeD[BASELINE DATA SOIL HYDROLOGY AGRONOMY SOCIOECONOMIC]
    nodeD --> nodeE[IMPACT PREDICTION MATRIX MODEL USLE SAR]
    nodeE --> nodeF[SIGNIFICANCE EVALUATION MAGNITUDE DURATION REVERSIBILITY]
    nodeF --> nodeG[MITIGATION MEASURES DRAINAGE LINING CROPPING PLAN]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT PLAN EMP]
    nodeH --> nodeI[MONITORING AND AUDIT]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[DECISION MAKER APPROVAL OR REJECTION]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[PROJECT EXECUTION WITH CONDITIONS]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram 2 — Agriculture and Irrigation Impact Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    act1[DAM OR CANAL CONSTRUCTION] --> act2[CHANGED HYDROLOGY]
    act2 --> act3[GROUNDWATER TABLE CHANGE]
    act3 --> act4[WATERLOGGING OR DROUGHT]
    act4 --> act5[SOIL SALINIZATION OR EROSION]
    act5 --> act6[CROP YIELD LOSS]
    act6 --> act7[FARMER LIVELIHOOD IMPACT]
    act7 --> act8[MITIGATION DRAINAGE LINING SALT TOLERANT CROPS]
    act8 --> act9[RESILIENT AGRICULTURE]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture of Agriculture EIA Sector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS
        in1[CLIMATIC DATA RAINFALL TEMPERATURE]
        in2[SOIL DATA TEXTURE PH ORGANIC CARBON]
        in3[WATER DATA QUALITY QUANTITY AVAILABILITY]
        in4[SOCIOECONOMIC DATA LAND HOLDING INCOME CROP PATTERN]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING
        pr1[USLE SOIL LOSS]
        pr2[SAR SALINITY]
        pr3[WATER BALANCE]
        pr4[CROP WATER REQUIREMENT]
        pr5[SOCIO IMPACT INDEX]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        out1[IMPACT MAGNITUDE MATRIX]
        out2[MITIGATION MEASURES]
        out3[EMP BUDGET AND SCHEDULE]
        out4[MONITORING PROTOCOL]
    end

    in1 --> pr4
    in2 --> pr1
    in2 --> pr2
    in3 --> pr2
    in3 --> pr3
    in3 --> pr4
    in4 --> pr5
    pr1 --> out1
    pr2 --> out1
    pr3 --> out1
    pr4 --> out1
    pr5 --> out1
    out1 --> out2
    out1 --> out3
    out1 --> out4
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram 4 — Irrigation Induced Salinization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[OVER IRRIGATION] --> step2[SEEPAGE AND PERCOLATION]
    step2 --> step3[RISING WATER TABLE]
    step3 --> step4[CAPILLARY RISE OF SALTS]
    step4 --> step5[SALT ACCUMULATION ON SURFACE]
    step5 --> step6[SALINE SOIL ECe ABOVE THRESHOLD]
    step6 --> step7[YIELD DECLINE OR CROP FAILURE]
    step7 --> step8[REMEDIATION SUBSURFACE DRAINAGE GYPSUM APPLICATION]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1.** Define the term **Environmental Impact Analysis** in the context of Agriculture and Irrigation projects. Mention any two objectives. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** *Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA) of Agriculture and Irrigation projects is a systematic process used to identify, predict, evaluate, and mitigate the environmental consequences of irrigation schemes (dams, canals, command areas), watershed programs, and agro-industrial projects on the agricultural ecosystem, soil, water, crops, and farming communities.*

**Two Objectives (2 Marks):**
1. To predict and minimize adverse impacts such as **waterlogging**, **salinization**, and **soil erosion** caused by irrigation projects.
2. To ensure **equitable water distribution**, sustainable crop productivity, and protection of farmer livelihoods through a structured **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Definition 1M + 2 Objectives × 1M = 3M. Use **keywords in bold** for examiner visibility.

---

#### **Q2.** What is **Waterlogging**? State any two of its causes and two preventive measures in an irrigation command area. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** *Waterlogging is the phenomenon in which the groundwater table rises to within ~2 m of the root zone, saturating the soil profile and depriving crop roots of oxygen, leading to yield decline or crop failure.*

**Two Causes (1 Mark):**
1. **Over-irrigation** without adequate surface and subsurface drainage.
2. **Seepage from unlined canals** raising the regional water table.

**Two Preventive Measures (1 Mark):**
1. **Lining of canals** and adoption of **drip/sprinkler irrigation** to reduce seepage.
2. Installation of **subsurface drainage systems** and growing **salt-tolerant crops**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write generic EIA definitions — tailor to Agriculture/Irrigation.
> - **Always** include a labeled **diagram** (block diagram or flowchart) — answers without diagrams lose **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> - **Numerical** sub-parts require **unit display** and **stepwise substitution** — skipping steps attracts partial marking penalty.
> - **Mitigation measures** must be **specific** (e.g., "subsurface tile drainage at 60 cm depth, 50 m spacing") — avoid vague terms like "improved drainage".

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

#### **Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **methodology for conducting EIA of a major irrigation project** with reference to identification of impacts on soil, water, crops, and socio-economic aspects. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Screening and Scoping (2 Marks):**

The project is screened under **EIA Notification 2006, Schedule 1(a)**. Scoping identifies the **Valued Environmental Components (VECs)**:
- Soil — texture, salinity, fertility, erosion status.
- Water — river hydrology, groundwater table, water quality.
- Crops — type, area, yield, cropping intensity.
- Socio-economic — farmer population, land holdings, CPRs.

**Step 2 — Baseline Data Collection (2 Marks):**
- Soil profile sampling at grid intervals (1 km × 1 km).
- Discharge measurement at upstream and downstream gauges.
- Crop-cutting experiments for yield estimation.
- Household survey using stratified random sampling.

**Step 3 — Impact Identification Using Leopold Matrix (2 Marks):**

| Activity | Soil | Water | Crop | Socio-econ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reservoir submergence** | Loss of topsoil | Seepage-induced waterlogging | Permanent cropland loss | Displacement |
| **Canal operation** | Waterlogging, salinization | Reduced river flow | Crop pattern shift | Tail-end scarcity |

**Step 4 — Significance Evaluation (1 Mark):**
Magnitude (–4 to +4) × Duration (1–4) × Reversibility (1–4). Scores > 30 are rated **"Significant"** and require mitigation.

---

#### **Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** A watershed has the following USLE parameters:
**R = 300 MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr, K = 0.40 t·ha·hr/ha·MJ·mm, LS = 2.0, C = 0.25, P = 1.0.**
Compute the **annual soil loss** and suggest **two mitigation measures** if the T-value is **11.2 t/ha/yr**. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Formula Statement (1 Mark):**
$ A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P $

**Step 2 — Substitution (2 Marks):**
$$
A \;=\; 300 \times 0.40 \times 2.0 \times 0.25 \times 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Calculation (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 300 \times 0.40 \;\rightarrow\; 120 \\
120 \times 2.0 &= 240 \\
240 \times 0.25 &= 60 \\
60 \times 1.0 &= 60 \;\text{t/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with T-value (1 Mark):**
$A = 60 \;\text{t/ha/yr} \;>\; T = 11.2 \;\text{t/ha/yr}$
Soil loss is **5.36 times** the permissible limit → **Mitigation mandatory**.

**Step 5 — Two Mitigation Measures (1 Mark):**
1. **Contour bunding and terracing** to reduce LS factor.
2. **Afforestation / cover crops** to reduce the C factor from 0.25 to ~0.05.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

#### **Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **causes, effects, and mitigation of waterlogging and salinization** in an irrigation command area. Use a diagram. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Causes (2 Marks):**
1. **Excess water application** without proper drainage.
2. **Seepage from unlined canals** raising water table.
3. **High salt content in irrigation water** (high SAR, RSC).
4. **Topographic low-lying areas** with poor natural drainage.

**Effects (2 Marks):**
1. **Root anoxia** leading to crop wilting and yield loss.
2. **Salt accumulation** (Na⁺, Cl⁻) at surface → soil becomes saline/alkaline.
3. Eventual **abandonment of farmland** (e.g., parts of Haryana, Rajasthan).

**Diagram (1 Mark):**
Capillary rise of salts → surface salt crust (block diagram from Section 4.5 is acceptable).

**Mitigation (2 Marks):**
1. **Lining of canals** to reduce seepage.
2. **Subsurface drainage** at 1.5–2.5 m depth.
3. **Gypsum application** (CaSO₄) to displace Na⁺ and reclaim sodic soils.
4. **Salt-tolerant crops** like barley, sugar beet, mustard.

---

#### **Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** Irrigation water has the following ionic concentrations in **meq/L**: **Na⁺ = 12, Ca²⁺ = 4, Mg²⁺ = 2, CO₃²⁻ = 1, HCO₃⁻ = 5**.
Compute **SAR and RSC** and comment on the **suitability of this water for irrigation**. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — SAR Formula (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{SAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Na}^+}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+}+\text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}
$$

**Step 2 — SAR Calculation (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAR} &= \dfrac{12}{\sqrt{\dfrac{4+2}{2}}} \\
&= \dfrac{12}{\sqrt{3}} \\
&= \dfrac{12}{1.732} \\
&= 6.93
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — RSC Formula (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{RSC} = (\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{HCO}_3^-) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+})
$$

**Step 4 — RSC Calculation (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{RSC} = (1 + 5) - (4 + 2) = 6 - 6 = 0 \;\text{meq/L}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**
- **SAR = 6.93** → falls in the **"No restriction"** zone (<10) → **suitable** from sodium hazard perspective.
- **RSC = 0 meq/L** → **safe**, no carbonate-induced alkalinity problem.
- **Conclusion:** This water is **suitable for irrigation** on most soil types; however, periodic soil testing is recommended.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Agriculture and Irrigation EIA**

- **EIA scope** includes dams, canals, watershed programs, agro-industries, land acquisition, and aquaculture.
- **Four impact categories**: soil, water, crops, socio-economy.
- **Key indicators**: ECe, SAR, RSC, T-value, USLE (A), Cropping Intensity.
- **Major impacts of irrigation projects**: submergence, waterlogging, salinization, sodicification, displacement, downstream flow reduction.
- **Mitigation toolkit**: canal lining, drip/sprinkler, subsurface drainage, gypsum amendment, salt-tolerant cultivars, watershed treatment, resettlement & rehabilitation (R&R) with livelihood restoration.
- **USLE formula**: $A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$ — must be memorized with units.
- **SAR threshold values**: <10 safe, 10–18 slight-moderate, 18–26 severe, >26 unsuitable.
- **Crop water requirement**: $ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$.
- **Irrigation efficiency**: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{conveyance} \times \eta_{application} \times \eta_{field}$.
- **Water balance equation** predicts waterlogging and decides drainage design.
- **Drip irrigation efficiency** can exceed **90%** — preferred EMP recommendation.
- **CPCB/MoEFCC** guidelines govern monitoring; baseline must include both **pre-monsoon and post-monsoon** data.
- **R&R (Rehabilitation & Resettlement)** is governed by **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013**.
- **Always include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers — non-negotiable for full marks.
- **Write units** in every numerical step — unmarked units are a common deduction source.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forest Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT636 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forest Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forest Management** is the science, art, and practice of controlling the establishment, growth, composition, health, and quality of forests to meet diverse human needs and environmental values.
