In the context of **Environmental Impact Analysis (EIA)**, Forest Management refers to the systematic process of evaluating, predicting, mitigating, and monitoring the biophysical, ecological, socio-economic, and cultural impacts of development projects on forest ecosystems, coupled with the implementation of sustainable silvicultural practices, conservation strategies, and policy frameworks that ensure the long-term resilience of forest resources.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition aligning with **National Forest Policy (1988)** and **Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 (Amended 2023)**, forest management in EIA is treated as an interdisciplinary analytical discipline that integrates:

- **Silviculture** (the practice of controlling forest growth)
- **Forest Mensuration** (measurement of trees and forest stands)
- **Wildlife Ecology** (fauna and biodiversity studies)
- **Anthropogenic Impact Assessment** (human-induced pressures)
- **Sustainable Yield Analysis** (balancing extraction with regeneration)
- **Remote Sensing & GIS-based Forest Cover Mapping**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** This module emphasizes the **EIA-specific framework** for evaluating forest impacts, the **regulatory and legal architecture** governing forest clearance (Forest Conservation Act, 2023 amendment), **Joint Forest Management (JFM)** protocols, **carbon sequestration accounting**, and the integration of **remote sensing techniques** in baseline forest cover assessment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **bank account** that takes 100 years to mature. You cannot withdraw money today that you have not yet deposited, and you must carefully track both *deposits* (new tree growth, regeneration) and *withdrawals* (logging, land conversion). If your withdrawals consistently exceed deposits, the account eventually becomes bankrupt — the forest degrades, biodiversity collapses, and the ecosystem fails.

**Forest Management = The Banker's Role in this slow-motion account:**

| Banking Concept | Forest Management Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Principal deposit | Mature standing stock (biomass) |
| Interest accrued | Annual increment (new growth) |
| Withdrawal | Harvest / felling / land conversion |
| Audit | Forest inventory & mensuration |
| Diversification | Mixed species plantations |
| Insurance | Biodiversity reserves & protected areas |
| Regulatory body (RBI) | Forest Conservation Act, MoEFCC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Forests Matter in EIA:** Forests are the **lungs of the planet**, the **world's largest carbon sink** (absorbing approximately **7.6 GtCO₂/year** globally, 2 GtCO₂/year in India), regulate the hydrological cycle, harbor **80% of terrestrial biodiversity**, and sustain **275 million people** in India directly through forest products. Any industrial, mining, infrastructure, or urban development project has a near-certainty of forest interface, making Forest Impact Assessment a **non-negotiable** component of EIA.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Legal Thresholds

The following **bold** metrics and thresholds must be memorized for KTU 2024 board examinations:

- **Forest Cover of India (ISFR 2021):** **21.71% of total geographical area (~72,021, sq. km)**
- **Tree Cover:** **2.91% (~9,631 sq. km)**
- **Total Forest + Tree Cover:** **24.62%**
- **National Forest Policy Target:** **33%** (to be achieved)
- **Carbon Sequestration Potential (Indian forests):** **~20–30 tonnes C/ha** in mature tropical forests
- **Minimum Compensatory Afforestation Land (as per FCA 2023):** **Equivalent to the area diverted** (1:1 ratio for degraded forest land; 1:1 with degraded forest in another State if not available)
- **Forest Density Classification (FSI):**
  * **Very Dense Forest (VDF):** Canopy density ≥ **70%**
  * **Moderately Dense Forest (MDF):** Canopy density **40–70%**
  * **Open Forest (OF):** Canopy density **10–40%**
  * **Scrub:** Canopy density < **10%**
- **Minimum viable forest patch for biodiversity:** **~100 hectares** (rule of thumb, varies by ecosystem)
- **Sustainable Yield Equation (Annual Allowable Cut):** $AAC = MAI \pm \Delta S$ where MAI = Mean Annual Increment, ΔS = change in growing stock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU:** The 2023 amendment to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, introduced the concept of "Forest Land" as land notified as forest under any government record, and "Non-Forest Land" deemed as forest for regulatory purposes. This has drastically tightened forest clearance norms in India.

---

## 1.4 Why Forest Management is a Critical Component of EIA

Forests form one of the **14 major environmental attributes** evaluated in a standard EIA Report (as per the **EIA Notification, 2006, amended 2020**). A proponent must conduct:

1. **Baseline Forest Inventory** — species composition, density, girth class distribution, regeneration status
2. **Deforestation Quantification** — direct (clear-felling) + indirect (edge effects, fragmentation)
3. **Wildlife Impact Assessment** — Schedule I species presence (Wildlife Protection Act, 1972)
4. **Carbon Stock Loss Estimation** — biomass × carbon fraction (default: 0.47)
5. **Afforestation / Compensatory Plan** — species selection, survival targets, monitoring KPIs
6. **Socio-Economic Impact** — Forest Rights Act (2006) compliance, tribal displacement
7. **Cumulative Impact Assessment** — multiple projects in a forest landscape

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily maps to **CO4** of PEPOT636 — *"Apply EIA methodologies to evaluate impacts on natural resources including forests, wildlife, water, and air, and propose mitigation measures in compliance with Indian regulatory frameworks."* Bloom's Levels: **L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Forest Management in EIA

Forest Management in EIA operates on a **four-pillar analytical structure** that must be understood sequentially:

### Pillar 1: Forest as an Ecosystem (Ecological Foundation)

A forest is a **biotic community** dominated by trees and other woody vegetation, functioning as a complex, self-regulating ecological system. EIA views forests through the lens of:

- **Structural attributes** (species, age, girth, height, canopy layers)
- **Compositional attributes** (diversity indices, species richness)
- **Functional attributes** (productivity, nutrient cycling, carbon flux)
- **Landscape-level attributes** (fragmentation, connectivity, edge effects)

### Pillar 2: Forest Inventory & Mensuration (Quantitative Baseline)

This is the **measurement science** of forests. Before any impact can be assessed, the baseline "standing crop" of the forest must be quantified.

**Key mensurational parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diameter at Breast Height | DBH | cm or m | Stem diameter at 1.37 m above ground |
| Basal Area | BA | m²/ha | Cross-sectional area of stems at DBH |
| Volume | V | m³/ha | Timber/wood volume per unit area |
| Site Index | SI | m | Average dominant tree height at age 50 |
| Stocking | S | % | Actual BA as % of normal BA |
| Growing Stock | GS | m³/ha | Total volume of all living trees |
| Mean Annual Increment | MAI | m³/ha/yr | Average growth over rotation period |
| Current Annual Increment | CAI | m³/ha/yr | Growth in a specific year |
| Canopy Density | CD | % | Projected crown cover as % of land area |

### Pillar 3: Impact Prediction & Assessment

This pillar answers: *"If we clear 50 hectares of this forest, what is lost?"*

The framework uses:

- **Direct impact metrics:** area lost, biomass destroyed, trees felled
- **Indirect impact metrics:** fragmentation, edge effects (penetration 100–300 m), micro-climate alteration
- **Cumulative impact:** synergistic loss from multiple projects in the same landscape
- **Valuation methods:** stumpage value, timber value, ecosystem services value (TEEB framework)

### Pillar 4: Mitigation Hierarchy & Monitoring

The standard sequence is:
**Avoid → Minimize → Restore → Offset**

This is the **Mitigation Hierarchy** mandated in all EIA studies.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS** for Forest Management in EIA. All questions in the board exam will require mastery of these equations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basal Area of a tree | $BA_{tree} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times DBH^{2}$ | DBH in m → BA in m² | Use $\frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ per stem |
| 2 | Stand Basal Area | $BA_{stand} = \frac{\sum BA_{tree}}{A}$ | A = plot area in ha | Result in m²/ha |
| 3 | Volume (single tree) | $V = BA \times H \times f$ | H = height, f = form factor (0.4–0.5) | m³ per tree |
| 4 | Stand Volume (per ha) | $V_{ha} = \frac{\sum V_{tree}}{A}$ | A in ha | m³/ha |
| 5 | Site Index | $SI = H_{dom} \text{ at age 50}$ | Height in m | Indicator of site quality |
| 6 | Mean Annual Increment | $MAI = \frac{V_{mature}}{A_{rotation}}$ | m³/ha/yr | Average over rotation |
| 7 | Current Annual Increment | $CAI = \frac{V_{t+1} - V_{t}}{1 yr}$ | m³/ha/yr | Annual growth rate |
| 8 | Allowable Annual Cut | $AAC = MAI$ (regulated forest) | m³/yr | Sustainable harvest |
| 9 | Above-Ground Biomass | $AGB = a \times DBH^{b}$ (allometric) | a, b = species-specific constants | kg dry weight |
| 10 | Below-Ground Biomass | $BGB = 0.26 \times AGB$ | — | Default root-to-shoot ratio (Mokany et al.) |
| 11 | Total Biomass | $TB = AGB + BGB$ | tonnes/ha | — |
| 12 | Carbon Stock | $C = TB \times 0.47$ | tonnes C/ha | IPCC default carbon fraction |
| 13 | CO₂ Equivalent | $CO_{2e} = C \times \frac{44}{12}$ | tonnes CO₂/ha | Molecular weight ratio |
| 14 | Canopy Density | $CD = \frac{Crown \ projected \ area}{Plot \ area} \times 100$ | % | Measured via hemispherical photos or satellite |
| 15 | Species Diversity (Shannon-Wiener) | $H' = -\sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln(p_i)$ | $p_i$ = proportion of species *i* | Dimensionless; typical 1.5–3.5 |
| 16 | Species Evenness | $J' = \frac{H'}{\ln(S)}$ | S = species richness | Ranges 0–1 |
| 17 | Species Richness | $S$ | Count | Number of species per unit area |
| 18 | Simpson's Index | $D = 1 - \sum p_i^{2}$ | — | Probability of interspecific encounter |
| 19 | Fragmentation Index | $FI = 1 - \frac{1}{N}$ (patches) | N = number of patches | 0 = continuous, 1 = highly fragmented |
| 20 | Net Present Value (Forest) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_{t} - C_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | r = discount rate, T = rotation | Long-term timber valuation |
| 21 | Compensatory Afforestation Ratio | $A_{CA} = R \times A_{diverted}$ | R = 1:1 to 1:4 by region | R = 1 in Kerala/WB; 1:4 in Rajasthan |
| 22 | Edge Effect Depth | $E \approx 100 \text{ to } 300 \text{ m}$ | metres | Empirical for tropical forests |
| 23 | Carbon Loss from Clearing | $C_{lost} = A \times TB \times 0.47$ | tonnes C | A = cleared area in ha |
| 24 | Carrying Capacity (Wildlife) | $K = \frac{B \times P_{CR}}{C_{consumed}}$ | B = biomass, P = productivity, C = daily intake | Animals per unit area |
| 25 | Deforestation Rate | $DR = \frac{A_{lost}}{T \times A_{total}} \times 100$ | %/yr | Annual forest loss rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Formulas:** A Mining EIA Report, for example, will require the proponent to compute **carbon loss (Equation 13, 23)**, **biomass lost (Equation 9, 11)**, **diversity index baseline (Equation 15)**, and **compensatory afforestation target (Equation 21)**. These are routinely asked in KTU viva voce and board exams.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Application: Engineering Fields That Need Forest EIA

| Engineering Field | Forest EIA Application |
|---|---|
| Civil Engineering (Roads/Highways) | Linear impact, fragmentation analysis, tree-cutting inventory |
| Mining Engineering | Open-pit forest clearance, carbon stock loss, slope stability |
| Hydropower Engineering | Submergence of forest land, reservoir flora, downstream ecology |
| Urban Planning | Urban green cover loss, peri-urban forest fragmentation |
| Industrial Engineering (Steel, Cement) | Limestone quarrying, fly-ash dumps in forested zones |
| Renewable Energy (Wind/Solar) | Wind farms in Western Ghats; solar in wastelands avoiding forests |
| Petrochemical (Pipelines) | ROU (Right of Use) corridor through forest, trans-boundary impacts |
| Coastal Engineering | Mangrove clearance for ports (CRZ Notification 2019) |

---

## 2.4 Theoretical Linkages: Sylvicultural Systems

Forest management in EIA also requires understanding the **silvicultural systems** the proposed project will affect or the compensatory plantation will use:

1. **Clear Felling System** — entire stand removed at once; maximum impact, easiest to inventory
2. **Shelterwood System** — mature trees left as seed source; partial impact
3. **Selection System** — individual trees removed; minimum impact, sustained yield
4. **Coppice System** — vegetative regrowth after cutting; common in Eucalyptus plantations
5. **Agroforestry / Social Forestry** — tree planting outside notified forest; mitigation option

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forest Conservation Act (FCA) 2023 Key Provisions for KTU:**
> - **Section 2:** Definition of "Forest" widened to include any land recorded as forest in any government record.
> - **Section 3:** No State Government can divert forest land for non-forest use without prior central approval.
> - **Compensatory Afforestation Fund (CAMPA):** All compensatory levies deposited in CAF Act, 2016.
> - **Net Present Value (NPV):** Project proponent must pay NPV of forest land based on its ecosystem services.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Forest Management Equations

### Derivation 1: Stand Basal Area from Field Inventory

**Problem Setup:** A 0.1-hectare circular sample plot contains 8 trees. Their DBH measurements (in cm) are: 22, 28, 35, 41, 47, 52, 60, 68. Calculate the stand basal area in m²/ha.

**Step 1: Convert DBH from cm to metres.**
$DBH_{22cm} = 0.22 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{28cm} = 0.28 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{35cm} = 0.35 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{41cm} = 0.41 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{47cm} = 0.47 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{52cm} = 0.52 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{60cm} = 0.60 \text{ m}$
$DBH_{68cm} = 0.68 \text{ m}$

**Step 2: Apply the basal area formula for each tree.**

$$BA_{tree} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}$$

For tree 1: $BA_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.22)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0484 = 0.0380 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 2: $BA_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.28)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0784 = 0.0616 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 3: $BA_3 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.35)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.1225 = 0.0962 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 4: $BA_4 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.41)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.1681 = 0.1320 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 5: $BA_5 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.47)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.2209 = 0.1735 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 6: $BA_6 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.52)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.2704 = 0.2124 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 7: $BA_7 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.60)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.3600 = 0.2827 \text{ m}^{2}$

For tree 8: $BA_8 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.68)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.4624 = 0.3631 \text{ m}^{2}$

**Step 3: Sum the basal areas.**

$$\sum BA_{tree} = 0.0380 + 0.0616 + 0.0962 + 0.1320 + 0.1735 + 0.2124 + 0.2827 + 0.3631$$

$$\sum BA_{tree} = 1.3595 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Convert to per-hectare basis (sample plot is 0.1 ha).**

$$BA_{stand} = \frac{1.3595}{0.1} = 13.595 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{ha}$$

**Interpretation:** A stand BA of ~13.6 m²/ha indicates a **moderately dense forest** (typical tropical moist deciduous baseline ranges 15–25 m²/ha).

---

### Derivation 2: Carbon Stock Loss from Deforestation

**Problem Setup:** A 100-hectare mining project in a tropical moist deciduous forest will clear 100 ha. Baseline biomass: **180 tonnes/ha (above-ground) + 47 tonnes/ha (below-ground)**. Compute:
(a) Total biomass lost
(b) Carbon stock lost
(c) CO₂ equivalent released

**Step 1: Compute Total Biomass per hectare.**

$$TB = AGB + BGB = 180 + 47 = 227 \text{ tonnes/ha}$$

**Step 2: Verify BGB using the default root-to-shoot ratio (0.26).**

$$BGB = 0.26 \times AGB = 0.26 \times 180 = 46.8 \approx 47 \text{ tonnes/ha} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Biomass for the 100 ha cleared area.**

$$TB_{total} = 227 \times 100 = 22{,}700 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Convert biomass to carbon stock (IPCC default fraction = 0.47).**

$$C_{lost} = 22{,}700 \times 0.47 = 10{,}669 \text{ tonnes of Carbon}$$

**Step 5: Convert carbon to CO₂ equivalent using molecular weight ratio (44/12).**

$$CO_{2e} = 10{,}669 \times \frac{44}{12} = 10{,}669 \times 3.6667$$

$$CO_{2e} = 39{,}120 \text{ tonnes CO}_{2}$$

**Result:** Clearing 100 ha of this forest releases **~39,120 tonnes of CO₂** to the atmosphere (excluding soil carbon). This is a typical *baseline* used in Mining EIA for **Climate Impact Assessment**.

---

### Derivation 3: Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index for EIA Baseline

**Problem Setup:** A 0.5 ha sample plot records 6 species with the following individual counts: Species A = 12, B = 8, C = 6, D = 4, E = 2, F = 1. Compute Shannon-Wiener Index (H').

**Step 1: Compute total individuals.**

$$N = 12 + 8 + 6 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 33$$

**Step 2: Compute proportion of each species.**

$$p_A = \frac{12}{33} = 0.3636$$
$$p_B = \frac{8}{33} = 0.2424$$
$$p_C = \frac{6}{33} = 0.1818$$
$$p_D = \frac{4}{33} = 0.1212$$
$$p_E = \frac{2}{33} = 0.0606$$
$$p_F = \frac{1}{33} = 0.0303$$

**Step 3: Apply the Shannon-Wiener formula.**

$$H' = -\sum_{i=1}^{S} p_i \ln(p_i)$$

For each species:

$$H'_A = -0.3636 \times \ln(0.3636) = -0.3636 \times (-1.0116) = 0.3679$$
$$H'_B = -0.2424 \times \ln(0.2424) = -0.2424 \times (-1.4171) = 0.3435$$
$$H'_C = -0.1818 \times \ln(0.1818) = -0.1818 \times (-1.7047) = 0.3100$$
$$H'_D = -0.1212 \times \ln(0.1212) = -0.1212 \times (-2.1102) = 0.2558$$
$$H'_E = -0.0606 \times \ln(0.0606) = -0.0606 \times (-2.8024) = 0.1698$$
$$H'_F = -0.0303 \times \ln(0.0303) = -0.0303 \times (-3.4965) = 0.1059$$

**Step 4: Sum all contributions.**

$$H' = 0.3679 + 0.3435 + 0.3100 + 0.2558 + 0.1698 + 0.1059 = 1.5529$$

**Result:** $H' = 1.55$. This is **moderate diversity** (typical of disturbed tropical forests; pristine tropical forests often show H' > 3.0). Used to establish pre-project baseline for **post-project comparison**.

---

### Derivation 4: Annual Allowable Cut for a Managed Forest

**Problem Setup:** A Teak plantation has a rotation age of 80 years, growing stock at maturity = 280 m³/ha. Compute the Mean Annual Increment (MAI) and the sustainable annual harvest for a 1000 ha forest.

**Step 1: Compute MAI.**

$$MAI = \frac{V_{mature}}{A_{rotation}} = \frac{280}{80} = 3.5 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{ha}/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2: Compute total AAC for 1000 ha.**

$$AAC_{total} = 3.5 \times 1000 = 3{,}500 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{yr}$$

**Result:** Sustainable harvest = **3,500 m³/yr** from this 1000 ha plantation.

---

### Derivation 5: Compensatory Afforestation Land & Cost

**Problem Setup:** A road project diverts 75 ha of degraded forest in Kerala. Calculate the CA requirement and the minimum fund allocation under CAMPA guidelines.

**Step 1: Apply the 1:1 rule (Kerala).**

$$A_{CA} = 1 \times 75 = 75 \text{ ha of degraded land}$$

**Step 2: Compute NPV (Net Present Value) cost (assumed ₹10 lakh/ha for Kerala).**

$$NPV_{cost} = 75 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 7.5 \text{ Crore (₹75 million)}$$

**Step 3: Add CA cost (assumed ₹5 lakh/ha).**

$$CA_{cost} = 75 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 3.75 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 4: Total cost to proponent.**

$$Total = 7.5 + 3.75 = 11.25 \text{ Crore}$$

**Note:** Additional levies include **Catchment Area Treatment Plan (CAT)**, **Wildlife Management Plan**, and **Rehabilitation & Resettlement costs**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Impact Identification Methods for Forest EIA

| Method | Application in Forest EIA | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Satellite Remote Sensing (LISS-III/IV)** | Forest cover change detection (FRD) | Large area, periodic | Cloud cover, low spatial resolution (23.5 m) |
| **LiDAR** | Canopy height, biomass estimation | High accuracy (3D) | Expensive, low penetration in dense canopy |
| **Field Inventory (Sample Plots)** | Species, DBH, density, regeneration | Ground-truth quality | Time-consuming, limited spatial coverage |
| **GIS Overlay** | Project footprint vs. forest boundary | Quick, visual | Dependent on base map accuracy |
| **DGPS-based Tree Mapping** | Tree-level impact for highways | Precise location | Manual, slow |
| **Camera Traps & Transects** | Wildlife presence, behaviour | Direct evidence | Sighting bias |
| **Soil Sampling** | Carbon loss, fertility | Quantitative | Invasive |
| **Public Consultation (FRA 2006)** | Tribal rights, forest dwellers' claims | Social legitimacy | Time-intensive, sensitive |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure for Forest Impact Assessment in EIA

The standard sequence (per **MoEFCC EIA Manual, 2022**) is:

**Step 1: Project Footprint Delineation**
Define the area where vegetation will be cleared (project + buffer + access road).

**Step 2: Baseline Data Collection**
- Forest type and density (FSI classification)
- Species list (flora and fauna)
- Sample plots (e.g., 10 m × 10 m for trees, 5 m × 5 m for shrubs, 1 m × 1 m for herbs)
- Tree count, DBH, height
- Biomass and carbon estimation
- Wildlife presence (Schedule I species)
- Socio-economic survey of forest dwellers

**Step 3: Impact Prediction**
- Direct loss (biomass, species)
- Indirect impacts (edge effects, fragmentation)
- Cumulative impacts (multiple projects in landscape)

**Step 4: Mitigation Plan**
- Avoid (route alignment, project site selection)
- Minimize (reduce footprint)
- Restore (replant, regenerate)
- Offset (compensatory afforestation, NPV)

**Step 5: Monitoring Plan**
- Survival rate of plantations (≥ 80% at 3 years)
- Periodic forest cover monitoring via satellite
- Carbon stock re-estimation
- Biodiversity index re-computation
